topics.title: Factors Determining Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Determining Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Demand** in engineering economics refers to the *quantities of a good or service that consumers are willing and able to purchase at various possible prices during a given period of time*, holding all other influencing factors constant (*ceteris paribus*).

The **determinants of demand** are the non-price variables and price-related variables that causally influence the position and slope of the demand curve of a consumer or a market. These factors shift the demand curve (causing a *change in demand*) or cause movement *along* a fixed demand curve (a *change in quantity demanded*).

The general **Demand Function** is mathematically expressed as:

$$D_x = f(P_x, Y, P_r, T, E, N, G, U)$$

where each variable represents a distinct causal determinant of consumer purchase behavior.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Mobile Phone Buying Decision"**
> 
> Imagine you are deciding whether to buy a new smartphone. Why might your decision change from one month to the next?
> 
> - If your **salary increases**, you may upgrade to a pricier model (income effect).
> - If **Apple releases a new iPhone**, your demand for older Samsung phones may drop (substitute effect).
> - If **fuel prices rise sharply**, your demand for fuel-efficient EVs may spike (complementary demand).
> - If your **friends start using a phone**, you may want it too (taste/preference effect).
> - If you **expect a price drop in Diwali sales**, you may postpone buying (expectation effect).
> - If **festive season** arrives and gifts are exchanged, the market demand for phones rises (demographic/seasonal effect).
> 
> **Every single factor above is a "determinant of demand."** Together, they explain *why* and *how much* a consumer buys — not just at a given price, but across time, income, and social conditions.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Demand** | Willingness + Ability to purchase at a given price |
| **Quantity Demanded** | A specific amount at a specific price (point on curve) |
| **Change in Demand** | Shift of the entire demand curve (caused by non-price factors) |
| **Change in Qty Demanded** | Movement *along* a fixed demand curve (caused by price) |
| **Ceteris Paribus** | "Other things being equal" — assumption that non-price factors are held constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Focus (2024 Scheme):**
> Examiners frequently test whether students can clearly distinguish between *change in demand* (curve shift) and *change in quantity demanded* (movement along the curve). This is a high-weightage 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Taxonomy of Demand Determinants

The factors that determine demand are classified into **two broad families**:

### A. Price Determinant (Intrinsic to the Good)
### B. Non-Price Determinants (Exogenous Variables)

Let us analyze each in exhaustive KTU-board-exam-ready depth.

---

### 2.1.1 Price of the Good Itself (Pₓ) — The "Own Price"

This is the **most fundamental determinant** of quantity demanded. The **Law of Demand** states:

> *Ceteris paribus, there is an inverse relationship between the price of a good and the quantity demanded of that good.*

- Higher Pₓ → Lower Qd
- Lower Pₓ → Higher Qd
- This causes **movement along** a given demand curve (not a shift).

**Why does this happen?** Three economic forces explain it:
1. **Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility** — additional units give less satisfaction.
2. **Income Effect** — at higher prices, real income (purchasing power) falls.
3. **Substitution Effect** — consumers switch to relatively cheaper alternatives.

---

### 2.1.2 Income of the Consumer (Y)

Income changes the *purchasing power* of the consumer. Goods are classified based on how demand responds to income:

| Type of Good | Income Change (↑Y) | Demand Response (ΔDₓ) | Income Elasticity (Ey) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal Good** | Increases | Demand increases (ΔD > 0) | $E_y > 0$ |
| **Inferior Good** | Increases | Demand decreases (ΔD < 0) | $E_y < 0$ |
| **Luxury Good** | Increases | Demand increases sharply | $E_y > 1$ |
| **Necessity Good** | Increases | Demand increases slightly | $0 < E_y < 1$ |

- Examples: Branded clothing = **Normal/Luxury**; Public-bus transport = **Inferior**; Salt, rice = **Necessity**.

---

### 2.1.3 Prices of Related Goods (Pᵣ)

Related goods fall into two categories with opposite effects:

| Type of Related Good | Definition | Example | Cross-Elasticity (Exy) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Substitute Good** | Goods that satisfy similar needs; used *in place of* each other | Tea ↔ Coffee; Maruti Swift ↔ Hyundai i20 | $E_{xy} > 0$ (positive) |
| **Complementary Good** | Goods that are *jointly consumed*; used *together* | Car ↔ Petrol; Smartphone ↔ Earphones; Printer ↔ Ink Cartridge | $E_{xy} < 0$ (negative) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Example:** In IT, the demand for **printers** is highly complementary to the demand for **ink cartridges**. If printer prices fall → more printers sold → *derived* demand for cartridges rises. KTU examiners love such applied cross-elasticity examples.

---

### 2.1.4 Tastes, Preferences, and Fashion (T)

This is a **subjective** determinant influenced by:
- Cultural shifts, advertising, social media trends
- Lifestyle changes, peer influence, celebrity endorsements
- Health awareness, environmental consciousness (e.g., rising demand for EVs)

A positive change in taste *shifts* the demand curve to the **right** (increase in demand); a negative change shifts it to the **left** (decrease in demand).

---

### 2.1.5 Expectations of Future Prices and Income (E)

Consumers act on **anticipations**:
- If buyers expect **future price to rise** → they buy *more now* → current demand ↑.
- If buyers expect **future price to fall** → they postpone → current demand ↓.
- If buyers expect **future income to rise** → they spend more today (or save — depends).
- If buyers expect **future income to fall** (recession fear) → they cut spending now.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This determinant is especially relevant in **engineered durable goods** markets — cars, appliances, real estate — where timing of purchase is discretionary.

---

### 2.1.6 Number of Buyers in the Market (N)

This applies to **market demand** (aggregate), not individual demand.
- Population growth → Market demand ↑
- Immigration / demographic shifts (e.g., youth bulge) → Market demand ↑
- Aging population → Demand for healthcare, retirement housing ↑

---

### 2.1.7 Government Policy, Taxation, and Subsidies (G)

- **Indirect taxes** (GST, excise) raise effective price → demand ↓
- **Subsidies** lower effective price → demand ↑
- **Price ceilings** (rent control) create shortages
- **Public distribution** schemes shift demand for staple goods

---

### 2.1.8 Credit Availability and Interest Rates (U)

- Cheap credit / low EMIs → Big-ticket purchases (cars, homes) become affordable → demand ↑
- Tight monetary policy / high interest rates → demand for credit-financed goods ↓

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the *exact formulas* the KTU board expects students to reproduce verbatim in the 14-mark analytical questions. Memorize the form, not just the symbols.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation Key |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Demand Function | $D_x = f(P_x, Y, P_r, T, E, N, G, U)$ | All non-price variables explicitly listed |
| 2 | Linear Demand Function (Standard) | $Q_d = a - bP_x$ | $a$ = intercept; $b$ = slope coefficient (b > 0) |
| 3 | Law of Demand (Inverse Relationship) | $\dfrac{\partial Q_d}{\partial P_x} < 0$ | Partial derivative w.r.t. own price is negative |
| 4 | Price Elasticity of Demand | $E_p = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P_x} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_d}{\Delta P_x} \cdot \dfrac{P_x}{Q_d}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | Income Elasticity of Demand | $E_y = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta Y} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_d}{\Delta Y} \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Q_d}$ | Positive for normal; negative for inferior |
| 6 | Cross-Price Elasticity of Demand | $E_{xy} = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d^X}{\%\Delta P_y} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \cdot \dfrac{P_y}{Q_x}$ | Positive for substitutes; negative for complements |
| 7 | Normal Good Condition | $E_y > 0$ | Demand rises with income |
| 8 | Inferior Good Condition | $E_y < 0$ | Demand falls with income |
| 9 | Substitute Good Condition | $E_{xy} > 0$ | Cross-elasticity positive |
| 10 | Complement Good Condition | $E_{xy} < 0$ | Cross-elasticity negative |

---

## 2.3 Exceptions to the Law of Demand (KTU Favourite)

The KTU 2024 scheme gives significant weight to *exceptions* — students often lose marks by missing these.

| # | Exception | Reason | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Giffen Goods** | Strong income effect dominates substitution effect (extremely inferior) | Staple food (e.g., cheap bread for very poor) |
| 2 | **Veblen Goods** | Conspicuous consumption; higher price = higher prestige | Luxury watches, designer handbags, rare art |
| 3 | **Expectation of Shortage / Speculative Demand** | Buyers hoard expecting future shortage or price rise | Panic buying of fuel, gold, masks |
| 4 | **Emergency Necessities** | Life-saving goods bought irrespective of price | Life-saving drugs, oxygen cylinders |
| 5 | **Ignorance of Consumers** | Quality wrongly judged by high price (snob appeal) | Branded cosmetics, premium wines |
| 6 | **Change in Fashion Necessities** | Articles of snob appeal | Antique jewelry, vintage cars |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** If a question asks for "any 3 exceptions to the Law of Demand," writing only *Giffen* and *Veblen* goods is **incomplete**. Always list *at least 4* for a full 3-mark answer. Examiners deduct marks for thin answers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Demand Determinants |
|---|---|
| **Production Planning** | Firms forecast demand using income, taste, and demographic data before scaling capacity |
| **Inventory Management** | JIT systems rely on accurate demand-function estimation |
| **Pricing Strategy** | Price elasticity (Eₚ) determines whether to raise or cut price to maximize revenue |
| **Product Design** | Cross-elasticity determines bundling decisions (complements like printer+ink) |
| **Capital Investment** | Long-term demand forecasts for power, telecom, transport infrastructure |
| **Marketing & Branding** | Shifts in tastes (T) are tracked via sentiment analytics to reposition products |
| **Public Policy** | Subsidy design (G) requires demand elasticity analysis to avoid fiscal waste |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Demand Equation from Tabular Data

**Problem:** A consumer's demand schedule for good X is given below. Derive the linear demand equation and identify the slope and intercept.

| Price (Pₓ in ₹) | Quantity Demanded (Qd) |
|---|---|
| 10 | 100 |
| 20 | 80 |
| 30 | 60 |
| 40 | 40 |
| 50 | 20 |

### Step 1 — Identify the Functional Form

> We assume a **linear demand function** of the form:
> 
> $$Q_d = a - bP_x$$
> 
> where $a$ is the autonomous (intercept) demand and $b$ is the absolute slope coefficient.

### Step 2 — Apply Two-Point Method

> Pick any two points from the table. Let us select $(P_1, Q_1) = (10, 100)$ and $(P_2, Q_2) = (50, 20)$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Slope (b)

> The slope of a linear demand curve in the Q–P plane is:
> 
> $$b = -\frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{P_2 - P_1}$$
> 
> Substituting the values:
> 
> $$b = -\frac{20 - 100}{50 - 10} = -\frac{-80}{40} = 2$$
> 
> Since we use the form $Q_d = a - bP_x$, we take $b = 2$ (positive magnitude).

### Step 4 — Compute the Intercept (a)

> Substitute $P_x = 10$ and $Q_d = 100$ into $Q_d = a - bP_x$:
> 
> $$100 = a - 2(10)$$
> 
> $$100 = a - 20$$
> 
> $$a = 120$$

### Step 5 — Final Linear Demand Equation

> $$\boxed{Q_d = 120 - 2P_x}$$

### Step 6 — Verification

> Test with $P_x = 30$:
> 
> $$Q_d = 120 - 2(30) = 120 - 60 = 60 \;\; \checkmark$$
> 
> Test with $P_x = 40$:
> 
> $$Q_d = 120 - 2(40) = 120 - 80 = 40 \;\; \checkmark$$
> 
> The equation correctly reproduces every observation in the demand schedule.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cause–Effect of Each Determinant

The following table is a **high-yield analytical matrix** that maps each determinant to its qualitative effect on demand. This is the type of structured answer KTU examiners reward with full marks.

| Determinant | Variable Symbol | Direction of Change | Effect on Demand Curve | Numerical Effect on Qd (assuming normal good) | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Own Price ↑ | $P_x \uparrow$ | Inverse | Movement *up* along curve | $Q_d \downarrow$ | Petrol price hike reduces monthly car usage |
| Own Price ↓ | $P_x \downarrow$ | Inverse | Movement *down* along curve | $Q_d \uparrow$ | Smartphone price drop boosts sales |
| Income ↑ (Normal Good) | $Y \uparrow$ | Direct | Rightward shift | $Q_d \uparrow$ | Rise in IT salaries boosts laptop demand |
| Income ↑ (Inferior Good) | $Y \uparrow$ | Inverse | Leftward shift | $Q_d \downarrow$ | Higher income reduces demand for low-cost buses |
| Substitute Price ↑ | $P_y \uparrow$ | Direct (for X) | Rightward shift | $Q_d \uparrow$ | Coffee price rise increases tea demand |
| Substitute Price ↓ | $P_y \downarrow$ | Inverse (for X) | Leftward shift | $Q_d \downarrow$ | iPhone price cut hurts Samsung sales |
| Complement Price ↑ | $P_z \uparrow$ | Inverse (for X) | Leftward shift | $Q_d \downarrow$ | Petrol price rise reduces SUV demand |
| Complement Price ↓ | $P_z \downarrow$ | Direct (for X) | Rightward shift | $Q_d \uparrow$ | Cheaper ink cartridges increase printer demand |
| Tastes ↑ | $T \uparrow$ | Direct | Rightward shift | $Q_d \uparrow$ | Fitness trend boosts gym equipment demand |
| Tastes ↓ | $T \downarrow$ | Inverse | Leftward shift | $Q_d \downarrow$ | Health scare reduces fast-food demand |
| Expectation of Future Price ↑ | $E \uparrow$ | Direct | Rightward shift | $Q_d \uparrow$ | Expected gold price rise triggers gold buying |
| Expectation of Future Price ↓ | $E \downarrow$ | Inverse | Leftward shift | $Q_d \downarrow$ | Expected EV price drop delays current purchases |
| Number of Buyers ↑ | $N \uparrow$ | Direct | Rightward shift | $Q_d \uparrow$ | City population growth increases housing demand |
| Government Tax ↑ | $G \uparrow$ | Inverse (effective) | Leftward shift | $Q_d \downarrow$ | Tobacco tax reduces cigarette consumption |
| Subsidy ↑ | $G \downarrow$ (cost to consumer) | Direct | Rightward shift | $Q_d \uparrow$ | PM Awas Yojana subsidy boosts affordable housing |
| Credit Availability ↑ | $U \uparrow$ | Direct | Rightward shift | $Q_d \uparrow$ | Low home-loan rates boost real estate demand |
| Credit Tightening | $U \downarrow$ | Inverse | Leftward shift | $Q_d \downarrow$ | Higher EMI rates cool auto-loan demand |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Identifying the Type of Good from Elasticities

**Problem Statement:**
> A KTU past-year style question: *For good X, the income elasticity of demand is 1.5 and the cross-price elasticity with good Y is +0.8. If the price of good Y rises by 10%, by what percentage does the demand for good X change? Identify the type of good and the relationship.*

### Step 1 — Identify Type of Good (Normal vs. Inferior)

> Given: $E_y = +1.5$
> 
> Since $E_y > 0$, good X is a **normal good**.
> 
> Since $E_y = 1.5 > 1$, it is a **luxury good** (income-elastic).

### Step 2 — Identify Relationship (Substitute vs. Complement)

> Given: $E_{xy} = +0.8$
> 
> Since $E_{xy} > 0$, goods X and Y are **substitutes**.

### Step 3 — Compute % Change in Demand for X

> By definition of cross-price elasticity:
> 
> $$E_{xy} = \frac{\%\Delta Q_x}{\%\Delta P_y}$$
> 
> Rearranging:
> 
> $$\%\Delta Q_x = E_{xy} \times \%\Delta P_y$$
> 
> $$\%\Delta Q_x = (+0.8) \times (+10\%) = +8\%$$
> 
> **Demand for good X rises by 8%.**

### Step 4 — Economic Interpretation

> When the price of substitute Y rises by 10%, consumers switch from Y to X, increasing X's demand by 8%. The magnitude (0.8 < 1) suggests the substitution is **moderate** — consumers do not fully switch, indicating the goods are *imperfect* substitutes (e.g., two smartphone brands).

### Final Answer

> $$\boxed{\text{Demand for X increases by 8\%. Good X is a luxury normal good; X and Y are imperfect substitutes.}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Distinction: Shift vs. Movement (Board Favourite)

This distinction is tested almost every KTU exam cycle. Below is the formal analytical breakdown.

> **Movement along the demand curve** = **Change in Quantity Demanded**
> - Caused by: A change in **own price (Pₓ)** only.
> - Curve remains in same position; we move to a different *point* on it.
> - Graphical effect: Slide up or down the existing curve.

> **Shift of the entire demand curve** = **Change in Demand**
> - Caused by: A change in any **non-price determinant** (Y, Pᵣ, T, E, N, G, U).
> - Curve moves to a new position (leftward or rightward).
> - Graphical effect: A brand-new curve is drawn.

> **Mnemonic for KTU exam:**
> 
> **"Price changes → movement. Anything else changes → shift."**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Demand Function

Below is a high-fidelity **Mermaid block diagram** mapping how each determinant feeds into the aggregate demand function. This is a fallback architecture model — it captures the *interactions* of all factors in a way that the KTU board accepts when students reproduce similar flow diagrams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Environment] --> B[Economic Conditions]
    A --> C[Social and Cultural Trends]
    A --> D[Government Policies]

    B --> E[Income of Consumer Y]
    B --> F[Interest Rates and Credit]
    B --> G[Price Levels Inflation]

    C --> H[Tastes and Preferences T]
    C --> I[Demographics Population N]

    D --> J[Taxation and Subsidies G]
    D --> K[Regulations and Price Controls]

    E --> L[Central Demand Function Engine]
    F --> L
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M{Type of Good Classifier}
    M --> N1[Normal Good Branch]
    M --> N2[Inferior Good Branch]
    M --> N3[Luxury Good Branch]
    M --> N4[Necessity Good Branch]

    N1 --> O[Final Demand Output Qd]
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    N4 --> O

    P[Own Price Px] --> L
    P --> Q[Movement Along the Curve]
    O --> R[Aggregate Market Demand]
```

## 4.2 Shift vs. Movement — Visual Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Change in Determinant] --> B{Is it Own Price Px?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Movement Along the Curve]
    B -- No --> D[Shift of the Entire Curve]
    C --> E[Change in Quantity Demanded]
    D --> F{What is the Direction?}
    F -- Increase --> G[Rightward Shift - Demand Increases]
    F -- Decrease --> H[Leftward Shift - Demand Decreases]
    G --> I[Change in Demand]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Distinguish between "change in demand" and "change in quantity demanded."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Change in Demand | Change in Quantity Demanded |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Increase or decrease in the *entire* demand schedule due to non-price factors | Movement from one point to another *on the same* demand curve |
| **Cause** | Change in Y, Pᵣ, T, E, N, G, U (non-price factors) | Change in own price (Pₓ) only |
| **Graphical effect** | Shift of the *whole* curve (left or right) | Movement *along* the existing curve |
| **Nature** | Extension/contraction of the market | Expansion/contraction of purchase at one price |

> *[Allocated: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for cause, 1 mark for graphical distinction]*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. List any four exceptions to the Law of Demand.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Giffen Goods** — Inferior staple goods where price rise paradoxically increases demand (e.g., cheap bread for very poor households).
2. **Veblen Goods** — Status-symbol luxury goods where higher price enhances prestige and demand (e.g., Rolex watches, designer fashion).
3. **Expectation of Future Price Rise** — Hoarding and panic buying (e.g., gold, fuel, medicines).
4. **Emergency Necessities** — Life-saving goods (e.g., insulin, oxygen) whose demand is inelastic to price.

> *[Allocated: 0.75 marks per correct exception with one-line example]*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — **OR** — Question B (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain in detail the various non-price determinants of demand. How do they cause a shift of the demand curve?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *The demand function for a good is given by $Q_d = 200 - 4P_x + 3Y - 2P_r$, where $P_x$ is the own price, $Y$ is income (in ₹ thousands), and $P_r$ is the price of a related good. Initially, $P_x = 20$, $Y = 50$, $P_r = 30$. Find the initial quantity demanded. If income rises to ₹70 thousand and the price of the related good rises to ₹40, what is the new quantity demanded? Interpret the signs of the coefficients.*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> **[7 marks breakdown: 5 marks for explanation + 2 marks for shift logic]**

The **non-price determinants of demand** are variables *other than the own price* that influence the consumer's willingness and ability to purchase. They are:

**1. Income of the Consumer (Y) — [1 mark]**
- For a **normal good**, a rise in income increases demand (rightward shift).
- For an **inferior good**, a rise in income decreases demand (leftward shift).
- This is captured by income elasticity $E_y$.

**2. Prices of Related Goods (Pᵣ) — [1 mark]**
- **Substitutes** (tea, coffee): A rise in the price of one raises the demand for the other (rightward shift).
- **Complements** (cars, petrol): A rise in the price of one reduces the demand for the other (leftward shift).
- This is captured by cross-elasticity $E_{xy}$.

**3. Tastes and Preferences (T) — [1 mark]**
- Influenced by fashion, advertising, lifestyle, social trends.
- A positive shift in taste moves the curve rightward; a negative shift moves it leftward.

**4. Expectations of Future Prices and Income (E) — [1 mark]**
- Expected future price rise → current demand increases (rightward shift).
- Expected recession or future income fall → current demand decreases (leftward shift).

**5. Number of Buyers (N) — [0.5 mark]**
- Population growth, immigration, demographic shifts shift the market demand curve.

**6. Government Policy (G) — [0.5 mark]**
- Taxes, subsidies, price ceilings, and public schemes shift demand.

**How they cause a shift — [2 marks]**
- Unlike a price change (which causes movement *along* a fixed demand curve), any change in a non-price determinant changes the *entire relationship* between price and quantity.
- The curve repositions itself: a *favorable* change (e.g., higher income for a normal good) shifts the curve to the **right** (D → D′), meaning more is demanded at *every* price.
- An *unfavorable* change shifts the curve to the **left** (D → D″), meaning less is demanded at every price.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Demand Function:**
$$Q_d = 200 - 4P_x + 3Y - 2P_r$$

**Initial Conditions:** $P_x = 20$, $Y = 50$, $P_r = 30$

**Step 1 — Compute Initial Quantity Demanded [2 marks]**

$$Q_d^1 = 200 - 4(20) + 3(50) - 2(30)$$

$$Q_d^1 = 200 - 80 + 150 - 60$$

$$\boxed{Q_d^1 = 210 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 2 — New Conditions:** $P_x = 20$ (unchanged), $Y = 70$, $P_r = 40$ [2 marks]

**Step 3 — Compute New Quantity Demanded**

$$Q_d^2 = 200 - 4(20) + 3(70) - 2(40)$$

$$Q_d^2 = 200 - 80 + 210 - 80$$

$$\boxed{Q_d^2 = 250 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 4 — Change in Demand**

$$\Delta Q_d = Q_d^2 - Q_d^1 = 250 - 210 = +40 \text{ units (increase)}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation of Signs of Coefficients [3 marks]**

| Coefficient | Sign | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $-4$ (coefficient of $P_x$) | Negative | **Law of Demand holds** — inverse relationship between own price and quantity demanded. Good behaves normally. |
| $+3$ (coefficient of $Y$) | Positive | Good is a **normal good** — demand rises with consumer income. |
| $-2$ (coefficient of $P_r$) | Negative | Good X and Good r are **complements** — a rise in the price of the related good reduces demand for X. |

**Conclusion:** As income rose from ₹50k to ₹70k, demand for X increased by 90 units (3 × 20); but a rise in the related good's price reduced demand by 20 units (2 × 10). Net effect = +70 units of demand change, consistent with the computed increase of 40 units after also accounting for the constant own-price term baseline (200 − 80 = 120 baseline).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *What is demand? Explain the Law of Demand. Discuss its assumptions and exceptions with suitable examples.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *For two goods X and Y, the following data is observed: When the price of Y rises from ₹40 to ₹50, the quantity demanded of X falls from 200 units to 180 units. Calculate the cross-price elasticity of demand. Identify the relationship between X and Y. What happens to the demand for X if the price of Y rises by another 20%?*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition of Demand [1 mark]:**
> Demand is the *quantity* of a good or service that consumers are *willing* and *able* to purchase at various possible prices during a *given period of time*, subject to the *ceteris paribus* condition.

**Law of Demand [2 marks]:**
> *"Ceteris paribus, there is an inverse relationship between the price of a good and its quantity demanded."* As price increases, quantity demanded decreases, and vice versa.
> 
> Mathematically: $\dfrac{\partial Q_d}{\partial P_x} < 0$

**Assumptions of the Law [2 marks]:**
1. No change in consumer income.
2. No change in prices of related goods.
3. No change in tastes and preferences.
4. No change in expectations about future.
5. No change in the number of consumers.
6. No government intervention (taxes/subsidies constant).
7. The good is a *normal* good (no Giffen/Veblen behaviour).

**Exceptions to the Law [2 marks]:**
1. **Giffen Goods** — cheap staple goods where income effect dominates.
2. **Veblen Goods** — luxury status-symbol goods.
3. **Speculative demand** — buying in anticipation of shortage.
4. **Emergency necessities** — life-saving drugs, oxygen.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- $P_y$ changes from ₹40 → ₹50
- $Q_x$ changes from 200 → 180 units

**Step 1 — Compute % Change in $P_y$ [1 mark]**

$$\%\Delta P_y = \frac{50 - 40}{40} \times 100 = +25\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute % Change in $Q_x$ [1 mark]**

$$\%\Delta Q_x = \frac{180 - 200}{200} \times 100 = -10\%$$

**Step 3 — Apply Cross-Price Elasticity Formula [2 marks]**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\%\Delta Q_x}{\%\Delta P_y} = \frac{-10\%}{+25\%} = -0.4$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Relationship [1 mark]**

> Since $E_{xy} = -0.4 < 0$, goods X and Y are **complementary goods**.

**Step 5 — Effect of Further 20% Price Rise of Y [2 marks]**

> Using the same elasticity (assuming it remains approximately constant):
> 
> $$\%\Delta Q_x = E_{xy} \times \%\Delta P_y = (-0.4) \times (+20\%) = -8\%$$
> 
> $$\text{New } Q_x = 180 - 8\% \times 180 = 180 - 14.4 = 165.6 \text{ units}$$
> 
> **The demand for X will fall by approximately 8% (from 180 to ~165.6 units).**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — "Factors Determining Demand"**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "change in demand" with "change in quantity demanded"** — This is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Memorize: *price → movement; non-price → shift.*
> 
> 2. **Writing "tastes and preferences" without examples** — Examiners want real-world examples (EV demand, fitness trends, social media) — abstract writing gets only partial credit.
> 
> 3. **Confusing "substitutes" and "complements"** — Substitutes have *positive* cross-elasticity; complements have *negative* cross-elasticity. Reverse the sign → 0 marks.
> 
> 4. **Incomplete list of exceptions to the Law of Demand** — Always write at least 4 (Giffen, Veblen, speculation, emergency). Writing only 2 means losing 1 full mark.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to state units and sign interpretation in elasticity problems** — A numerical answer without interpretation of the sign is treated as incomplete. Always say: *"Since the coefficient is positive, the good is normal"*, etc.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** — For 7-mark analytical questions, a *labelled demand-curve diagram* showing shift vs. movement is expected. Drawing a curve without labels fetches 0 marks for the diagram component.
> 
> 7. **Mixing up "income elasticity" with "price elasticity"** — They measure *different* things. Ey uses income (Y); Ep uses own price (Px).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Factors Determining Demand**

- ✅ **Demand** = Willingness *and* ability to buy at a given price over a time period.
- ✅ **Law of Demand**: Ceteris paribus, P↑ → Qd↓ and P↓ → Qd↑.
- ✅ **General Demand Function**: $D_x = f(P_x, Y, P_r, T, E, N, G, U)$.
- ✅ **Linear demand form**: $Q_d = a - bP_x$ (with $a, b > 0$).
- ✅ **Income (Y) — Normal Good**: $E_y > 0$ → demand rises with income.
- ✅ **Income (Y) — Inferior Good**: $E_y < 0$ → demand falls with income.
- ✅ **Luxury Good**: $E_y > 1$; **Necessity**: $0 < E_y < 1$.
- ✅ **Substitutes**: $E_{xy} > 0$ (e.g., tea & coffee).
- ✅ **Complements**: $E_{xy} < 0$ (e.g., car & petrol, printer & ink).
- ✅ **Tastes & Preferences (T)**: shift the curve — rightward if positive, leftward if negative.
- ✅ **Expectations (E)**: expected future price rise → current demand ↑ (hoarding).
- ✅ **Number of Buyers (N)**: demographic growth → market demand ↑.
- ✅ **Government Policy (G)**: taxes ↓ demand; subsidies ↑ demand.
- ✅ **Credit Availability (U)**: cheap credit → big-ticket demand ↑.
- ✅ **Change in Demand** = *Shift* of the entire curve (caused by non-price factors).
- ✅ **Change in Quantity Demanded** = *Movement along* a fixed curve (caused by own price).
- ✅ **Exceptions to Law of Demand**: Giffen, Veblen, Speculative, Emergency, Snob appeal, Ignorance — list at least 4.
- ✅ **Three Elasticities** to master: $E_p$, $E_y$, $E_{xy}$ — know the formulas, signs, and interpretations.
- ✅ **Engineering applications**: forecasting, pricing, inventory, bundling, public policy.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on this topic, structure your answer in three layers: **(1) Definition/Concept → (2) Detailed explanation with formula/diagram → (3) Numerical/Analytical application**. Examiners award maximum marks only to structured, layered answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Law of Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Law of Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law of Demand (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *"Other things being equal (ceteris paribus), the quantity demanded of a commodity bears an **inverse relationship** with its own price. As the price of a commodity rises, the quantity demanded falls, and as the price falls, the quantity demanded rises, assuming all other determinants of demand remain constant."*

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$\text{Qd} = f(\text{P})$$

where the relationship is **inverse** or **negative** in nature.

---

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Precise KTU Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> The examiner **specifically tests** the difference between these three terms. Memorize them as boxed definitions.

| Term | Precise Definition | Example |
|------|-------------------|---------|
| **Demand (D)** | The *entire* willingness and ability of a consumer to purchase various quantities of a good at various prices during a specific time period. | A schedule of all possible (P, Qd) combinations. |
| **Quantity Demanded (Qd)** | The *specific* amount of a good that a consumer is willing and able to buy at a *particular* price. | "5 kg of rice at ₹40/kg." |
| **Ceteris Paribus** | Latin for "all other things being equal." The fundamental *assumption* of the Law of Demand. | Income, tastes, prices of related goods — all held constant. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap Water vs. Bottled Water" Intuition 💧

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Hook (Read this first if the topic feels abstract):**
>
> Imagine you walk into a mall and notice two stalls selling **bottled water (1 L)**. Stall A sells it for **₹20**, and Stall B sells it for **₹60**.
>
> - When the price is **low (₹20)**, you instinctively want to **buy more bottles** (one for now, one for the gym, one to keep in the car).
> - When the price **shoots up to ₹60**, your enthusiasm drops — maybe you'll buy **just one**, or even skip it.
>
> This everyday, almost reflexive behavior — *"price goes up → I buy less; price goes down → I buy more"* — is the **Law of Demand** in action. It's not a rule written in a textbook; it's a **human behavioral pattern** observed across virtually all markets.

---

## 1.4 Why This Law Matters for Engineers (KTU Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The UCHUT346 course positions Economics *for Engineers* — meaning you are expected to apply demand analysis to:
> - **Product pricing** in engineering ventures
> - **Market demand forecasting** for tech products
> - **Cost-volume-profit** decisions in industrial projects
> - **Break-even analysis** (covered later in Module 1)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Assumptions of the Law of Demand

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **assumptions** the examiner *will* expect you to state before defining the law. Losing marks here is a common pitfall.

The Law of Demand holds **only** when the following are satisfied:

1. **Ceteris Paribus** — Income, tastes, prices of related goods, and consumer expectations are all constant.
2. **Rational Consumer Behavior** — Consumers aim to maximize satisfaction (utility) per unit of money spent.
3. **No Change in Money Income** — The consumer's purchasing power is unchanged.
4. **Prices of Related Goods are Constant** — Substitutes and complements are stable in price.
5. **No Change in Tastes & Preferences** — Fashion, trends, and habits are stable.
6. **No Expectation of Future Price Changes** — Consumers don't speculate or hoard.
7. **Uniform Distribution of Income & Wealth** — No extreme inequality distorting aggregate behavior.

---

## 2.2 The Demand Schedule and the Demand Curve

### 2.2.1 Demand Schedule (Tabular Form)

A demand schedule is a **table** showing the quantity of a commodity demanded at various prices.

| Price per Unit (₹) | Quantity Demanded (Units) |
|---:|---:|
| 10 | 100 |
| 20 | 80 |
| 30 | 60 |
| 40 | 40 |
| 50 | 20 |
| 60 | 0 |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** As price **falls** from 60 to 10, quantity demanded **rises** from 0 to 100. This inverse pattern *is* the law in action.

### 2.2.2 Demand Curve (Graphical Form)

The demand curve is the **graphical representation** of the demand schedule. It is conventionally drawn with:
- **X-axis** → Quantity Demanded (Qd)
- **Y-axis** → Price (P)
- **Slope** → **Negative** (downward from left to right)

```
P (Price)
^
|  *  
|    *  
|      *  
|        *  
|          *  
|             *  
|___________________> Qd (Quantity Demanded)
```
*(The asterisks form a downward-sloping line — the demand curve.)*

---

## 2.3 The Demand Function (General Form)

The **general demand function** recognizes that demand depends on *multiple* variables, not just price:

$$\text{Qd} = f(\text{P}_x, \text{Y}, \text{P}_r, \text{T}, \text{E}, \text{N})$$

Where:

| Symbol | Variable | Typical Effect on Qd |
|------|---------|---------------------|
| $P_x$ | Price of the good itself | **Inverse** (this is the Law of Demand) |
| $Y$ | Consumer income | Positive (normal) or negative (inferior) |
| $P_r$ | Price of related goods | Positive (substitute) or negative (complement) |
| $T$ | Tastes and preferences | Positive |
| $E$ | Consumer expectations | Varies |
| $N$ | Number of consumers in market | Positive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the law says *"all other things equal,"* it means we **freeze** $Y$, $P_r$, $T$, $E$, and $N$, and study the relationship between $Qd$ and $P_x$ alone.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every Part A and Part B question on the Law of Demand.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Demand Function** | $Q_d = a - bP$ | $a$ = intercept, $b$ = slope coefficient, $P$ = price |
| 2 | **Inverse Demand Function** | $P = \dfrac{a}{b} - \dfrac{Q_d}{b}$ | Price expressed as a function of quantity |
| 3 | **Slope of Demand Curve** | $\dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} = -b$ | Always **negative** for a normal good |
| 4 | **Price Elasticity of Demand** | $E_d = \dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q_d}$ | **Dimensionless** ratio; negative sign often ignored |
| 5 | **Elasticity (Linear Demand Form)** | $E_d = \dfrac{-bP}{a - bP}$ | A function of the point chosen on the curve |
| 6 | **Total Revenue (TR)** | $TR = P \times Q_d$ | Rupees, dollars, etc. |
| 7 | **Total Revenue (Substitution)** | $TR = P(a - bP) = aP - bP^2$ | Quadratic in $P$ |
| 8 | **Marginal Revenue (MR)** | $MR = \dfrac{d(TR)}{dQ_d}$ | Change in TR per additional unit sold |
| 9 | **Point Elasticity Formula** | $E_d = \dfrac{\Delta Q_d}{\Delta P} \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q_d}$ | Used for discrete data |
| 10 | **Income Elasticity** | $E_y = \dfrac{dQ_d}{dY} \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Q_d}$ | Positive for normal goods, negative for inferior |
| 11 | **Cross Elasticity** | $E_{xy} = \dfrac{dQ_x}{dP_y} \cdot \dfrac{P_y}{Q_x}$ | Positive = substitute, negative = complement |

---

## 2.5 Why the Demand Curve Slopes Downward (KTU Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a 7-mark KTU short essay type question.** The examiner expects *at least three* of the following four reasons.

### Reason 1: The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility 📉
Each additional unit consumed yields **less satisfaction** than the previous one. To induce the consumer to buy one more unit, the **price must fall**.

### Reason 2: Substitution Effect 🔄
When the price of a good rises, it becomes **relatively more expensive** than its substitutes. Consumers switch to the cheaper alternatives, reducing quantity demanded of the original good.

### Reason 3: Income Effect 💰
A price increase effectively **reduces the real income** (purchasing power) of the consumer. With the same money income, the consumer can now buy fewer goods, so quantity demanded falls.

### Reason 4: Entry of New Consumers 👥
A lower price attracts consumers who were previously **priced out of the market**, increasing the total quantity demanded.

---

## 2.6 Exceptions to the Law of Demand (High-Yield)

> [!WARNING]
> The law is **not universal**. KTU examiners love asking about these exceptions.

| Exception | Mechanism | Example |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **Giffen Goods** | An *inferior* good where the income effect dominates the substitution effect. | Cheap staples like coarse rice during a famine. |
| **Veblen Goods (Prestige/Snob Goods)** | Higher price → higher status → higher demand. | Luxury watches, designer handbags, vintage wine. |
| **Emergency Necessities** | During crisis, demand rises *with* price due to panic buying. | Lamps during a power outage. |
| **Speculative Goods** | When consumers expect *future* price rises, current demand rises. | Gold during inflation expectations. |
| **Ignorance of Quality** | Consumer wrongly assumes higher price = higher quality. | "Expensive = better" fallacy. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From the Linear Demand Function to Point Elasticity

We will **derive** the elasticity formula for the linear demand function step by step.

### Given:
$$\text{Qd} = a - bP$$

where $a > 0$ and $b > 0$ are constants.

### Step 1: Compute the Slope of the Demand Curve

Differentiate $Q_d$ with respect to $P$:

$$\dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} = \dfrac{d}{dP}(a - bP)$$

$$\dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} = 0 - b$$

$$\dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} = -b$$

> The slope is **negative**, confirming the inverse relationship.

### Step 2: Recall the Definition of Price Elasticity of Demand

$$E_d = \dfrac{\text{Percentage change in } Q_d}{\text{Percentage change in } P}$$

$$E_d = \dfrac{\dfrac{\Delta Q_d}{Q_d}}{\dfrac{\Delta P}{P}}$$

In the **infinitesimal (point) form**, this becomes:

$$E_d = \dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q_d}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Slope into the Elasticity Formula

$$E_d = (-b) \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q_d}$$

$$E_d = \dfrac{-bP}{Q_d}$$

### Step 4: Express $Q_d$ in Terms of the Original Function

Since $Q_d = a - bP$, we substitute:

$$E_d = \dfrac{-bP}{a - bP}$$

$$\boxed{E_d = \dfrac{-bP}{a - bP}}$$

### Step 5: Interpret the Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Examiner's Favourite):**
> Even though the demand curve has a *constant slope* $(-b)$, the **elasticity is NOT constant** along a linear demand curve. It varies with the point $(P, Q_d)$ chosen.

| Point on Curve | Price Region | Elasticity $E_d$ | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top (high P, low Q) | $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ | **Elastic** | Demand is sensitive to price |
| Midpoint | $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ | **Unit Elastic** | TR is maximized here |
| Bottom (low P, high Q) | $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ | **Inelastic** | Demand is insensitive to price |

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!TIP]
> **Practice this style of problem** — it is the standard KTU Part B format.

### Problem:
The demand function for a product is given by:
$$Q_d = 200 - 4P$$

Find:
- (i) The price at which demand is zero (choke price)
- (ii) The price at which demand is unit elastic
- (iii) Total revenue at unit elasticity
- (iv) Marginal revenue function

### Solution:

**Step (i): Choke Price (where $Q_d = 0$)**

Set $Q_d = 0$:

$$0 = 200 - 4P$$

$$4P = 200$$

$$P = ₹50$$

> The **choke price** is ₹50 — above this, no consumer buys the product.

**Step (ii): Unit Elasticity Price**

Unit elasticity means $E_d = -1$ (or $\vert E_d \vert = 1$).

Using the formula $E_d = \dfrac{-bP}{a - bP}$ with $a = 200$, $b = 4$:

$$-1 = \dfrac{-4P}{200 - 4P}$$

Multiply both sides by $(200 - 4P)$:

$$-(200 - 4P) = -4P$$

$$-200 + 4P = -4P$$

$$8P = 200$$

$$P = ₹25$$

> At **P = ₹25**, demand is **unit elastic**. This is the midpoint of the demand curve.

**Step (iii): Total Revenue at Unit Elasticity**

First, find $Q_d$ at $P = 25$:

$$Q_d = 200 - 4(25) = 200 - 100 = 100 \text{ units}$$

Then, compute $TR$:

$$TR = P \times Q_d = 25 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{TR = ₹2500}$$

> **Insight:** $TR$ is *maximized* at the unit-elastic point. Below ₹25, $TR$ falls; above ₹25, $TR$ also falls.

**Step (iv): Marginal Revenue Function**

$$TR = P \times Q_d = P(200 - 4P) = 200P - 4P^2$$

$$MR = \dfrac{d(TR)}{dQ_d}$$

But $Q_d = 200 - 4P \Rightarrow P = 50 - \dfrac{Q_d}{4}$

So:

$$TR = \left(50 - \dfrac{Q_d}{4}\right)Q_d = 50Q_d - \dfrac{Q_d^2}{4}$$

Differentiate with respect to $Q_d$:

$$MR = 50 - \dfrac{2Q_d}{4}$$

$$MR = 50 - \dfrac{Q_d}{2}$$

$$\boxed{MR = 50 - \dfrac{Q_d}{2}}$$

> **Verification:** At $Q_d = 100$, $MR = 50 - 50 = 0$. This confirms that $TR$ is *maximized* when $MR = 0$, which occurs exactly at the unit-elastic point.

---

## 3.3 Movement Along vs. Shift of the Demand Curve (Critical Distinction)

> [!WARNING]
> This is the **single most commonly confused** concept in KTU exams. The examiner's valuation key will mark you down for mixing these up.

| Phenomenon | Cause | What Moves? | Curve Itself? | Graphical Sign |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Change in Quantity Demanded** | Change in **own price** ($P_x$) | A point slides **along** the existing demand curve. | The curve **stays put** | Movement from $A$ to $B$ along the same $D_1$ |
| **Change in Demand** | Change in **non-price factors** (income, tastes, related goods, etc.) | The **entire curve shifts**. | The curve **moves** to a new position $D_2$ | Shift from $D_1$ to $D_2$ or $D_0$ |

### Three Possible Shifts:

1. **Rightward Shift (Increase in Demand):** Rise in income (for a normal good), rise in price of substitute, positive change in taste.
2. **Leftward Shift (Decrease in Demand):** Fall in income (for a normal good), fall in price of substitute, negative change in taste.
3. **Rotation:** Changes in price expectations may cause the curve to rotate about its intercept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** "**Price changes cause movement; non-price factors cause shifts.**"

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Factors Affecting Demand

| # | Factor | Direction of Change | Effect on Demand Curve | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rise in consumer income | Income ↑ | Demand for **normal** good shifts right | Buying more cars with a raise |
| 2 | Rise in consumer income | Income ↑ | Demand for **inferior** good shifts left | Buying less instant noodles |
| 3 | Rise in price of substitute | $P_{sub}$ ↑ | Demand for the good shifts right | Tea price up → coffee demand up |
| 4 | Rise in price of complement | $P_{comp}$ ↑ | Demand for the good shifts left | Petrol price up → car demand down |
| 5 | Positive change in taste | Taste ↑ | Demand shifts right | Viral social media trend |
| 6 | Expectation of future price rise | Expected $P$ ↑ | Current demand shifts right | Pre-purchase of gold before Diwali |
| 7 | Increase in number of buyers | $N$ ↑ | Demand shifts right | Population growth → housing demand up |
| 8 | Seasonal change | Season ↑ | Demand shifts (varies) | AC demand in summer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Law of Demand Flow Architecture

This diagram visualizes the **causal architecture** behind the Law of Demand, showing how price and non-price factors independently affect quantity demanded.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Determinants of Demand] --> B[Own Price of Good Px]
    A --> C[Non-Price Factors]
    
    B --> D[Law of Demand Active]
    D --> E[Inverse Relationship]
    E --> F[Movement Along Demand Curve]
    
    C --> G[Consumer Income Y]
    C --> H[Prices of Related Goods Pr]
    C --> I[Tastes and Preferences T]
    C --> J[Expectations E]
    C --> K[Number of Buyers N]
    
    G --> L[Shift of Demand Curve]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    
    L --> M[Rightward Shift: Demand Increase]
    L --> N[Leftward Shift: Demand Decrease]
    
    F --> O[Final Outcome: Equilibrium Quantity]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    
    P[Ceteris Paribus Assumption] --> A
    P -.holds constant.-> C
    P -.isolates.-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:**
> - The **dashed lines** from `Ceteris Paribus Assumption` show the *constraint* that allows us to isolate the effect of own price.
> - **Solid arrows** represent causal influence.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Elasticity Regions on a Linear Demand Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Upper["UPPER REGION"]
        A1[High Price, Low Quantity] -->|Ed greater than 1| A2[ELASTIC REGION]
    end
    
    subgraph Middle["MIDPOINT"]
        B1[Ed equals 1] --> B2[UNIT ELASTIC POINT]
        B2 --> B3[Total Revenue Maximum]
    end
    
    subgraph Lower["LOWER REGION"]
        C1[Low Price, High Quantity] -->|Ed less than 1| C2[INELASTIC REGION]
    end
    
    Upper --> Middle --> Lower
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Application:** In industrial pricing, engineers and managers must identify *where on the demand curve their product currently sits* to make informed pricing decisions. A product in the elastic region benefits from price *reductions*; a product in the inelastic region can sustain price *increases* without losing demand.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Movement vs. Shift — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Demand Has Changed] --> Q1{What caused the change?}
    
    Q1 -->|Change in Own Price Px| M1[Movement Along Same Curve]
    M1 --> M2{Px increased or decreased?}
    M2 -->|Px increased| M3[Upward Movement, Qd decreases]
    M2 -->|Px decreased| M4[Downward Movement, Qd increases]
    
    Q1 -->|Change in Non-Price Factor| S1[Shift of Entire Curve]
    S1 --> S2{Direction of Shift?}
    S2 -->|Rightward| S3[Demand Increase, D1 to D2]
    S2 -->|Leftward| S4[Demand Decrease, D1 to D0]
    
    S1 --> S5[Non-Price Factor Source]
    S5 --> S51[Income]
    S5 --> S52[Related Goods Prices]
    S5 --> S53[Tastes]
    S5 --> S54[Expectations]
    S5 --> S55[Number of Buyers]
```

---

## 4.4 Demand Curve — Coordinate Plot (Schematic)

```
  Price (P)
    ^
    |  *  (Elastic Region)
 P  |     *
    |        *  <-- Midpoint: Unit Elastic, TR maximum
    |           *  (Inelastic Region)
    |              *
    |                 *
    |________________________> Quantity (Qd)
    0                          Q_max
```

**Key Points Labelled:**
- **P_max** (vertical intercept): The choke price — the price at which demand falls to zero.
- **Q_max** (horizontal intercept): The maximum quantity demanded when price is zero.
- **Midpoint:** Where $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ and Total Revenue is maximized.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Aligned with KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) Pattern:**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (Short Answer)
> - **Part B:** 1 question (with internal choice) × 14 marks = 14 marks
> - **Total Module Weightage:** 20 marks

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question | UCHUT346 Module 1]**

> **State and explain the Law of Demand. Why is the assumption of "ceteris paribus" essential to this law?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Law of Demand** states that, *ceteris paribus* (all other things being equal), there is an **inverse relationship** between the price of a commodity and the quantity demanded of that commodity. In other words, as the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded falls, and vice versa.

Mathematically:

$$Q_d = f(P), \quad \text{with} \quad \dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} < 0$$

The **ceteris paribus** assumption is essential because demand is influenced by several non-price factors — income, tastes, prices of related goods, expectations, and the number of buyers. If any of these factors were allowed to change simultaneously, we could not isolate the **pure effect of price** on quantity demanded. The assumption effectively *freezes* all other variables, allowing a clean causal interpretation: the observed change in quantity demanded is attributable solely to the change in price.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question | UCHUT346 Module 1]**

> **Distinguish between "change in demand" and "change in quantity demanded." Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Change in Quantity Demanded | Change in Demand |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Change in the *own price* of the good | Change in a *non-price factor* (income, taste, etc.) |
| **Graphical Effect** | Movement **along** the same demand curve | The entire demand curve **shifts** |
| **Direction** | Extension (down) or Contraction (up) | Increase (right shift) or Decrease (left shift) |

**Example 1 — Change in Quantity Demanded:** When the price of a litre of milk rises from ₹50 to ₹60, a household reduces its purchase from 2 litres to 1.5 litres per week. This is a movement *along* the same demand curve.

**Example 2 — Change in Demand:** During a viral fitness trend, consumer tastes shift in favour of protein supplements. At every price level, more units are demanded. The entire demand curve shifts **rightward**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you are expected to attempt **one** Part B question (with sub-parts), and internal choice is provided.

### ⭐ Question B-A (14 Marks) — *Attempt Any One Sub-Part Fully*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question | UCHUT346 Module 1]**

> **(a)** Explain any **four** reasons why the demand curve slopes downward from left to right. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** The demand function for a product is given by $Q_d = 240 - 6P$. Find:
> - (i) The price elasticity of demand at $P = ₹20$.
> - (ii) The price at which elasticity equals unity.
> - (iii) Total revenue at the unit-elastic point. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The demand curve slopes downward from left to right due to the following reasons:

**1. The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility (3 marks):**
According to this psychological law, as a consumer consumes additional units of a good, the marginal utility derived from each successive unit declines. To induce the consumer to purchase an additional unit, the price must be reduced. This inverse price-quantity relationship is what produces the downward slope.

**2. The Substitution Effect (2 marks):**
When the price of a good rises, it becomes relatively more expensive compared to its substitutes. Rational consumers switch to these cheaper alternatives, reducing the quantity demanded of the original good. Conversely, a price fall makes the good relatively cheaper, increasing its demand. This substitution behaviour reinforces the negative slope.

**3. The Income Effect (1 mark):**
A rise in price reduces the consumer's real income (purchasing power). With the same money income, the consumer can now afford fewer units of all goods, including this one. A price fall, conversely, increases real income and quantity demanded.

**4. Entry of New Consumers (1 mark):**
A lower price attracts consumers who were previously excluded from the market. These new buyers add to the total quantity demanded, reinforcing the inverse relationship.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $Q_d = 240 - 6P$, so $a = 240$ and $b = 6$.

**(i) Price Elasticity at $P = ₹20$:**

First, compute $Q_d$ at $P = 20$:

$$Q_d = 240 - 6(20) = 240 - 120 = 120 \text{ units}$$

Apply the elasticity formula:

$$E_d = \dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q_d} = -6 \cdot \dfrac{20}{120}$$

$$E_d = -6 \cdot \dfrac{1}{6} = -1$$

$$\boxed{E_d = -1 \quad \text{or} \quad \vert E_d \vert = 1 \text{ (Unit Elastic)}}$$

> **Insight:** At ₹20, demand is **unit elastic**.

**(ii) Price at which Elasticity Equals Unity:**

Unit elasticity means $\vert E_d \vert = 1$, so:

$$1 = \dfrac{6P}{240 - 6P}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$240 - 6P = 6P$$

$$240 = 12P$$

$$\boxed{P = ₹20}$$

> **Consistency Check:** This matches the result from part (i). At $P = ₹20$, the demand is unit elastic.

**(iii) Total Revenue at the Unit-Elastic Point:**

At $P = ₹20$, $Q_d = 120$ units.

$$TR = P \times Q_d = 20 \times 120$$

$$\boxed{TR = ₹2400}$$

> **Verifying maximization:** $TR = P(240 - 6P) = 240P - 6P^2$. Taking the derivative: $\dfrac{d(TR)}{dP} = 240 - 12P = 0 \Rightarrow P = 20$. This confirms $TR$ is *maximized* at $P = ₹20$.

---

### ⭐ Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question | UCHUT346 Module 1]**

> **(a)** What is meant by **elasticity of demand**? Derive the formula for price elasticity of demand from the linear demand function $Q_d = a - bP$. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **exceptions to the Law of Demand** with suitable examples. How do these exceptions affect the validity of the law? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Price Elasticity of Demand ($E_d$)** measures the *responsiveness* of quantity demanded to a change in the price of the good, all other factors held constant. It is defined as the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting with the linear demand function:

$$Q_d = a - bP$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate $Q_d$ with respect to $P$:

$$\dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} = -b$$

**Step 2:** Apply the elasticity definition:

$$E_d = \dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q_d}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} = -b$:

$$E_d = -b \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q_d}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $Q_d = a - bP$ back:

$$E_d = \dfrac{-bP}{a - bP}$$

**Step 5:** For ease of interpretation, the **absolute value** is often reported:

$$\vert E_d \vert = \dfrac{bP}{a - bP}$$

**Interpretation (within part a):**
- If $\vert E_d \vert > 1$: Demand is **elastic** (sensitive to price).
- If $\vert E_d \vert = 1$: Demand is **unit elastic** (TR is maximized).
- If $\vert E_d \vert < 1$: Demand is **inelastic** (insensitive to price).
- If $\vert E_d \vert = 0$: Demand is **perfectly inelastic** (vertical curve).
- If $\vert E_d \vert = \infty$: Demand is **perfectly elastic** (horizontal curve).

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

The Law of Demand is a *general tendency*, not a universal rule. Several important **exceptions** exist:

**1. Giffen Goods (2 marks):**
A Giffen good is a *strongly inferior* good in which the income effect of a price change is so powerful that it **overwhelms** the substitution effect. As a result, demand *rises* when price *rises*. The classic example is coarse rice during famines — when the price of rice rises, poor households can no longer afford pricier food items and are forced to consume *more* of the cheap staple, violating the law.

**2. Veblen Goods / Prestige Goods (2 marks):**
For *luxury* or *status* goods, a higher price is itself a sign of exclusivity and social prestige. Consumers buy *more* at higher prices to signal status. Examples: Rolex watches, designer handbags, vintage wines, luxury cars. The demand curve may slope *upward* in certain price ranges.

**3. Speculative Goods (1 mark):**
When consumers expect prices to *rise further* in the future, they buy *more* at current higher prices to avoid paying even more later. Example: hoarding of gold or real estate during inflationary expectations.

**4. Goods of Conspicuous Consumption (1 mark):**
Closely related to Veblen goods, this refers to goods whose consumption value lies partly in being *seen* by others. Higher price enhances perceived value.

**5. Emergency / Panic Demand (1 mark):**
During emergencies (floods, pandemics, wars), demand for essentials like medicines, fuel, and food *rises* with price due to panic buying and scarcity psychology.

**Impact on Validity (within part b):**
These exceptions do **not** invalidate the Law of Demand — they define its **boundaries of applicability**. The law holds robustly for the vast majority of normal goods under ordinary conditions. Exceptions merely highlight that the law is a *general principle* with specific conditions (ceteris paribus) and assumptions (rational consumer, no speculation, no panic) under which it operates.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on "Law of Demand" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting "Ceteris Paribus"** — The single most common 1-mark loss. Always state the assumption *first* before defining the law.
> 2. **Confusing Movement with Shift** — Writing "demand increases when price falls" is imprecise. Correct phrasing: "quantity demanded increases when price falls." "Demand" refers to the *curve*; "quantity demanded" refers to a *point on the curve*.
> 3. **Sign Conventions in Elasticity** — Reporting $E_d = -1$ without specifying $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ can be penalized. Always clarify whether you are using the signed or absolute value.
> 4. **Mixing Up TR Maximization Conditions** — TR is maximized at unit elasticity, not at the midpoint of the price axis. Don't confuse the algebraic midpoint with the economic midpoint.
> 5. **Skipping Steps in Derivations** — A 7-mark derivation question expects **all five** steps shown explicitly. Skipping a substitution step can cost 2 marks.
> 6. **Omitting Units** — Always state units (₹, units, ₹/unit) in numerical answers. KTU evaluators reward completeness.
> 7. **Listing Exceptions Without Explanation** — Naming Giffen goods is not enough; briefly explain *why* they violate the law (income effect dominates substitution effect).
> 8. **Writing "Demand = Want"** — Demand requires both *willingness* and *ability* to pay. A want without purchasing power is not demand.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this and revise 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ⭐ The **Law of Demand** states an **inverse** relationship between price and quantity demanded, *ceteris paribus*.
- ⭐ The demand curve is **downward sloping** (negatively sloped) for normal goods.
- ⭐ The **general demand function** is $Q_d = f(P_x, Y, P_r, T, E, N)$.
- ⭐ The **linear demand function** is $Q_d = a - bP$, with slope $-b$ and intercept $a$.
- ⭐ The **price elasticity formula** is $E_d = \dfrac{dQ_d}{dP} \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q_d} = \dfrac{-bP}{a - bP}$.
- ⭐ Elasticity is **NOT constant** along a linear demand curve, even though the slope is.
- ⭐ **Unit elasticity** occurs at the *midpoint* of a linear demand curve, where $TR$ is **maximized**.
- ⭐ **Change in own price** → **Movement along** the demand curve (change in $Q_d$).
- ⭐ **Change in non-price factors** → **Shift** of the demand curve (change in demand).
- ⭐ **Four reasons for downward slope:** Diminishing marginal utility, substitution effect, income effect, entry of new consumers.
- ⭐ **Five key exceptions:** Giffen goods, Veblen goods, speculative goods, conspicuous consumption, emergency/panic demand.
- ⭐ For **normal goods**, $\dfrac{dQ_d}{dY} > 0$; for **inferior goods**, $\dfrac{dQ_d}{dY} < 0$.
- ⭐ **Substitutes** have positive cross-elasticity; **complements** have negative cross-elasticity.
- ⭐ Always state the **ceteris paribus** assumption explicitly — it is a hallmark of a well-written answer.
- ⭐ In numerical problems, **show all five steps** of the elasticity derivation for full marks.

> **End of Module 1 — Law of Demand Study Notes**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: DEMAND ANALYSIS & PRICE ELASTICITY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Demand Function & The Demand Curve

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Demand Function** expresses the functional relationship between the quantity demanded of a commodity ($Q_d$) and its various determinants. The most fundamental form isolates the **own price** ($P$) of the commodity as the key explanatory variable, holding all other factors constant (*ceteris paribus*).

Mathematically, the individual demand function is expressed as:

$$Q_d^x = f(P_x, P_r, P_c, Y, T, E, A, N, \ldots)$$

Where the explanatory variables are defined below in the standard notation used in the KTU 2024 Engineering Economics syllabus:

- $Q_d^x$ = Quantity demanded of good $X$
- $P_x$ = Price of the good $X$ itself
- $P_r$ = Prices of **related goods** (substitutes and complements)
- $P_c$ = **Perceived production cost** expectation by the consumer
- $Y$ = **Income** of the consumer
- $T$ = **Tastes and preferences**
- $E$ = **Expectations** about future prices and income
- $A$ = **Advertising** and promotional expenditure
- $N$ = **Number of buyers** in the market

When all variables except $P_x$ are held constant, the **inverse demand function** $P = f(Q_d)$ is plotted on a two-dimensional graph with **price on the Y-axis** and **quantity on the X-axis**. This plot is called the **Demand Curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Law of Demand (Alfred Marshall's Postulate):**
> *"Other things being equal, the quantity demanded of a commodity varies inversely with its price."* This inverse relationship yields a demand curve that **slopes downward from left to right**, with the **negative slope** being the universal signature of a normal demand curve.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Why Does the Demand Curve Slope Downward?

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bottle in a Marathon" Story:**
> Imagine you are running a marathon on a hot day. At the **5th kilometre**, you are mildly thirsty. The organizer is selling water bottles at **₹200 per bottle**. Most runners in your group will hesitate and decide to wait.
>
> Now, picture the **20th kilometre**. You are exhausted, severely dehydrated, and your body is screaming for water. The same bottle is now being sold for **₹500**. Despite the higher price, you will pay it without blinking.
>
> This real-life behaviour illustrates the two foundational forces that make the demand curve slope downward:
>
> 1. **The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility** — As you consume successive units of a good, the additional satisfaction derived from each new unit falls. Therefore, you are willing to pay a **high price for the first few units** (when utility is high) and a **lower price for additional units** (when marginal utility is low). This creates the inverse price-quantity trade-off.
>
> 2. **The Income Effect** — When the price of a good falls, your **real income** (purchasing power) effectively rises. You can now buy the same quantity with money left over, or buy more units with the same money. Either way, quantity demanded rises.
>
> 3. **The Substitution Effect** — When the price of, say, Maggi noodles rises, you instinctively look for alternatives (Yippee, Top Ramen). You *substitute* away from the expensive good toward its relatively cheaper substitutes, increasing the quantity demanded of the cheaper alternatives.

### 1.3 Price Elasticity of Demand — The Headline Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Alfred Marshall, 1890 — *Principles of Economics*):**
> **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED or $E_d$)** is the **degree of responsiveness** of the quantity demanded of a commodity to a change in its own price, everything else held constant. It is defined as the **ratio of the percentage change in quantity demanded** to the **percentage change in price**.

The mathematical statement is:

$$E_d = \frac{\text{Percentage Change in Quantity Demanded}}{\text{Percentage Change in Price}} = \frac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Price Elasticity critical for Engineers and Managers?**
> In engineering economics, the concept of elasticity is the **bridge between qualitative demand analysis and quantitative business decisions**. A civil engineer evaluating a toll-road project, a software engineer pricing a SaaS product, or a mechanical engineer deciding on production volume for a new model of an electric vehicle — *all of them* need to know how sensitive customer demand is to price changes. The elasticity coefficient gives them this single, dimensionless, comparable metric.

### 1.4 The "Elastic" vs. "Inelastic" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Stretchy Rubber Band" Metaphor:**
> Hold a rubber band. Pull it slightly and it stretches a lot — that is **elastic demand** (a small price change causes a large change in quantity demanded, like luxury goods, restaurant meals, foreign travel).
>
> Now look at a **steel rod**. Push or pull it gently and it barely budges — that is **inelastic demand** (a price change causes almost no change in quantity demanded, like life-saving medicines, salt, rice for a poor household, petrol in the short run).
>
> The **elasticity coefficient $E_d$** is the mathematical "stretchiness meter" that quantifies this intuition precisely.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions

The elasticity coefficient is a **pure number** (dimensionless), bounded in most practical situations by:

- **$E_d = 0$** → Perfectly Inelastic (vertical demand curve)
- **$0 < E_d < 1$** → Relatively Inelastic (steep curve, less responsive)
- **$E_d = 1$** → Unit Elastic (a special 45° hyperbolic curve under constant revenue)
- **$1 < E_d < \infty$** → Relatively Elastic (flat curve, highly responsive)
- **$E_d = \infty$** → Perfectly Elastic (horizontal demand curve)

By convention, the **absolute value (modulus) of $E_d$** is used for classification, since the law of demand guarantees that $\Delta Q$ and $\Delta P$ move in opposite directions, making the raw coefficient **negative**. Hence, we write the standard inequality as $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ for elastic demand.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The Three Pillars of Downward Slope

The demand curve's negative slope is not a single phenomenon; it is the **cumulative outcome of three sub-effects** that KTU examiners frequently test in Part A questions:

#### Pillar 1: The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility
- As a consumer purchases successive units of a good (1st, 2nd, 3rd...), the **marginal utility (MU)** of each additional unit declines.
- The consumer's **willingness to pay** for each unit is anchored to its MU.
- Therefore, the consumer pays a high price for the first unit (high MU) and progressively less for subsequent units (lower MU). This **price-quantity trade-off** is the microeconomic engine of the demand curve.

#### Pillar 2: The Income Effect
- When price $P$ falls, the consumer's **real income** $M/P$ rises.
- For a **normal good**, this increased real income induces the consumer to buy more.
- For an **inferior good**, the consumer may buy less (a negative income effect that can dominate the substitution effect, leading to a positively-sloped **Giffen demand curve** — an advanced KTU-level concept).

#### Pillar 3: The Substitution Effect
- When $P_x$ falls, good $X$ becomes **relatively cheaper** compared to its substitutes.
- The consumer rationally **reallocates consumption** from expensive substitutes toward the now-cheaper good $X$, increasing $Q_d^x$.

### 2.2 The Total Outlay (Total Revenue) Test — Marshall's Heuristic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alfred Marshall's Total Outlay Test:**
> This is a **non-mathematical, observational method** to determine the elasticity of demand at a point by observing the behaviour of the **Total Revenue (TR) = Price × Quantity** when price changes.
>
> Marshall showed that the **movement of TR in response to a price fall** acts as a "thermometer" for elasticity.

| Observation When Price Falls | Implied Elasticity | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Total Outlay (TR) **rises** | $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ (Elastic) | Quantity rises by a *larger %* than price falls |
| Total Outlay (TR) **falls** | $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ (Inelastic) | Quantity rises by a *smaller %* than price falls |
| Total Outlay (TR) is **unchanged** | $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ (Unit Elastic) | Quantity rises by *exactly* the same % as price falls |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Total Outlay Test (Price Falls):** **"E-I-U"** → **E**lastic (TR up), **I**nelastic (TR down), **U**nit (TR same). Reverse the logic when price rises.

### 2.3 Geometric / Point Elasticity — The Slope-Intercept Insight

For a **linear (straight-line) demand curve** of the form $P = a - bQ$, the point elasticity at any point $(Q, P)$ is given by:

$$E_d = \frac{P}{Q} \cdot \frac{1}{\vert \text{slope} \vert} = \frac{\text{Lower segment length}}{\text{Upper segment length}}$$

The right-hand formula is **Marshall's geometric theorem**: at any point on a linear demand curve, the elasticity equals the ratio of the **lower segment** (from that point to the X-axis intercept) divided by the **upper segment** (from that point to the Y-axis intercept). This yields the powerful result:

- At the **midpoint** of any linear demand curve, $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ (unit elastic).
- **Above the midpoint** (high price, low quantity), $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ (elastic).
- **Below the midpoint** (low price, high quantity), $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ (inelastic).
- At the **Y-intercept** (price-axis), $\vert E_d \vert = \infty$ (perfectly elastic).
- At the **X-intercept** (quantity-axis), $\vert E_d \vert = 0$ (perfectly inelastic).

### 2.4 Arc Elasticity — The Midpoint Formula

A major problem with the simple percentage method is that it gives **different values** depending on whether you measure the change from point 1 to point 2 or from point 2 to point 1. To overcome this asymmetry, the **Arc Elasticity formula** (also called the **midpoint method** or **arc method of elasticity**) was developed:

$$E_d^{arc} = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_2 + Q_1} \cdot \frac{P_2 + P_1}{P_2 - P_1}$$

By using the **average (mean) of the two prices and quantities** as the base, the arc method yields a **unique, symmetric value** of elasticity between the two points, regardless of the direction of measurement.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula, boundary condition, and classification rule** the KTU 2024 Scheme examiner can test on this topic. **Memorize the boundary conditions and the sign conventions precisely — these are common sources of mark loss.**

| **Formula / Rule** | **Mathematical Statement** | **KTU Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition of $E_d$ (Proportional Method) | $E_d = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}$ | Direct percentage change calculation |
| Marshall's Point Formula | $E_d = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | Using **initial** $P$ and $Q$ as base |
| Arc Elasticity (Midpoint Method) | $E_d = \dfrac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_2 + Q_1} \cdot \dfrac{P_2 + P_1}{P_2 - P_1}$ | Using **average** $P$ and $Q$ as base |
| Geometric (Linear Curve) | $E_d = \dfrac{\text{Lower segment}}{\text{Upper segment}}$ | Slopes and intercept-based problems |
| Total Revenue Identity | $TR = P \times Q$ | Output of Total Outlay Test |
| $\dfrac{d(TR)}{dP}$ relationship | $\dfrac{d(TR)}{dP} = Q \left(1 - \vert E_d \vert\right)$ | Marginal Revenue linkage |
| Elastic demand | $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ | Steep fall in TR when P rises |
| Inelastic demand | $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ | Steep rise in TR when P rises |
| Unit elastic demand | $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ | TR at its maximum value |
| Perfectly elastic | $E_d = \infty$ | Horizontal demand curve |
| Perfectly inelastic | $E_d = 0$ | Vertical demand curve |
| Sign convention | $E_d$ is **negative**; $\vert E_d \vert$ is reported | Always quote absolute value in classification |

### 2.6 The Engineering-Economics Utility of $E_d$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Application Matrix for B.Tech Students:**
>
> - **Pricing a new electric vehicle** (Mechanical / Electrical): A startup needs to know whether a 5% price hike will lose them 2% customers (inelastic — go ahead) or 25% customers (elastic — hold the price).
> - **Toll road concession** (Civil): The NHAI concessionaire uses elasticity to forecast traffic volume as a function of toll hikes.
> - **SaaS subscription model** (Computer Science): Software firms use elasticity to optimize freemium-to-paid conversion pricing tiers.
> - **Government excise duty** (Public Policy / Economics): The Finance Ministry's decision to tax petrol heavily relies on the knowledge that petrol demand in the short run is **highly inelastic** (no immediate substitutes), so a tax hike raises revenue without much consumption loss.
> - **Demand forecasting for inventory** (Industrial Engineering): Warehouse stocking decisions for spare parts and raw materials are calibrated using elasticity estimates.
