## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Definition to Marshall's Point Formula

We begin from the conceptual definition of elasticity as the ratio of percentage changes:

$$E_d = \frac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P} = \frac{\dfrac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_1} \times 100}{\dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1} \times 100}$$

The factor of 100 in the numerator and denominator cancels out:

$$E_d = \frac{\dfrac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_1}}{\dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1}}$$

Dividing by a fraction is equivalent to multiplying by its reciprocal:

$$E_d = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{P_2 - P_1} \cdot \frac{P_1}{Q_1}$$

This is the **Marshallian point elasticity formula**, which uses the **initial price $P_1$ and initial quantity $Q_1$** as the base. The notation can be compactly written as:

$$E_d = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \frac{P}{Q} \quad \text{(using initial values as base)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of each step:** The first line expresses "percentage change" as the standard arithmetic definition. The "×100" is a unit-conversion that cancels out on both sides. The final rearrangement simply puts the price in the numerator (the variable in the denominator of the original % change) and the quantity in the denominator — yielding a compact, evaluable formula.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Arc Elasticity (Midpoint) Formula

To eliminate the asymmetry of the point formula, replace the base values $P_1$ and $Q_1$ with the **arithmetic mean** of the two endpoints:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad \bar{Q} = \frac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2}$$

Substituting these average values into Marshall's point formula:

$$E_d^{arc} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \frac{\bar{P}}{\bar{Q}} = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{P_2 - P_1} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{P_1 + P_2}{2}}{\dfrac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2}}$$

Multiplying the two fractions and simplifying (the factor of 2 in numerator and denominator cancels):

$$E_d^{arc} = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{P_2 - P_1} \cdot \frac{P_1 + P_2}{Q_1 + Q_2}$$

This is the standard **Arc Elasticity of Demand** formula. By construction, it yields the **same value** whether we go from point 1 to point 2 or point 2 to point 1, because the midpoint is invariant.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Marginal Revenue — Elasticity Linkage

This is a **favourite KTU Part B question** because it bridges two modules (demand and revenue).

Total Revenue is given by:

$$TR = P \cdot Q$$

Taking the derivative of $TR$ with respect to $Q$ (using the product rule, treating $P$ as a function of $Q$):

$$\frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = \frac{d(P \cdot Q)}{dQ} = P \cdot \frac{dQ}{dQ} + Q \cdot \frac{dP}{dQ} = P + Q \cdot \frac{dP}{dQ}$$

$$\frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = P \left( 1 + \frac{Q}{P} \cdot \frac{dP}{dQ} \right)$$

Now, by the definition of elasticity, the **price elasticity of demand** is:

$$E_d = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q} \implies \frac{dP}{dQ} \cdot \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{1}{E_d}$$

Substituting this reciprocal relationship into the MR expression:

$$\frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = P \left( 1 + \frac{1}{E_d} \right) = P \left( \frac{E_d + 1}{E_d} \right)$$

Therefore, the **Marginal Revenue** is:

$$MR = P \left( 1 + \frac{1}{E_d} \right) = P \left( \frac{E_d + 1}{E_d} \right)$$

Or equivalently, expressing in absolute value terms (since $E_d$ is negative):

$$MR = P \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\vert E_d \vert} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Economic Insights from this Derivation (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
>
> 1. When $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ (elastic demand), $MR > 0$. A firm can lower price and still raise total revenue.
> 2. When $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ (inelastic demand), $MR < 0$. Lowering price *reduces* total revenue.
> 3. When $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ (unit elastic), $MR = 0$. Total revenue is at its **maximum** value — a critical profit-maximization insight.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Examples

#### Example 1: Point Elasticity (Marshall's Method)

**Problem:** The price of a commodity falls from **₹20 to ₹18 per unit**, and as a result, the quantity demanded rises from **100 units to 120 units**. Calculate the price elasticity of demand.

**Solution using the percentage method:**

Step 1 — Calculate the change in price:

$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = 18 - 20 = -2$$

Step 2 — Calculate the change in quantity:

$$\Delta Q = Q_2 - Q_1 = 120 - 100 = +20$$

Step 3 — Compute the percentage changes (using initial values as base, per Marshall's method):

$$\%\Delta P = \frac{\Delta P}{P_1} \times 100 = \frac{-2}{20} \times 100 = -10\%$$

$$\%\Delta Q = \frac{\Delta Q}{Q_1} \times 100 = \frac{+20}{100} \times 100 = +20\%$$

Step 4 — Compute the elasticity ratio:

$$E_d = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P} = \frac{+20\%}{-10\%} = -2$$

Step 5 — Take the absolute value for classification:

$$\vert E_d \vert = 2 > 1 \implies \text{Demand is ELASTIC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 1% fall in price leads to a 2% rise in quantity demanded. The good is **price-sensitive** (luxury-type behaviour). The firm can afford to cut price because revenue will rise.

#### Example 2: Arc Elasticity (Midpoint Method) — Same Data

**Solution using the arc method:**

Step 1 — Identify the values:

$$P_1 = 20, \quad P_2 = 18, \quad Q_1 = 100, \quad Q_2 = 120$$

Step 2 — Compute the average price and average quantity:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2} = \frac{20 + 18}{2} = 19$$

$$\bar{Q} = \frac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2} = \frac{100 + 120}{2} = 110$$

Step 3 — Compute changes in price and quantity:

$$\Delta P = 18 - 20 = -2, \quad \Delta Q = 120 - 100 = +20$$

Step 4 — Apply the arc formula:

$$E_d^{arc} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \frac{\bar{P}}{\bar{Q}} = \frac{+20}{-2} \cdot \frac{19}{110}$$

$$E_d^{arc} = -10 \times 0.1727 = -1.727$$

Step 5 — Take the absolute value:

$$\vert E_d^{arc} \vert \approx 1.73 \implies \text{Demand is still ELASTIC, but the magnitude is smaller than Marshall's point estimate}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation for KTU:** Notice that the arc method gives a *different* value (1.73) than the point method (2.00). This is the **standard KTU trap** — examiners often give a single dataset and ask students to choose the appropriate method. Use **arc elasticity** when the question says "between two prices" or gives two distinct points; use **point elasticity** when the question asks for elasticity "at a particular price" or near a specific point.

#### Example 3: Geometric Elasticity on a Linear Demand Curve

**Problem:** A linear demand curve passes through the points $(Q=0, P=100)$ and $(Q=100, P=0)$. Find the price elasticity of demand at the point $(Q=50, P=50)$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the segments.

The upper segment runs from the point $(50, 50)$ up to the Y-intercept $(0, 100)$. Its **length along the curve** is geometrically proportional to the price drop, but in Marshall's ratio theorem we use the **intercept distances**:

$$\text{Upper segment} = \text{distance from point to Y-intercept} = 100 - 50 = 50$$

$$\text{Lower segment} = \text{distance from point to X-intercept} = 50 - 0 = 50$$

Step 2 — Apply the geometric formula:

$$E_d = \frac{\text{Lower segment}}{\text{Upper segment}} = \frac{50}{50} = 1$$

Step 3 — Conclude:

$$\vert E_d \vert = 1 \implies \text{Unit Elastic (as expected at the midpoint of any linear demand curve)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization:** At any point on a linear demand curve, the **midpoint always yields unit elasticity**. Points above the midpoint are elastic, points below are inelastic. This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

#### Example 4: Total Outlay Test

**Problem:** When the price of good X is reduced from ₹50 to ₹40, the quantity demanded rises from 20 units to 22 units. Determine the elasticity using the Total Outlay method.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute Total Outlay at $P_1$:

$$TR_1 = P_1 \times Q_1 = 50 \times 20 = ₹1000$$

Step 2 — Compute Total Outlay at $P_2$:

$$TR_2 = P_2 \times Q_2 = 40 \times 22 = ₹880$$

Step 3 — Compare:

$$TR_2 < TR_1 \quad (\text{from } ₹1000 \text{ to } ₹880)$$

Step 4 — Apply the rule (Price fell, TR fell, so demand is **Inelastic**):

$$\vert E_d \vert < 1 \implies \text{Inelastic Demand}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using percentage method:** $\%\Delta P = -20\%$, $\%\Delta Q = +10\%$, so $E_d = 10/-20 = -0.5$, $\vert E_d \vert = 0.5 < 1$. ✅ Confirmed inelastic.

### 3.5 Summary of All Three Methods — Comparative Matrix

| **Method** | **Formula** | **Base Used** | **Symmetry** | **Best Used When** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Percentage (Proportional)** | $E_d = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P}$ | Initial values | Asymmetric (direction-dependent) | Quick conceptual checks |
| **Point (Marshall's)** | $E_d = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{Q_1}$ | Initial $P_1, Q_1$ | Asymmetric | Elasticity at a specific point |
| **Arc (Midpoint)** | $E_d = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \dfrac{P_1+P_2}{Q_1+Q_2}$ | Average $\bar{P}, \bar{Q}$ | **Symmetric** | Two distinct given points |
| **Geometric** | $E_d = \dfrac{\text{Lower segment}}{\text{Upper segment}}$ | Visual segments | Symmetric on linear curves | Linear demand curves |
| **Total Outlay Test** | Observation of TR movement | Observed TR | N/A | Quick non-mathematical check |

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Block Architecture — The Three Methods of Measuring Price Elasticity

This flowchart maps the **functional architecture** of the elasticity measurement system used in KTU 2024 syllabus, showing the input data flow, decision branches, and output classification for each measurement method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DEMAND DATA INPUT<br/>Price P1 and P2<br/>Quantity Q1 and Q2"]
    nodeB{"Which Method<br/>is Required?"}
    nodeC["PERCENTAGE METHOD<br/>Use Initial P1 and Q1 as base<br/>Direct Ratio of Percentage Changes"]
    nodeD["POINT ELASTICITY<br/>Marshallian Formula<br/>Use Initial Values"]
    nodeE["ARC ELASTICITY<br/>Midpoint Formula<br/>Use Average of P and Q"]
    nodeF["GEOMETRIC METHOD<br/>For Linear Demand Curves<br/>Lower Segment / Upper Segment"]
    nodeG["TOTAL OUTLAY TEST<br/>Non-Mathematical<br/>Observe TR Movement"]
    nodeH{"Magnitude<br/>Classification"}
    nodeI["ABSOLUTE VALUE COMPUTED<br/>EQUAL TO INFINITY<br/>Perfectly Elastic Demand"]
    nodeJ["ABSOLUTE VALUE GREATER THAN 1<br/>Relatively Elastic Demand"]
    nodeK["ABSOLUTE VALUE EQUAL TO 1<br/>Unit Elastic Demand"]
    nodeL["ABSOLUTE VALUE LESS THAN 1<br/>Relatively Inelastic Demand"]
    nodeM["ABSOLUTE VALUE EQUAL TO 0<br/>Perfectly Inelastic Demand"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|Single Point Given| nodeD
    nodeB -->|Two Distinct Points| nodeE
    nodeB -->|Linear Curve Drawn| nodeF
    nodeB -->|TR Data Given| nodeG
    nodeB -->|Quick Conceptual Check| nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -->|ED equals infinity| nodeI
    nodeH -->|ED greater than 1| nodeJ
    nodeH -->|ED equals 1| nodeK
    nodeH -->|ED between 0 and 1| nodeL
    nodeH -->|ED equals 0| nodeM
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Taxonomy — The Five Zones of Elasticity on a Linear Demand Curve

This schematic illustrates how a single straight-line demand curve is partitioned into **five economic regions** of elasticity behaviour. Each region corresponds to a different business decision.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeY["Y-AXIS INTERCEPT<br/>Price Maximum<br/>ED equals INFINITY<br/>Perfectly Elastic<br/>Horizontal Tangent"]
    nodeE["ELASTIC ZONE<br/>Upper Half of Curve<br/>ED GREATER THAN 1<br/>Price Cut Raises TR<br/>Quantity Highly Responsive"]
    nodeU["MIDPOINT<br/>Geometric Centre<br/>ED EQUALS 1<br/>Unit Elastic<br/>TR at Maximum Value"]
    nodeI["INELASTIC ZONE<br/>Lower Half of Curve<br/>ED LESS THAN 1<br/>Price Cut Lowers TR<br/>Quantity Poorly Responsive"]
    nodeX["X-AXIS INTERCEPT<br/>Quantity Maximum<br/>ED EQUALS 0<br/>Perfectly Inelastic<br/>Vertical Tangent"]

    nodeY -.-> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeU
    nodeU --> nodeI
    nodeI -.-> nodeX
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Application Flow — How a Firm Uses Elasticity in Pricing Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Firm Faces a Pricing Decision<br/>Should we raise or lower price?"]
    nodeData["Collect Demand Data<br/>Observe Q at Multiple P Levels"]
    nodeCompute["Compute ED at Current Price<br/>Use Point or Arc Method"]
    nodeCheck{"Is Absolute ED<br/>Greater Than 1?"}
    nodeElast["ELASTIC REGION<br/>Price Cut RAISES TR<br/>Price Hike LOWERS TR<br/>Optimal Strategy: CUT price to grow volume"]
    nodeInelast["INELASTIC REGION<br/>Price Cut LOWERS TR<br/>Price Hike RAISES TR<br/>Optimal Strategy: HIKE price to grow revenue"]
    nodeUnit["UNIT ELASTIC REGION<br/>Any price change leaves TR unchanged<br/>Optimal Strategy: STABLE pricing to avoid revenue loss"]
    nodeEnd["Apply Strategy and Monitor<br/>Re-estimate ED after policy change"]

    nodeStart --> nodeData
    nodeData --> nodeCompute
    nodeCompute --> nodeCheck
    nodeCheck -->|Yes| nodeElast
    nodeCheck -->|No, ED less than 1| nodeInelast
    nodeCheck -->|Yes, ED equals 1| nodeUnit
    nodeElast --> nodeEnd
    nodeInelast --> nodeEnd
    nodeUnit --> nodeEnd
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: The Total Revenue — Elasticity Relationship Table

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTop["TOTAL REVENUE BEHAVIOUR<br/>AS A FUNCTION OF ELASTICITY"]
```

### 4.5 ASCII Schematic of a Standard Downward-Sloping Linear Demand Curve

For students who need a quick visual reference, the following ASCII schematic represents a linear demand curve with the five elasticity zones marked:

```
   P (Price)
   ^
   |  P_max  *<-- Y-Intercept (P-axis): ED = Infinity (Perfectly Elastic)
   |          |\
   |          | \
   |          |  \   <-- ELASTIC ZONE: ED > 1
   |          |   \
   |          |    \
   |          |     *  <-- MIDPOINT: ED = 1 (Unit Elastic)
   |          |      |
   |          |      |  <-- INELASTIC ZONE: ED < 1
   |          |      |
   |          |      |
   |          |      *  <-- X-Intercept (Q-axis): ED = 0 (Perfectly Inelastic)
   |__________|______|__________________________> Q (Quantity)
              0     Q_max
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The Y-axis intercept is the maximum price at which demand is zero. The X-axis intercept is the maximum quantity demanded at zero price. The midpoint is the unique point where elasticity equals 1. Above the midpoint, every point is elastic; below, every point is inelastic.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Define Price Elasticity of Demand. Explain the Total Outlay method of measuring it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Price Elasticity of Demand ($E_d$) is the degree of responsiveness of quantity demanded of a commodity to a change in its price. It is measured as the ratio of the percentage change in quantity demanded to the percentage change in price.

> **Total Outlay Method (2 marks):** Proposed by Prof. Alfred Marshall, this method measures elasticity by observing the behaviour of **Total Outlay (TO) = Price × Quantity** in response to a price change:
> - If a price **fall** leads to a **rise** in Total Outlay, demand is **elastic** ($\vert E_d \vert > 1$).
> - If a price **fall** leads to a **fall** in Total Outlay, demand is **inelastic** ($\vert E_d \vert < 1$).
> - If a price **fall** leaves Total Outlay **unchanged**, demand is **unit elastic** ($\vert E_d \vert = 1$).
> The reverse logic applies when price rises.

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: Distinguish between point elasticity and arc elasticity of demand.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Basis** | **Point Elasticity** | **Arc Elasticity** |
|---|---|---|
| **Base used** | Initial price and quantity ($P_1, Q_1$) | Average of the two prices and quantities ($\bar{P}, \bar{Q}$) |
| **Symmetry** | Asymmetric (depends on direction of change) | Symmetric (direction-independent) |
| **Use case** | Elasticity at a *specific* point on the demand curve | Elasticity *between* two given points |
| **Formula** | $E_d = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{Q_1}$ | $E_d = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \dfrac{P_1 + P_2}{Q_1 + Q_2}$ |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q: (a)** Define Price Elasticity of Demand. Explain the **percentage method** and **geometric method** of measuring price elasticity of demand. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The price of a good falls from **₹40 to ₹36** per unit. As a result, the quantity demanded rises from **50 units to 80 units**. Calculate the price elasticity of demand using (i) the Percentage Method, and (ii) the Arc Method. Comment on the elasticity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Price Elasticity of Demand is the ratio of the percentage change in quantity demanded to the percentage change in price. It is given by:
> $$E_d = \frac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}$$
> It is essentially a pure number that measures the sensitivity of consumer demand to price changes.

> **Percentage Method (2.5 marks):** In this method, the elasticity is calculated as the ratio of two percentage changes. The original price and quantity are used as the base.
> - $\%\Delta Q = \dfrac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_1} \times 100$
> - $\%\Delta P = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1} \times 100$
> - $E_d = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P}$
> This method has the **drawback** that it gives different results depending on the direction of price change.

> **Geometric Method (2.5 marks):** Applicable to linear demand curves. Marshall showed that at any point on a linear demand curve:
> $$E_d = \frac{\text{Length of Lower Segment}}{\text{Length of Upper Segment}}$$
> This yields: (i) $E_d = \infty$ at the Y-axis intercept, (ii) $E_d = 1$ at the midpoint, (iii) $E_d = 0$ at the X-axis intercept. Above the midpoint the demand is elastic, below it is inelastic.

---

**Model Answer to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:** $P_1 = ₹40$, $P_2 = ₹36$, $Q_1 = 50$ units, $Q_2 = 80$ units.

**(i) Percentage Method (3.5 marks):**

$$\%\Delta P = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1} \times 100 = \frac{36 - 40}{40} \times 100 = \frac{-4}{40} \times 100 = -10\%$$

$$\%\Delta Q = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_1} \times 100 = \frac{80 - 50}{50} \times 100 = \frac{30}{50} \times 100 = +60\%$$

$$E_d^{point} = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P} = \frac{+60\%}{-10\%} = -6$$

$$\vert E_d^{point} \vert = 6 > 1 \implies \text{Demand is HIGHLY ELASTIC}$$

**(ii) Arc Method (3.5 marks):**

Step 1: Compute averages:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2} = \frac{40 + 36}{2} = ₹38$$

$$\bar{Q} = \frac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2} = \frac{50 + 80}{2} = 65 \text{ units}$$

Step 2: Apply the arc formula:

$$E_d^{arc} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \frac{\bar{P}}{\bar{Q}} = \frac{80 - 50}{36 - 40} \cdot \frac{38}{65}$$

$$E_d^{arc} = \frac{+30}{-4} \cdot \frac{38}{65} = -7.5 \times 0.5846 = -4.385$$

$$\vert E_d^{arc} \vert \approx 4.39 > 1 \implies \text{Demand is HIGHLY ELASTIC (confirmed)}$$

**Comment:** A 10% fall in price causes a 60% rise in quantity demanded. The product is highly price-sensitive — typical of luxury goods or non-essential items. The firm can profitably lower price to gain market share.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q: (a)** What is Price Elasticity of Demand? Explain its **importance and applications** in business decision-making and government policy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm's demand curve is given by $Q = 200 - 4P$. Find the price at which **Total Revenue is maximum**, and compute the corresponding elasticity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Price Elasticity of Demand is the ratio of percentage change in quantity demanded to the percentage change in price. $E_d = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P}$

> **Applications in Business Decisions (3 marks):**
> 1. **Pricing Decisions:** Firms use elasticity to decide whether to raise or lower prices. In elastic markets, lowering price raises total revenue; in inelastic markets, raising price raises revenue.
> 2. **Sales Forecasting:** Elasticity estimates help firms predict how sales volume will respond to promotional price changes.
> 3. **Product Differentiation:** A firm with a differentiated product (low substitutability) enjoys inelastic demand and can charge a premium.
> 4. **Price Discrimination:** Markets are segmented based on elasticity differences — high elasticity in poor markets, low elasticity in rich markets.
> 5. **Inventory Management:** Elasticity guides stocking levels for perishable and durable goods.

> **Applications in Government Policy (3 marks):**
> 1. **Taxation Policy:** The government taxes goods with **inelastic demand** (e.g., petrol, tobacco, alcohol) because consumption does not fall significantly, yielding stable tax revenue.
> 2. **Subsidy Decisions:** Subsidies are most effective on goods with elastic demand (e.g., food grains) because a small price cut induces large consumption gains.
> 3. **Foreign Trade:** Devaluation of currency improves trade balance only if demand for exports and imports is elastic — the **Marshall-Lerner condition**.
> 4. **Public Pricing:** Government pricing of utilities (electricity, water) considers elasticity to balance efficiency and equity.

---

**Model Answer to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $Q = 200 - 4P$, which means $P = \dfrac{200 - Q}{4} = 50 - 0.25Q$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Set up the Total Revenue function:

$$TR = P \cdot Q = (50 - 0.25Q) \cdot Q = 50Q - 0.25Q^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Maximize $TR$ by taking the first derivative and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = 50 - 0.5Q = 0$$

$$0.5Q = 50 \implies Q^* = 100 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Find the price at $Q^* = 100$:

$$P^* = 50 - 0.25(100) = 50 - 25 = ₹25$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Calculate the elasticity at $(Q^*=100, P^*=₹25)$:

The slope of the demand curve (in $P = 50 - 0.25Q$ form) is $\dfrac{dP}{dQ} = -0.25$.

$$E_d = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q} = \frac{1}{-0.25} \cdot \frac{25}{100} = -4 \times 0.25 = -1$$

$$\vert E_d \vert = 1 \implies \text{Unit Elastic at Revenue Maximum}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Verify maximum using the second derivative:

$$\frac{d^2(TR)}{dQ^2} = -0.5 < 0 \implies \text{Confirmed: TR is MAXIMIZED}$$

**Conclusion:** Total revenue is maximum at $P = ₹25$ and $Q = 100$ units, where the demand is **unit elastic** ($\vert E_d \vert = 1$). This is a fundamental KTU result: **Total revenue is maximized at the point of unit elasticity**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Students often forget to take the absolute value of $E_d$ and report a negative number as the final answer. Always state: $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ (unit elastic), not $E_d = -1$.
> 2. **Confusing slope with elasticity:** For a linear demand curve, the *slope* is constant, but the *elasticity* varies along the curve. Examiners frequently test this distinction. Do not equate $dQ/dP$ with $E_d$.
> 3. **Mixing percentage and arc methods:** Use arc method when the question says "between two prices" and percentage method only when the question explicitly says "at a given price."
> 4. **Missing the second derivative check:** In revenue-maximization problems, you must verify it is a maximum (not a minimum) by showing the second derivative is negative.
> 5. **Forgetting the demand function's domain:** The valid range of $P$ in this example is $0 \leq P \leq 50$, and the corresponding $Q$ is $0 \leq Q \leq 200$. The optimum must lie in this domain.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Demand Curve, Price Elasticity of Demand, Measurement of Price Elasticity and Its Applications":**
>
> ✅ **Demand Function:** $Q_d = f(P_x, P_r, P_c, Y, T, E, A, N, \ldots)$ — memorize all 8 variables and their meanings.
>
> ✅ **Law of Demand:** Inverse relationship between $P$ and $Q_d$, *ceteris paribus*. Demand curve slopes **downward** (negative slope).
>
> ✅ **Three Pillars of Downward Slope:** (1) Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility, (2) Income Effect, (3) Substitution Effect.
>
> ✅ **PED Definition:** $E_d = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}$ — a pure dimensionless number.
>
> ✅ **Sign Convention:** $E_d$ is **negative** (Law of Demand); classification uses the **absolute value** $\vert E_d \vert$.
>
> ✅ **Five Elasticity Zones:** $\vert E_d \vert = 0$ (perfectly inelastic), $0 < \vert E_d \vert < 1$ (inelastic), $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ (unit elastic), $1 < \vert E_d \vert < \infty$ (elastic), $\vert E_d \vert = \infty$ (perfectly elastic).
>
> ✅ **Three Methods of Measurement:**
> - **Percentage/Proportional Method** (asymmetric)
> - **Geometric Method** (linear curves: lower segment / upper segment)
> - **Total Outlay Method** (Marshall's non-mathematical heuristic)
> - **Arc Method** (symmetric, uses average $P$ and $Q$)
>
> ✅ **Midpoint Theorem:** At the midpoint of any linear demand curve, $\vert E_d \vert = 1$. Above midpoint: elastic. Below midpoint: inelastic.
>
> ✅ **Total Outlay Test (E-I-U Mnemonic):** Price falls → TR up = **E**lastic, TR down = **I**nelastic, TR unchanged = **U**nit elastic.
>
> ✅ **MR — Elasticity Linkage:** $MR = P\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\vert E_d \vert}\right)$. TR is maximum when $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ and $MR = 0$.
>
> ✅ **Top 5 Applications:** (1) Pricing decisions, (2) Government tax policy, (3) Foreign trade and Marshall-Lerner condition, (4) Price discrimination, (5) Demand forecasting and inventory management.
>
> ✅ **Common Pitfalls:** Sign convention errors, slope-vs-elasticity confusion, mismatching method to problem type, and forgetting the second-order condition in optimization problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supply: Factors Determining Supply, Law of Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Supply: Factors Determining Supply, Law of Supply

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Supply

In the context of **Economics for Engineers (UCHUT346)**, **Supply** refers to the quantity of a commodity or service that producers (sellers/manufacturers) are **willing and able to offer for sale** at various possible prices during a given period of time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Supply is the quantity of a good or service that a producer is willing and able to sell at a given price, per unit of time, ceteris paribus (other things being equal)."*

The two essential elements are:
1. **Willingness** to sell (intent).
2. **Ability** to sell (capacity, resources, technology).

The phrase *"per unit of time"* is critical — supply is a **flow concept** (per hour, per day, per month, per year), not a stock at a single instant.

### Individual Supply vs. Market Supply
- **Individual Supply:** Supply by a single producer/firm.
- **Market Supply:** Horizontal summation of supply schedules of **all individual producers** in the market at each price level.

$$\text{Market Supply} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_s^{i} \text{ at each price level}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lemonade Stand" Intuition

Imagine a child, **Anu**, running a lemonade stand in Kerala during summer. She can make 10 glasses per day because she has a fixed amount of lemons, sugar, ice, and time.

- At **₹10 per glass**: Anu sells 10 glasses, but she notices customers are eager. She could make more if she could charge more.
- At **₹30 per glass**: Anu wakes up early, buys extra lemons, hires her brother to help, and supplies 25 glasses.
- At **₹60 per glass**: Anu even borrows money to buy a second hand juicer — she supplies 40 glasses.

**Why?** As price rises, selling more becomes **more profitable**, so producers like Anu are motivated to bring more quantity to the market. This simple observation is the entire **Law of Supply** in action.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Keywords)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Quantity Supplied ($Q_s$)** | The actual amount offered at a specific price. |
| **Supply (Function)** | The entire relationship between price and quantity supplied. |
| **Supply Schedule** | A tabular representation of price-quantity pairs. |
| **Supply Curve** | A graphical representation of the supply schedule. |
| **Ceteris Paribus** | "All other factors held constant." |
| **Law of Supply** | The inverse-positive relationship between price and quantity supplied. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students often confuse **Supply** with **Stock**. **Stock** is the total quantity available at a point in time (e.g., 500 kg of rice in a godown). **Supply** is the portion of stock that producers are *willing* to sell at a given price during a *time period*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Law of Supply — Formal Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law of Supply (Alfred Marshall's Statement):**
> *"Other things being equal, the quantity supplied of a commodity is directly (positively) related to its price. As price rises, quantity supplied rises, and as price falls, quantity supplied falls."*

In plain terms: **Price ↑ ⇒ Supply ↑** and **Price ↓ ⇒ Supply ↓** (ceteris paribus).

### 2.1.1 Assumptions of the Law of Supply
The Law of Supply holds only when the following are **constant**:
1. Prices of related goods (substitutes, complements) in production.
2. Prices of **factors of production** (labour wages, raw material cost, rent, interest).
3. State of **technology** (production techniques unchanged).
4. **Government policies** (taxes, subsidies, regulations unchanged).
5. **Goals/objectives** of the producer (profit assumption holds).
6. **Expectations** of future prices (no speculative hoarding).
7. **Number of sellers** in the market.

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Representation of the Law

The most general form of the supply function is:

$$Q_s = f(P, P_r, P_f, T, G, N, E, \cdots)$$

Where:
- $Q_s$ = Quantity supplied
- $P$ = Price of the good itself
- $P_r$ = Prices of related goods
- $P_f$ = Prices of factors of production
- $T$ = Technology
- $G$ = Government policy
- $N$ = Number of sellers
- $E$ = Expectations

When all other variables (the *ceteris paribus* set) are held constant, the law reduces to the **bivariate supply function**:

$$Q_s = f(P)$$

The **linear form** (most commonly used in KTU numerical problems) is:

$$Q_s = a + bP$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule:** The coefficient **$b > 0$** is mandatory. If $b < 0$, it violates the Law of Supply and represents a *non-conforming* or *exceptional* case (e.g., backward-bending labour supply, auction panic sales, perishables).

### 2.1.3 Supply Schedule — A Numerical Example

| Price per unit (₹) | Quantity Supplied (Units) |
|---:|---:|
| 10 | 50 |
| 20 | 70 |
| 30 | 90 |
| 40 | 110 |
| 50 | 130 |
| 60 | 150 |

**Observation:** Each ₹10 rise in price increases quantity supplied by 20 units. The relationship is **positive and linear** — the canonical Law of Supply behavior.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Supply Function | $Q_s = a + bP$ | $b > 0$ for Law of Supply |
| 2 | Inverse Supply Function | $P = \alpha + \beta Q_s$ | $\beta > 0$ |
| 3 | Slope of Supply Curve | $\dfrac{\Delta Q_s}{\Delta P} = b$ | Positive value |
| 4 | Market Supply | $Q_s^{M} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_s^{i}$ | Horizontal summation |
| 5 | Change in Supply (Shift) | $Q_s = a + bP + cX$ | $X$ = other factor |
| 6 | Elasticity of Supply | $E_s = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}$ | Always non-negative (signed) |
| 7 | Price Elasticity (Point) | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{Q_s} \cdot \dfrac{dQ_s}{dP}$ | Unitless |
| 8 | Exceptions | $b < 0$ (rare) | Perishables, speculation, rare cases |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use `|` (vertical bar) in the KTU formula table — it breaks markdown rendering.** Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX, or write "absolute value of" in plain text.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility for Engineers

The Law of Supply is foundational to:
- **Production Planning & Inventory Management:** Firms forecast output based on expected selling prices.
- **Pricing Strategy:** Engineers in manufacturing estimate break-even volumes at various price points.
- **Industrial Economics:** Predicting how producers of cement, steel, semiconductors respond to price signals.
- **Project Feasibility:** Higher expected price ⇒ higher supply committed ⇒ engineering capacity expansion decisions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Supply Equation (Two-Point Method)

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark numerical** in KTU exams. The methodology is below.

### Problem Setup
Given two price-quantity observations from a supply schedule, derive the supply equation. Use the table:

| Point | Price (₹) | Quantity Supplied (Units) |
|---:|---:|---:|
| A | 20 | 100 |
| B | 50 | 250 |

### Step 1 — Recall the General Linear Form

$$Q_s = a + bP$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Two Points

Substituting Point A: $(P, Q_s) = (20, 100)$

$$100 = a + b(20)$$

Substituting Point B: $(P, Q_s) = (50, 250)$

$$250 = a + b(50)$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the Two Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
250 - 100 &= \big[a + 50b\big] - \big[a + 20b\big] \\
150 &= 30b \\
b &= \frac{150}{30} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back to Find $a$

$$
\begin{aligned}
100 &= a + 5(20) \\
100 &= a + 100 \\
a &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Write the Final Supply Equation

$$\boxed{Q_s = 0 + 5P \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_s = 5P}$$

### Step 6 — Verify the Law of Supply is Satisfied

Here $b = 5 > 0$. ✓ The Law of Supply holds.
At $P = 20$: $Q_s = 5 \times 20 = 100$ ✓
At $P = 50$: $Q_s = 5 \times 50 = 250$ ✓

## 3.2 Supply Curve Slope & Elasticity Derivation

Given the supply equation $Q_s = 5P$, derive the slope and elasticity.

### Slope of the Supply Curve

The slope is the derivative $\frac{dQ_s}{dP}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= 5P \\
\frac{dQ_s}{dP} &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A ₹1 increase in price increases quantity supplied by 5 units.

### Point Elasticity of Supply

Using the formula $E_s = \dfrac{P}{Q_s} \cdot \dfrac{dQ_s}{dP}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \frac{P}{Q_s} \cdot 5 \\
&= \frac{P}{5P} \cdot 5 \\
&= \frac{5P}{5P} \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For any linear supply function of the form $Q_s = bP$ (i.e., passing through the origin), elasticity of supply is identically equal to 1 (unitary elasticity).** This is a KTU favorite quick-answer question.

## 3.3 Derivation of Market Supply (Horizontal Summation)

Suppose two firms in a market have the following supply equations:

- Firm 1: $Q_{s1} = 10 + 2P$
- Firm 2: $Q_{s2} = 5 + 3P$

### Step 1 — Set Up the Summation

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s^{M} &= Q_{s1} + Q_{s2} \\
&= (10 + 2P) + (5 + 3P)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Combine Like Terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s^{M} &= (10 + 5) + (2P + 3P) \\
&= 15 + 5P
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Validate the Law

$b = 5 > 0$ ✓ Market supply obeys the Law of Supply.

### Verification at a Sample Price $P = 10$

- $Q_{s1} = 10 + 2(10) = 30$
- $Q_{s2} = 5 + 3(10) = 35$
- $Q_s^{M} = 30 + 35 = 65$

From formula: $Q_s^{M} = 15 + 5(10) = 65$ ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Does the Supply Curve Shift vs. Move?

A **movement along the curve** is caused only by a change in the **own price** $P$. A **shift of the curve** is caused by changes in *ceteris paribus* factors.

| Factor | Direction of Shift | Economic Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| Rise in factor (input) prices | **Leftward** (decrease) | Production becomes costlier → less supplied. |
| Improvement in technology | **Rightward** (increase) | Productivity rises → more supplied. |
| Increase in number of sellers | **Rightward** (increase) | More producers → more total supply. |
| Imposition of a tax | **Leftward** (decrease) | Net price received falls → less supplied. |
| Government subsidy | **Rightward** (increase) | Cost subsidized → more supplied. |
| Rise in price of a substitute-in-production | **Rightward** (increase) | Resources shift toward the higher-priced good. |
| Expectation of future price rise | **Current supply falls (leftward)** | Producers hoard for later. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** The most common valuation error is confusing a *movement along* the curve with a *shift* of the curve. Only $P$ (own price) causes movement. All other factors cause shifts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Supply Function

The following Mermaid diagram maps how various determinants feed into the producer's supply decision, producing the resulting supply curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Producer Decision Engine]:::core
    P1[Own Price P]:::driver
    P2[Factor Prices Pf]:::driver
    P3[Technology Level T]:::driver
    P4[Number of Sellers N]:::driver
    P5[Government Policy G]:::driver
    P6[Expectations E]:::driver
    P7[Related Goods Prices Pr]:::driver
    OUT1[Quantity Supplied Qs]:::output
    SC[Supply Curve SS]:::output

    P1 --> A0
    P2 --> A0
    P3 --> A0
    P4 --> A0
    P5 --> A0
    P6 --> A0
    P7 --> A0
    A0 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> SC

    classDef core fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef driver fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**How to read this:** Every box on the left is a *ceteris paribus* determinant feeding into the central decision block. Only **Own Price P** causes a *movement along* the existing supply curve. All other boxes cause a *shift* of the entire supply curve SS to a new position.

## 4.2 Supply Curve with Movement vs. Shift (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisX((P axis horizontal)):::axis
    axisY((Q axis vertical)):::axis

    P1[Price rises P1 to P2]:::event
    MOVE[Movement along curve: P1 to P2, Q rises]:::m
    CURVE[Upward sloping supply curve S1]:::curve
    SHIFT[Curve shifts right: S1 to S2, at same price higher Q]:::s

    axisX --> CURVE
    axisY --> CURVE
    P1 --> MOVE
    CURVE --> MOVE
    CURVE --> SHIFT

    classDef axis fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef event fill:#FFF3B0,stroke:#806000,color:#000000
    classDef m fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef curve fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef s fill:#A0D0FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- **Movement** (yellow→red box): Caused by own-price change; we slide *along* the original curve $S_1$.
- **Shift** (green→blue box): Caused by non-price factors; the entire curve moves to a new position $S_2$.

## 4.3 Ceteris Paribus Set — Visual Bubble Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CP[Ceteris Paribus Set: All factors held constant]:::hub
    F1[Factor Prices Pf]:::node
    F2[Technology T]:::node
    F3[Number of Sellers N]:::node
    F4[Government Policy G]:::node
    F5[Expectations E]:::node
    F6[Related Goods Prices Pr]:::node
    F7[Producer Goals]:::node
    LAW[Law of Supply holds]:::law

    CP --> F1
    CP --> F2
    CP --> F3
    CP --> F4
    CP --> F5
    CP --> F6
    CP --> F7
    F1 --> LAW
    F2 --> LAW
    F3 --> LAW
    F4 --> LAW
    F5 --> LAW
    F6 --> LAW
    F7 --> LAW

    classDef hub fill:#FFEB99,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef node fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef law fill:#C8E6C8,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-to-Concept Mapping:** The seven leaf nodes ($F_1$ to $F_7$) are the precise list of *ceteris paribus* conditions KTU examiners expect you to enumerate. If you name any one of these in the exam, you score full marks on the "assumptions" sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define Supply. Distinguish between Stock and Supply.

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

**Supply** is the quantity of a commodity that producers are willing and able to sell at a given price, per unit of time, *ceteris paribus*.

**Distinction:**

| Basis | Stock | Supply |
|---|---|---|
| Concept | Static (point of time) | Flow (over a period) |
| Determined by | Production + Past accumulation | Current price & willingness |
| Example | 10,000 tonnes of wheat in a FCI godown | 1,500 tonnes offered at ₹25/kg this month |

*(Examiner's note: A clean 3-line tabular distinction with one example = full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** State the Law of Supply. Why is the assumption *ceteris paribus* essential?

**Model Answer:**

**Law of Supply:** *"Other things being equal, an increase in price leads to an increase in quantity supplied, and a decrease in price leads to a decrease in quantity supplied."*

**Why *ceteris paribus* is essential:** The Law establishes a relationship between *only* two variables — own price ($P$) and quantity supplied ($Q_s$). For this isolated relationship to be observable and measurable, all other influencing factors (input costs, technology, policy, etc.) must be held constant. Without this assumption, simultaneous changes in other variables would mask or distort the true price-quantity relationship, making the Law untestable.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for the statement, 1 mark for explaining the role of ceteris paribus.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

(a) Explain the **Law of Supply** with its assumptions. State its exceptions. *(7 marks)*
(b) The supply schedule of a commodity is given below. Derive the supply equation and calculate the elasticity of supply at $P = ₹30$. *(7 marks)*

| Price (₹) | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Quantity Supplied | 100 | 200 | 300 | 400 | 500 |

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Law of Supply, Assumptions, Exceptions *(7 marks)*

**1. Statement of the Law (2 marks):**
*"Other things being equal, the quantity supplied of a commodity is directly (positively) related to its price."*

$$Q_s = f(P), \quad \text{with} \quad \frac{\partial Q_s}{\partial P} > 0$$

**2. Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. No change in prices of factors of production.
2. No change in technology.
3. No change in government policy (taxes/subsidies).
4. No change in the number of sellers.
5. No change in producer expectations about future prices.
6. No change in prices of related goods.

**3. Exceptions (2 marks):**
1. **Perishable goods:** Sellers may dump stock at low prices to avoid loss.
2. **Agricultural goods** in the short run (supply inelastic in immediate post-harvest period).
3. **Speculative/anticipatory markets:** Sellers may withhold supply expecting higher future prices.
4. **Backward-bending labour supply curve** beyond a certain wage level.
5. **Auction/panic sales** in distress situations.

---

#### Part (b) — Derive Supply Equation & Elasticity at P = ₹30 *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Identify the Functional Form**

Assume a linear supply function: $Q_s = a + bP$

**Step 2 — Pick Two Points from the Schedule**

Use $(P_1, Q_{s1}) = (10, 100)$ and $(P_2, Q_{s2}) = (50, 500)$.

**Step 3 — Solve the System**

$$
\begin{aligned}
100 &= a + 10b \quad \text{...(i)} \\
500 &= a + 50b \quad \text{...(ii)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting (i) from (ii):

$$
\begin{aligned}
500 - 100 &= (a + 50b) - (a + 10b) \\
400 &= 40b \\
b &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute back into (i):

$$
\begin{aligned}
100 &= a + 10(10) \\
100 &= a + 100 \\
a &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Supply Equation**

$$\boxed{Q_s = 10P}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Elasticity at P = ₹30**

First, find $Q_s$ at $P = 30$:

$$Q_s = 10 \times 30 = 300$$

Compute $\frac{dQ_s}{dP} = 10$.

Apply the point elasticity formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \frac{P}{Q_s} \cdot \frac{dQ_s}{dP} \\
&= \frac{30}{300} \cdot 10 \\
&= 0.1 \times 10 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{E_s = 1 \text{ (Unit Elastic)}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part A):** Common mistakes costing 1–2 marks:
> - Forgetting the **ceteris paribus** clause in the Law statement (-1 mark).
> - Listing "law of demand" exceptions as supply exceptions (-1 mark).
> - Using the **inverse** supply formula when the question requires $Q_s = f(P)$ (-1 mark).
> - Computing elasticity using the **arc** formula when the question explicitly asks for **point** elasticity at $P=30$ (-1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

(a) Enumerate and explain the **factors determining supply**. Distinguish between a *movement along* and a *shift of* the supply curve with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*
(b) Two firms produce a homogeneous good with supply equations: $Q_{s1} = 20 + 2P$ and $Q_{s2} = 10 + 3P$. Derive the market supply equation and verify the Law of Supply. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Factors Determining Supply + Movement vs. Shift *(7 marks)*

**1. Factors Determining Supply (4 marks):**

1. **Price of the good itself** ($P$) — The most direct determinant.
2. **Prices of factors of production** ($P_f$) — Higher input cost reduces profit margin and supply.
3. **State of technology** ($T$) — Better technology → lower unit cost → higher supply.
4. **Prices of related goods** ($P_r$) — Goods that share the same production resources.
5. **Government policy** ($G$) — Taxes reduce supply; subsidies raise supply.
6. **Number of sellers** ($N$) — More sellers → higher market supply.
7. **Producer expectations** ($E$) — Expected future price rise → current supply falls.

**2. Movement vs. Shift (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Movement Along the Curve | Shift of the Curve |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Change in **own price** | Change in **other factors** |
| Effect | Slide from one point to another on the *same* curve | Entire curve moves to a new position |
| Example | A rise in petrol price leads to higher quantity supplied by an oil refinery. | A cut in excise duty shifts the supply curve rightward — at every price, more is supplied. |

**Example for Movement:** If a smartphone's price rises from ₹15,000 to ₹20,000, the firm increases output from 1,000 to 1,400 units — a *movement upward along* the same supply curve.

**Example for Shift:** The government announces a 10% production-linked subsidy for semiconductor firms. At every price, firms can now supply more — the entire supply curve shifts rightward.

---

#### Part (b) — Market Supply Derivation *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Identify Individual Supply Equations**

- Firm 1: $Q_{s1} = 20 + 2P$
- Firm 2: $Q_{s2} = 10 + 3P$

**Step 2 — Apply Horizontal Summation**

Market supply is the sum of individual supplies at every price:

$$Q_s^{M} = Q_{s1} + Q_{s2}$$

**Step 3 — Combine Like Terms**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s^{M} &= (20 + 2P) + (10 + 3P) \\
&= (20 + 10) + (2P + 3P) \\
&= 30 + 5P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Market Supply Equation**

$$\boxed{Q_s^{M} = 30 + 5P}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the Law of Supply**

The coefficient of $P$ is $b = 5 > 0$. Therefore, as $P$ increases, $Q_s^{M}$ increases — **the Law of Supply holds at the market level.** ✓

**Step 6 — Numerical Check at $P = 20$**

- $Q_{s1} = 20 + 2(20) = 60$ units
- $Q_{s2} = 10 + 3(20) = 70$ units
- $Q_s^{M} = 60 + 70 = 130$ units

From the derived formula:

$$Q_s^{M} = 30 + 5(20) = 30 + 100 = 130 \text{ units} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):** Common pitfalls:
> - Conflating **firm-level** and **market-level** supply when writing the equation (-1 mark).
> - Failing to **explicitly state** $b > 0$ as the verification of the Law of Supply (-1 mark).
> - Using **vertical** summation instead of horizontal summation when both firms produce the same homogeneous good (-2 marks).
> - Mixing up *movement along* with *shift* in the Movement vs. Shift sub-part (-1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- **Definition:** Supply = willingness + ability, per unit of time, *ceteris paribus*.
- **Stock ≠ Supply:** Stock is static; supply is a flow over time.
- **Law of Supply:** $\frac{\partial Q_s}{\partial P} > 0$ — direct/positive relationship.
- **Linear form:** $Q_s = a + bP$ with $b > 0$.
- **Ceteris Paribus set** (must memorize): factor prices, technology, number of sellers, government policy, expectations, related goods' prices, producer goals.
- **Movement vs. Shift:** Only own price causes movement; all else causes shift.
- **Market supply** is the **horizontal** sum of individual supplies.
- **Exceptions** to the Law: perishables, agriculture (short run), speculation, backward-bending labour supply, distress sales.
- **Elasticity of $Q_s = bP$ is identically 1** (passes through origin).
- **Inverse supply function:** $P = \alpha + \beta Q_s$, with $\beta > 0$.
- **Two-point derivation method** is the standard KTU numerical technique — practice it until automatic.
- **Always end numerical answers** with a verification step (plug a value back, confirm Law holds with $b > 0$).
- **Examiner's mantra:** A diagram or schedule in your answer fetches 1–2 *extra* marks even if the algebra is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supply Curve, Equilibrium Price Determination, Changes in Demand and Supply and Its Effects on Equilibrium Price and Quantity, Production: Production Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Supply Curve, Equilibrium Price Determination, Changes in Demand and Supply and Its Effects on Equilibrium Price and Quantity, Production: Production Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Supply Curve — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supply (KTU 2024 Definition):** Supply refers to the quantity of a good or service that producers are **willing and able** to offer for sale at various possible prices during a given period of time, *other things being equal (ceteris paribus)*.

The **Supply Curve** is the graphical representation of the law of supply — it depicts the **functional relationship** between the price of a commodity (independent variable, plotted on the Y-axis) and the quantity supplied (dependent variable, plotted on the X-axis), holding all other determinants constant.

- **Law of Supply (with Ceteris Paribus):** *As the price of a good rises, the quantity supplied of that good also rises, and as the price falls, the quantity supplied falls, other factors remaining constant.*
- The supply curve generally **slopes upward from left to right**, indicating a **positive (direct) relationship** between price and quantity supplied.
- The slope of the supply curve is mathematically expressed as $\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta Q_s}$ — a **positive value**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:** Quantity supplied is a *point* on the supply curve, whereas **Supply** refers to the *entire schedule/curve*. Students often lose marks by interchanging these terms.

#### Intuitive Analogy: The Mango Seller

Imagine a small-town mango seller named *Ravi*. At ₹20 per kg, he brings **10 kg** of mangoes to the market. If suddenly the price jumps to ₹80 per kg (perhaps a festival or scarcity), what does Ravi do? He rushes home, picks more mangoes from his trees, maybe even borrows from neighbours — and brings **30 kg** to the market. Why? Because **higher price = higher profit incentive = more willingness to supply**.

This is the essence of the supply curve: **producers respond to price signals by adjusting output**.

### 1.2 Market Equilibrium Price — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Market Equilibrium:** Market equilibrium is a state of **rest** or **balance** where the **quantity demanded** by consumers exactly equals the **quantity supplied** by producers at a specific price.

- The price at which equilibrium is achieved is called the **Equilibrium Price** (also termed *Market Clearing Price* or *Settlement Price*).
- The corresponding quantity bought and sold is the **Equilibrium Quantity**.
- Graphically, equilibrium is the **intersection point** of the **Demand Curve** (downward-sloping) and the **Supply Curve** (upward-sloping).

#### Intuitive Analogy: The College Canteen Lunch

Suppose a college canteen has a "chapati meal" priced at ₹40. At ₹40, students *demand* 200 meals but the canteen *supplies* only 120 meals — a **shortage** of 80 meals. The canteen owner notices students waiting, raises the price to ₹60. At ₹60, demand drops to 150 meals, but supply rises to 150 meals — **equilibrium is reached!** No shortage, no surplus. The canteen is "clearing" the market.

### 1.3 Changes in Demand and Supply — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> - **Change in Quantity Supplied/Demanded:** A movement *along* the same curve caused by a change in the good's own price.
> - **Change in Supply/Demand (Shift):** A shift of the *entire curve* caused by a change in *non-price determinants* (income, tastes, input costs, technology, taxes, number of sellers, etc.).

### 1.4 Production Function — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production Function (KTU 2024 Definition):** A production function expresses the **technical relationship** between physical inputs (factors of production) and physical output of a firm during a given period.

Symbolically expressed as:
$$Q_x = f(A, B, C, \ldots)$$

Where:
- $Q_x$ = Quantity of output $X$ produced
- $A, B, C, \ldots$ = Quantities of inputs (land, labour, capital, entrepreneurship, raw materials)

It tells us the **maximum output** that can be produced from a given combination of inputs, given the **state of technology**.

#### Intuitive Analogy: The Idli-Making Machine

Consider a small hotel producing idlis. The **inputs** are rice batter, water, salt, the idli steamer, and the cook's effort. The **output** is the number of idlis. If you double the batter (and proportional steamer capacity), you double the idlis — this is the production function in action. The function tells the hotel owner: *"For every 1 kg of batter, with the given steamer and cook, you will get exactly 40 idlis."*

### 1.5 Key Determinants and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Assumptions (Ceteris Paribus Conditions):**
> - **Technology:** Held constant (unless explicitly shifted)
> - **Input Prices:** Constant for supply analysis
> - **Consumer Income & Tastes:** Constant for demand analysis
> - **Time Period:** Short run vs. Long run distinction is **critical** for KTU board valuation.
> - **Number of Buyers/Sellers:** Constant unless industry entry/exit is specified.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Law of Supply — Step-by-Step Logic

The supply curve is derived from the producer's profit-maximizing behaviour. Let us break it down:

1. **Step 1 — Profit Motive:** Producers are rational economic agents who aim to maximize profits.
2. **Step 2 — Price as a Signal:** A higher market price translates to higher potential revenue per unit sold.
3. **Step 3 — Marginal Cost Principle:** Firms will produce additional units as long as the price (which approximates marginal revenue in perfect competition) is greater than or equal to the **Marginal Cost (MC)** of producing the next unit.
4. **Step 4 — Upward Slope:** As price rises, more units whose MC was previously higher than price become profitable to produce, expanding the quantity supplied.
5. **Step 5 — Ceteris Paribus:** All non-price determinants (input costs, technology, taxes, number of sellers) are held constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the supply curve slope upward?** Because of the **Law of Diminishing Marginal Returns** — to produce more output, a firm must employ less efficient resources, raising the marginal cost, which requires a higher price to induce production.

### 2.2 The Law of Demand (Recap for Equilibrium Analysis)

For equilibrium, demand is essential. The demand curve slopes **downward** because:
- **Substitution Effect:** As price rises, consumers switch to cheaper substitutes.
- **Income Effect:** As price rises, real purchasing power falls, reducing consumption.
- **Diminishing Marginal Utility:** Each additional unit consumed yields less satisfaction.

### 2.3 Market Equilibrium — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Step 1 — Define Demand and Supply Equations:** Express both as functions of price.
2. **Step 2 — Set $Q_d = Q_s$:** At equilibrium, market forces must clear.
3. **Step 3 — Solve for Equilibrium Price ($P^*$):** Algebraically obtain the price.
4. **Step 4 — Substitute Back:** Plug $P^*$ into either equation to obtain equilibrium quantity ($Q^*$).
5. **Step 5 — Verification:** Check that at $P^*$, no shortage ($Q_d > Q_s$) or surplus ($Q_d < Q_s$) exists.

### 2.4 Effects of Changes in Demand and Supply on Equilibrium

**Case 1: Increase in Demand (Demand curve shifts right, $D \rightarrow D'$)**
- $P^*$ **rises** ↑
- $Q^*$ **rises** ↑

**Case 2: Decrease in Demand (Demand curve shifts left, $D \rightarrow D''$)**
- $P^*$ **falls** ↓
- $Q^*$ **falls** ↓

**Case 3: Increase in Supply (Supply curve shifts right, $S \rightarrow S'$)**
- $P^*$ **falls** ↓
- $Q^*$ **rises** ↑

**Case 4: Decrease in Supply (Supply curve shifts left, $S \rightarrow S''$)**
- $P^*$ **rises** ↑
- $Q^*$ **falls** ↓

**Case 5: Simultaneous Increase in Demand and Supply**
- $P^*$ — *Ambiguous* (depends on relative magnitudes)
- $Q^*$ — **definitely rises** ↑

**Case 6: Simultaneous Decrease in Demand and Supply**
- $P^*$ — *Ambiguous*
- $Q^*$ — **definitely falls** ↓

**Case 7: Increase in Demand + Decrease in Supply**
- $P^*$ — **definitely rises** ↑↑
- $Q^*$ — *Ambiguous*

**Case 8: Decrease in Demand + Increase in Supply**
- $P^*$ — **definitely falls** ↓↓
- $Q^*$ — *Ambiguous*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Trap:** Students must distinguish between a **change in quantity demanded** (movement along the demand curve due to price change) and a **change in demand** (shift of the entire demand curve). Confusing these is a **3-mark penalty** in board exams.

### 2.5 Types of Production Functions

**A. Short-Run Production Function:**
- At least one input is **fixed** (typically capital).
- $Q = f(L, \bar{K})$ — Output depends on variable labour $(L)$ with fixed capital $(\bar{K})$.

**B. Long-Run Production Function:**
- **All inputs are variable.**
- $Q = f(L, K)$ — Output depends on both labour and capital, with freedom to change.

**C. Cobb-Douglas Production Function (KTU High-Yield):**
$$Q = A \cdot L^{\alpha} \cdot K^{\beta}$$
Where:
- $A$ = Total Factor Productivity (TFP)
- $L$ = Labour input
- $K$ = Capital input
- $\alpha$ = Output elasticity of labour (typically $0 < \alpha < 1$)
- $\beta$ = Output elasticity of capital (typically $0 < \beta < 1$)
- $\alpha + \beta$ = Returns to scale parameter (1 = constant, >1 = increasing, <1 = decreasing)

**D. Linear Production Function:**
$$Q = aL + bK$$
Perfectly substitutable inputs.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Demand | $Q_d = a - bP$ | $a > 0$, $b > 0$; slope = $-b$ |
| Linear Supply | $Q_s = -c + dP$ | $d > 0$, $c \geq 0$; slope = $d$ |
| Equilibrium Condition | $Q_d = Q_s$ | Set and solve for $P^*$ |
| Equilibrium Price | $P^* = \frac{a + c}{b + d}$ | Derived by algebraic substitution |
| Equilibrium Quantity | $Q^* = \frac{ad - bc}{b + d}$ | Substitute $P^*$ into $Q_d$ or $Q_s$ |
| Price Elasticity of Supply | $E_s = \frac{\%\Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}$ | Always non-negative for normal goods |
| Point Elasticity of Supply | $E_s = \frac{dQ_s}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}$ | Use calculus-based definition |
| Cobb-Douglas Production | $Q = A \cdot L^{\alpha} \cdot K^{\beta}$ | $\alpha + \beta = 1$ implies constant returns to scale |
| Returns to Scale | $\lambda Q = f(\lambda L, \lambda K)$ | $\lambda = $ scaling factor |
| Total Product (TP) | $TP = Q$ | Total output from all inputs |
| Average Product (AP) | $AP_L = \frac{TP}{L}$ | Output per unit of labour |
| Marginal Product (MP) | $MP_L = \frac{\Delta TP}{\Delta L}$ or $\frac{dQ}{dL}$ | Additional output from one more labour unit |
| Slope of Supply Curve | $\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta Q_s}$ | Positive value |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** In all equation derivations below, the variables follow standard economic convention: $a, b, c, d$ are constants, with $a, b, d > 0$ and $c \geq 0$. Equilibrium always requires $Q_d = Q_s$.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Production Engineers** use production functions to estimate output capacity when scaling up manufacturing lines.
- **Cost Analysts** in firms use supply curve concepts to predict the impact of tax changes on industry-wide output.
- **Market Analysts** use equilibrium models to forecast commodity prices (steel, oil, semiconductors) used in engineering projects.
- **Operations Managers** apply Cobb-Douglas to optimize the labour-capital mix in factories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Price and Quantity from Linear Demand and Supply

**Given:**
- Demand function: $Q_d = a - bP$
- Supply function: $Q_s = -c + dP$

**Step 1 — Apply the Equilibrium Condition:**

At market equilibrium, the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied. This is the fundamental condition of market clearing.

$$Q_d = Q_s$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Two Functions into the Equilibrium Condition:**

Replace $Q_d$ and $Q_s$ with their respective functional forms.

$$a - bP = -c + dP$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the Price Terms on One Side:**

Move all $P$ terms to the right-hand side and constants to the left-hand side.

$$a + c = dP + bP$$

**Step 4 — Factor Out $P$ on the Right-Hand Side:**

Apply distributive factoring to combine the two price terms.

$$a + c = P(d + b)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Equilibrium Price ($P^*$):**

Divide both sides by $(b + d)$ to isolate $P$. Note that $b + d > 0$ since both demand and supply slopes are non-zero.

$$P^* = \frac{a + c}{b + d}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $P^*$ Back into the Demand Function:**

Now use the demand equation to find the equilibrium quantity. The demand function is preferred because it is direct.

$$Q^* = a - b \cdot P^*$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the Value of $P^*$:**

$$Q^* = a - b \cdot \left(\frac{a + c}{b + d}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Find a Common Denominator and Simplify:**

Express $a$ with denominator $(b + d)$ to combine the terms.

$$Q^* = \frac{a(b + d) - b(a + c)}{b + d}$$

**Step 9 — Expand the Numerator:**

$$Q^* = \frac{ab + ad - ab - bc}{b + d}$$

**Step 10 — Cancel the $ab$ Terms:**

The $ab$ terms cancel, leaving a clean expression.

$$Q^* = \frac{ad - bc}{b + d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> - **Equilibrium Price:** $P^* = \frac{a + c}{b + d}$
> - **Equilibrium Quantity:** $Q^* = \frac{ad - bc}{b + d}$

**Step 11 — Verification of Equilibria Condition:**

To verify, substitute back into supply function:

$$Q_s = -c + d \cdot P^* = -c + d \cdot \frac{a+c}{b+d} = \frac{-c(b+d) + d(a+c)}{b+d} = \frac{-bc - cd + ad + cd}{b+d} = \frac{ad - bc}{b+d}$$

This matches $Q^*$, confirming our derivation. ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Numerical Application

**Problem:** Given $Q_d = 100 - 4P$ and $Q_s = -20 + 6P$, find equilibrium price and quantity.

**Step 1 — Apply equilibrium condition:**

$$100 - 4P = -20 + 6P$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $P$:**

$$100 + 20 = 6P + 4P$$

$$120 = 10P$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P^*$:**

$$P^* = \frac{120}{10} = 12$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into demand function to find $Q^*$:**

$$Q^* = 100 - 4(12) = 100 - 48 = 52$$

**Step 5 — Verify using supply function:**

$$Q_s = -20 + 6(12) = -20 + 72 = 52 \checkmark$$

**Answer:** $P^* = 12$ units of currency, $Q^* = 52$ units of output.

---

### 3.3 Effect of a Shift in Demand — Worked Example

**Problem:** Suppose due to a rise in consumer income, demand becomes $Q_d' = 150 - 4P$ (original demand was $Q_d = 100 - 4P$). Supply remains $Q_s = -20 + 6P$. Find the new equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Apply new equilibrium condition:**

$$150 - 4P = -20 + 6P$$

**Step 2 — Solve for new $P^{*\prime}$:**

$$170 = 10P \implies P^{*\prime} = 17$$

**Step 3 — Find new $Q^{*\prime}$:**

$$Q^{*\prime} = 150 - 4(17) = 150 - 68 = 82$$

**Step 4 — Compare with old equilibrium:**

$$\Delta P = 17 - 12 = +5 \quad (\text{Price rose})$$

$$\Delta Q = 82 - 52 = +30 \quad (\text{Quantity rose})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** An **increase in demand** (rightward shift) leads to a **higher equilibrium price** and a **higher equilibrium quantity** — confirming Case 1 in Section 2.4.

---

### 3.4 Effect of a Shift in Supply — Worked Example

**Problem:** Suppose due to a fall in input costs, supply becomes $Q_s' = -50 + 6P$ (original $Q_s = -20 + 6P$). Demand remains $Q_d = 100 - 4P$.

**Step 1 — New equilibrium condition:**

$$100 - 4P = -50 + 6P$$

**Step 2 — Solve:**

$$150 = 10P \implies P^{*\prime} = 15$$

Wait — this is *higher* than original $P^* = 12$. Let us re-examine: the new supply is $Q_s = -50 + 6P$, meaning at $P = 0$, suppliers would *pay* to avoid selling 50 units (intercepts are economic curiosities). The shift here actually represents a *decrease* in supply at lower prices but a *change in intercept*.

Let us reconsider with a standard rightward shift: $Q_s' = +20 + 6P$ (c = -20, so supply increased).

$$100 - 4P = 20 + 6P \implies 80 = 10P \implies P^{*\prime} = 8$$

$$Q^{*\prime} = 100 - 4(8) = 68$$

**Comparison:** $\Delta P = 8 - 12 = -4$ (Price fell) ✓; $\Delta Q = 68 - 52 = +16$ (Quantity rose) ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** An **increase in supply** (rightward shift) leads to a **lower equilibrium price** and a **higher equilibrium quantity** — confirming Case 3 in Section 2.4.

---

### 3.5 Cobb-Douglas Production Function — Returns to Scale Analysis

**Given:** $Q = A \cdot L^{\alpha} \cdot K^{\beta}$ with $A = 2$, $\alpha = 0.6$, $\beta = 0.4$.

**Initial Inputs:** $L = 100$ units, $K = 50$ units.

**Step 1 — Calculate Initial Output $Q_1$:**

$$Q_1 = 2 \cdot (100)^{0.6} \cdot (50)^{0.4}$$

$$Q_1 = 2 \cdot 15.85 \cdot 4.78 \approx 151.5 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Scale Inputs by $\lambda = 2$:**

New inputs: $L' = 200$, $K' = 100$.

**Step 3 — Calculate New Output $Q_2$:**

$$Q_2 = 2 \cdot (200)^{0.6} \cdot (100)^{0.4}$$

$$Q_2 = 2 \cdot 24.78 \cdot 6.31 \approx 312.7 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Check Returns to Scale:**

$$Q_2 / Q_1 = 312.7 / 151.5 \approx 2.06 \approx \lambda^{0.6+0.4} = 2^1 = 2$$

Since $\alpha + \beta = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1$, the production function exhibits **Constant Returns to Scale (CRS)**. A doubling of inputs leads to a doubling of output. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Returns to Scale Rule for Cobb-Douglas:**
> - If $\alpha + \beta > 1$: **Increasing Returns to Scale (IRS)**
> - If $\alpha + \beta = 1$: **Constant Returns to Scale (CRS)**
> - If $\alpha + \beta < 1$: **Decreasing Returns to Scale (DRS)**

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Change in Equilibrium Summary

| Shift Scenario | Demand Curve | Supply Curve | $\Delta P^*$ | $\Delta Q^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand ↑ (right shift) | Rightward | No change | ↑ Rises | ↑ Rises |
| Demand ↓ (left shift) | Leftward | No change | ↓ Falls | ↓ Falls |
| Supply ↑ (right shift) | No change | Rightward | ↓ Falls | ↑ Rises |
| Supply ↓ (left shift) | No change | Leftward | ↑ Rises | ↓ Falls |
| Demand ↑ + Supply ↑ | Rightward | Rightward | Ambiguous | ↑ Rises |
| Demand ↓ + Supply ↓ | Leftward | Leftward | Ambiguous | ↓ Falls |
| Demand ↑ + Supply ↓ | Rightward | Leftward | ↑↑ Strongly Rises | Ambiguous |
| Demand ↓ + Supply ↑ | Leftward | Rightward | ↓↓ Strongly Falls | Ambiguous |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: Market Equilibrium — Basic Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Price Axis P"] --> nodeB["Supply Curve S Slopes Upward"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Demand Curve D Slopes Downward"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Equilibrium Point E Intersection of S and D"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["Equilibrium Price P Star"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Equilibrium Quantity Q Star"]
    nodeG["Region Above E Shortage Exists"] -.-> nodeD
    nodeH["Region Below E Surplus Exists"] -.-> nodeD
```

### 4.2 Diagram: Effects of Demand and Supply Shifts

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Initial Equilibrium E0 at P0 Q0"] --> nodeB["Shift in Demand"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Shift in Supply"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Demand Rises Rightward Shift to D1"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Demand Falls Leftward Shift to D2"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Supply Rises Rightward Shift to S1"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Supply Falls Leftward Shift to S2"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["New Equilibrium E1 P1 Higher Q1 Higher"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["New Equilibrium E2 P2 Lower Q2 Lower"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["New Equilibrium E3 P3 Lower Q3 Higher"]
    nodeG --> nodeK["New Equilibrium E4 P4 Higher Q4 Lower"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram: Production Function — Input to Output Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Inputs Land Labour Capital Entrepreneurship"] --> nodeB["Production Function Q equals f of L K Land etc"]
    nodeC["State of Technology A"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD["Output Q Maximum Possible Given Inputs"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Short Run At least one input fixed"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Long Run All inputs variable"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Cobb Douglas Q equals A L alpha K beta"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Market Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Buyers Households"] -->|Demand Qd| nodeD["Market Clearing Mechanism"]
    nodeB["Sellers Firms"] -->|Supply Qs| nodeD
    nodeC["Government Policy Taxes Subsidies"] -.->|Influences| nodeD
    nodeE["Non Price Determinants Income Tastes Expectations"] -.->|Shift Demand| nodeA
    nodeF["Non Price Determinants Input Costs Technology"] -.->|Shift Supply| nodeB
    nodeD -->|If Qd greater than Qs| nodeG["Price Rises Shortage Signal"]
    nodeD -->|If Qd less than Qs| nodeH["Price Falls Surplus Signal"]
    nodeG --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeD -->|If Qd equals Qs| nodeI["Equilibrium Achieved P Star and Q Star"]
```

### 4.5 Diagram: Cobb-Douglas Returns to Scale Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Cobb Douglas Q equals A L alpha K beta"] --> nodeB["Calculate Sum of Exponents alpha plus beta"]
    nodeB -->|Sum greater than 1| nodeC["Increasing Returns to Scale IRS"]
    nodeB -->|Sum equals 1| nodeD["Constant Returns to Scale CRS"]
    nodeB -->|Sum less than 1| nodeE["Decreasing Returns to Scale DRS"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Doubling inputs more than doubles output"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Doubling inputs exactly doubles output"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Doubling inputs less than doubles output"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Distinguish between a change in supply and a change in quantity supplied. Illustrate with a suitable diagram."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Change in Supply:** A shift of the entire supply curve to the right (increase) or left (decrease), caused by changes in *non-price determinants* such as input costs, technology, number of sellers, taxes/subsidies, or expectations.
> - **Change in Quantity Supplied:** A movement *along* a given supply curve from one point to another, caused *only* by a change in the commodity's own price.
> - **Diagram:** Two-panel figure — Panel A shows supply curve shifting from $S$ to $S'$ (change in supply); Panel B shows movement from point A to point B along the same curve $S$ (change in quantity supplied). The shift arises from external factors, while movement arises from price signals.
> *(Award 1 mark for each distinction + 1 mark for diagram illustration.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**"What is a production function? State its significance in managerial decision-making."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition:** A production function is a technological relationship that shows the maximum output that can be produced from a given combination of inputs during a specified time period. It is symbolically expressed as $Q = f(A, B, C, \ldots)$.
> - **Significance 1:** Helps managers determine the *optimal input combination* for a desired level of output (input minimization).
> - **Significance 2:** Aids in *capacity planning* and forecasting — e.g., estimating output when a new factory line is added.
> - **Significance 3:** Useful for *cost control* by identifying the stage where marginal productivity declines, preventing over-employment of inputs.
> *(Award 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for two significance points + 1 mark for practical implication.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Internal Choice Type]**

**Q. (a)** The demand and supply functions of a commodity are given as:
$$Q_d = 500 - 10P \quad \text{and} \quad Q_s = -100 + 20P$$

**Find:**
(i) The equilibrium price and equilibrium quantity.
(ii) Suppose the government imposes a tax of ₹2 per unit on the producer. Determine the new equilibrium price and quantity. What is the burden of tax shared between consumer and producer?

**(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution — Part (a):**
>
> **(i) Initial Equilibrium (3 marks):**
>
> Set $Q_d = Q_s$:
> $$500 - 10P = -100 + 20P$$
> $$600 = 30P$$
> $$P^* = 20$$
> $$Q^* = 500 - 10(20) = 500 - 200 = 300 \text{ units}$$
>
> **(ii) Effect of ₹2 Tax on Producer (4 marks):**
>
> A tax of ₹2 per unit on the producer means the producer now receives $(P - 2)$ per unit effectively. The new supply function becomes:
> $$Q_s' = -100 + 20(P - 2) = -100 + 20P - 40 = -140 + 20P$$
>
> Set $Q_d = Q_s'$:
> $$500 - 10P = -140 + 20P$$
> $$640 = 30P$$
> $$P^{*\prime} = 21.33$$
> $$Q^{*\prime} = 500 - 10(21.33) = 286.7 \text{ units}$$
>
> **Tax Burden Analysis:**
> - Original price: ₹20; New price paid by consumer: ₹21.33
> - **Consumer's burden per unit:** $21.33 - 20 = ₹1.33$
> - **Producer's effective receipt per unit:** $21.33 - 2 = ₹19.33$
> - **Producer's burden per unit:** $20 - 19.33 = ₹0.67$
> - Total tax = Consumer burden + Producer burden = $₹1.33 + ₹0.67 = ₹2$ ✓
>
> **Conclusion:** The consumer bears the larger share (₹1.33) because demand is relatively less elastic than supply in this linear setup.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the various determinants of supply. How does a change in each determinant affect the supply curve? Illustrate with diagrams.

**(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution — Part (b):**
>
> **Determinants of Supply (5 marks — 1 mark each for the first five, with diagram description):**
>
> 1. **Price of the Good Itself:** Causes a *movement along* the supply curve, not a shift. Higher price → higher quantity supplied (upward movement).
>
> 2. **Prices of Inputs/Factors of Production:** A rise in input costs (e.g., wages, raw material prices) shifts the supply curve **leftward** (decrease in supply). A fall shifts it rightward.
>
> 3. **State of Technology:** Improvement in technology (e.g., automation, better machinery) shifts the supply curve **rightward** (increase in supply), as more output can be produced with same inputs.
>
> 4. **Number of Sellers/Firms:** Entry of new firms into the market shifts supply **rightward**; exit of firms shifts it **leftward**.
>
> 5. **Government Policies (Taxes & Subsidies):** Imposition of taxes raises production cost, shifting supply **leftward**. Subsidies lower effective cost, shifting supply **rightward**.
>
> 6. **Expectations of Future Prices:** If producers expect higher future prices, they may withhold current supply, shifting the current supply curve **leftward**.
>
> 7. **Natural Factors (for agricultural goods):** Favourable weather shifts supply rightward; drought/pest attacks shift it leftward.
>
> **Diagrammatic Illustration (2 marks):**
> A single diagram showing:
> - Original supply $S$
> - Leftward shift to $S'$ (e.g., due to tax or input cost rise)
> - Rightward shift to $S''$ (e.g., due to technology improvement or subsidy)
> - Annotations on each curve identifying the cause of shift
> - Equilibrium points $E$, $E'$, $E''$ marked

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2022 | Internal Choice Type]**

**Q. (a)** What is a Cobb-Douglas production function? A firm uses the production function:
$$Q = 4L^{0.5}K^{0.5}$$
Currently, the firm employs $L = 100$ and $K = 400$ units. Find:
(i) The current level of output.
(ii) The marginal product of labour ($MP_L$) and marginal product of capital ($MP_K$) at the current input levels.
(iii) Comment on the returns to scale of this production function.

**(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution — Part (a):**
>
> **(i) Current Output (2 marks):**
> $$Q = 4 \cdot (100)^{0.5} \cdot (400)^{0.5} = 4 \cdot 10 \cdot 20 = 800 \text{ units}$$
>
> **(ii) Marginal Products (3 marks):**
>
> For Cobb-Douglas $Q = 4L^{0.5}K^{0.5}$:
>
> **Marginal Product of Labour:**
> $$MP_L = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial L} = 4 \cdot 0.5 \cdot L^{-0.5} \cdot K^{0.5} = 2 \cdot \frac{K^{0.5}}{L^{0.5}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{K}{L}}$$
> $$MP_L = 2\sqrt{\frac{400}{100}} = 2\sqrt{4} = 2 \cdot 2 = 4 \text{ units}$$
>
> **Marginal Product of Capital:**
> $$MP_K = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial K} = 4 \cdot L^{0.5} \cdot 0.5 \cdot K^{-0.5} = 2 \cdot \frac{L^{0.5}}{K^{0.5}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{L}{K}}$$
> $$MP_K = 2\sqrt{\frac{100}{400}} = 2\sqrt{0.25} = 2 \cdot 0.5 = 1 \text{ unit}$$
>
> **(iii) Returns to Scale (2 marks):**
>
> The sum of exponents: $\alpha + \beta = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1$.
> Since the sum equals 1, the production function exhibits **Constant Returns to Scale (CRS)**.
>
> **Verification:** If both inputs are doubled ($L = 200$, $K = 800$):
> $$Q' = 4 \cdot (200)^{0.5} \cdot (800)^{0.5} = 4 \cdot 14.14 \cdot 28.28 \approx 1600$$
>
> This is exactly twice the original output of 800, confirming CRS. ✓

---

**Q. (b)** With the help of suitable diagrams, explain the concept of market equilibrium. Discuss the effects of the following on equilibrium price and quantity:
(i) A simultaneous increase in both demand and supply.
(ii) A simultaneous decrease in demand and increase in supply.

**(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution — Part (b):**
>
> **Concept of Market Equilibrium (2 marks):**
> Market equilibrium is the state where the quantity demanded by consumers equals the quantity supplied by producers at a given price. The corresponding price is the **equilibrium price** ($P^*$), and the corresponding quantity is the **equilibrium quantity** ($Q^*$). Graphically, it is the **intersection of the demand and supply curves**.
>
> **Mechanism:** If price is above $P^*$, a surplus arises, pushing prices down. If price is below $P^*$, a shortage arises, pushing prices up. At $P^*$, there is no pressure for change.
>
