# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
*Distinguish between explicit cost and implicit cost. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Explicit Cost** is the actual monetary payment made to outsiders for obtaining goods and services. It is recorded in the books of accounts as a verifiable expense.

*Examples:* (i) Wages paid to workers. (ii) Rent paid to the landlord.

**Implicit Cost** is the imputed monetary value of the owner's own resources used in the business. It is *not* an actual cash outflow but represents a real opportunity cost.

*Examples:* (i) Salary the owner could have earned by working elsewhere. (ii) Interest foregone on the owner's own capital invested in the firm.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "implicit cost is not a real cost." This is *wrong*. Implicit cost is **real** and is included in economic profit calculation. KTU deducts 1 mark for this conceptual error.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
*A company's fixed cost is ₹50,000, variable cost per unit is ₹150, and selling price is ₹250. Calculate the break-even quantity in units.*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
$FC = 50{,}000$ ₹, $v = 150$ ₹/unit, $SP = 250$ ₹/unit.

**Step 2 — Compute contribution per unit:**

$$C_{unit} = SP - v = 250 - 150 = 100 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the break-even formula:**

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{C_{unit}} = \frac{50{,}000}{100} = 500 \text{ units}$$

**Answer:** The company must sell **500 units** to break even.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Full internal choice is provided.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — Cost Classification and Behaviour**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Estimated]**

**(a)** Classify the following costs into (i) Fixed/Variable/Semi-variable, and (ii) Direct/Indirect. Justify each classification.
1. Depreciation on factory machinery (straight-line method).
2. Cost of raw materials.
3. Electricity bill of the factory.
4. Salary of the factory supervisor.
5. Wages of a worker who works exclusively on Product A.

**(b)** A manufacturing firm has the following cost data for the last four months:

| Month | Output (units) | Total Cost (₹) |
|-------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | 500 | 30,000 |
| 2 | 800 | 42,000 |
| 3 | 1000 | 50,000 |
| 4 | 1200 | 58,000 |

Using the **High-Low Method**, separate the fixed and variable components of the cost. Also estimate the total cost for an output of 1500 units.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**(1) Depreciation on factory machinery (SLM):**
- (i) *Fixed* — does not change with output under the straight-line method.
- (ii) *Indirect* — common to all products, cannot be traced to a single product directly.

**(2) Cost of raw materials:**
- (i) *Variable* — varies directly with output.
- (ii) *Direct* — traceable to the specific product.

**(3) Electricity bill:**
- (i) *Semi-variable* — contains a fixed minimum charge plus a variable usage charge.
- (ii) *Indirect* — common to all products in the factory.

**(4) Salary of factory supervisor:**
- (i) *Fixed* — paid at a fixed monthly rate.
- (ii) *Indirect* — supervises all products, not traceable to a single product.

**(5) Wages of a worker exclusive to Product A:**
- (i) *Variable* — if paid on piece-rate; could be fixed if monthly salary, but typically variable.
- (ii) *Direct* — wholly traceable to Product A.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Identify high and low activity levels:**

- High: 1200 units → ₹58,000
- Low: 500 units → ₹30,000

**Step 2 — Compute variable cost per unit ($b$):**

$$b = \frac{58{,}000 - 30{,}000}{1200 - 500} = \frac{28{,}000}{700} = 40 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 3 — Compute fixed cost ($a$) using the high point:**

$$a = 58{,}000 - (40)(1200) = 58{,}000 - 48{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4 — Cost function:**

$$TC = 10{,}000 + 40Q$$

**Step 5 — Estimate cost at 1500 units:**

$$TC_{1500} = 10{,}000 + 40(1500) = 10{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 70{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Answer:** Fixed cost = ₹10,000; Variable cost rate = ₹40/unit; Estimated cost at 1500 units = **₹70,000**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — Break-Even Analysis and Opportunity Cost**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** A small-scale LED bulb manufacturing unit has a fixed cost of **₹1,20,000 per month**. The variable cost per bulb is **₹60** and the selling price is **₹120**.
1. Determine the break-even point (in units and in rupees of sales).
2. Calculate the profit/loss if the firm produces and sells 2500 bulbs in a month.
3. If the firm wants a target profit of ₹60,000 per month, how many bulbs must be sold?

**(b)** An engineer leaves her corporate job paying **₹12,00,000 per annum** to start a tech-startup using **₹30,00,000 of her own savings**. The savings would have earned **7% annual interest** in a bank. In the first year, her explicit business expenses are **₹4,00,000** and total revenue is **₹22,00,000**.
1. Compute the accounting profit and the economic profit for the year.
2. Should she continue with the startup? Justify with a numerical argument.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$FC = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}, \quad v = 60 \text{ ₹/unit}, \quad SP = 120 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 2 — Contribution per unit:**

$$C_{unit} = 120 - 60 = 60 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 3 — Break-even quantity:**

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{C_{unit}} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{60} = 2000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Break-even sales revenue:**

$$\text{Sales at BE} = SP \times Q_{BE} = 120 \times 2000 = 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 5 — Profit/Loss at 2500 bulbs:**

$$\text{Profit} = (SP - v)Q - FC = (60)(2500) - 1{,}20{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6 — Target profit of ₹60,000:**

$$Q_{target} = \frac{FC + \text{Target Profit}}{C_{unit}} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000 + 60{,}000}{60} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{60} = 3000 \text{ units}$$

**Final Answers:**
1. Break-even = **2000 units** or **₹2,40,000 in sales**.
2. Profit at 2500 units = **₹30,000**.
3. Bulbs for ₹60,000 target profit = **3000 units**.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Identify all cost components:**

- Explicit cost = ₹4,00,000
- Implicit cost (foregone salary) = ₹12,00,000
- Implicit cost (foregone interest on own capital) = $0.07 \times 30{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}10{,}000$ ₹

**Step 2 — Accounting Profit:**

$$\text{Accounting Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Explicit Cost} = 22{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3 — Economic Profit:**

$$\text{Total Economic Cost} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}10{,}000 = 18{,}10{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Economic Profit} = 22{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}10{,}000 = 3{,}90{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
Since economic profit is **positive (₹3,90,000)**, the engineer is earning *more* than her next best alternative. She should **continue with the startup**. The accounting profit of ₹18,00,000 is misleading because it ignores the substantial implicit costs of her own time and capital.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. *Forgetting units* in the final answer (e.g., writing "$Q_{BE} = 2000$" without "units").
> 2. *Confusing accounting profit with economic profit* in opportunity cost problems.
> 3. *Including sunk costs* (e.g., past R&D) in break-even calculations.
> 4. *Misclassifying* depreciation as a variable cost. Under SLM, depreciation is **fixed**.
> 5. *Using total cost* instead of *contribution* in break-even formulas.
> 6. *Forgetting the fixed component* when applying the high-low method.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Cost** is a *monetary sacrifice* of resources, not just an expenditure.
- **Fixed Cost** stays constant in *total*; **Variable Cost** varies with *output*; **Semi-Variable** has both.
- **Direct Cost** is traceable to a single product; **Indirect Cost** is allocated across products.
- **Explicit Cost** = actual cash paid; **Implicit Cost** = imputed value of owner's resources.
- **Sunk Cost** is *past, irrecoverable, and irrelevant* to current decisions — **never include it** in decision-making.
- **Opportunity Cost** = return from the *next best foregone* alternative. It is the foundation of economic profit.
- **Marginal Cost** is the *additional cost* of producing *one more unit* = $\frac{d(TC)}{dQ}$.
- **Break-Even Point** is where $TR = TC$, calculated as $Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{SP - VC}$.
- **Contribution** = $SP - VC$. This is the per-unit amount available to cover $FC$ and then generate profit.
- **High-Low Method** decomposes semi-variable cost into $a + bQ$ using the highest and lowest activity levels only.
- **AFC** is a rectangular hyperbola (always falls as $Q$ rises); **AVC** and **AC** are typically U-shaped in real factories with learning-curve effects.
- In the *short run*, a firm should continue operating if $SP \geq AVC$ (to minimise loss).
- In the *long run*, the firm must have $SP \geq AC$ to survive.
- **Accounting Profit** ignores implicit costs; **Economic Profit** includes them. The two differ precisely by the value of *implicit costs*.
- KTU questions on this topic frequently combine **two sub-parts**: (i) a *classification* or *theory* question (7 marks), and (ii) a *numerical* problem (7 marks).
- Always show *Step 1, Step 2, …* in numerical answers — KTU examiners award step marks even if the final answer is incorrect.
- The **relevant range** concept is implicit in cost analysis: $FC$ is fixed only within a relevant range of output.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Private Cost and Social Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Private Cost and Social Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Private Cost (PC)

**Private Cost** is the cost actually incurred by a producer (firm or individual) for the production of a good or service. It represents the **internal expenditure** borne directly by the decision-maker, and is fully captured in the **market price** of the output.

Formally, Private Cost comprises two components:
- **Explicit (Accounting) Costs**: Out-of-pocket monetary payments — wages, rent, raw materials, fuel, interest on borrowed capital.
- **Implicit (Opportunity) Costs**: The value of the next-best alternative foregone — owner's time, self-owned land, own capital.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Private cost is the cost borne by the producer in producing a commodity. It is the actual expenditure incurred by the firm on factors of production, resources, and inputs used in the manufacturing process.*

### 1.2 Social Cost (SC)

**Social Cost** is the **total cost to society** of producing a good or service. It equals the private cost of production **plus** any additional costs imposed on third parties who are not part of the production or consumption decision (i.e., external costs/negative externalities).

Mathematically:

$$\text{Social Cost (SC)} = \text{Private Cost (PC)} + \text{External Cost (EC)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When production has **no external effects** (no pollution, no congestion, no third-party impact), Social Cost = Private Cost. This is the assumption of perfect competition in a frictionless market.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Riverside Steel Factory

Imagine a **steel factory** located beside a river in Kerala.
- The factory owner pays for iron ore, labour, electricity, and land — these are **private costs**.
- However, the factory also discharges chemical effluents into the river, killing fish and contaminating drinking water for downstream villages.
- The villagers suffer health costs, lost livelihoods, and water purification expenses — these are **external costs** the factory owner does **not** pay.
- **Private Cost** = what the factory pays.
- **Social Cost** = Private Cost + the damage suffered by the villagers and environment.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Think of Private Cost as *"my cheque book"* and Social Cost as *"everyone's cheque book combined."* The difference is the bill someone else is forced to pay without consenting.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Term | Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Marginal Private Cost | **MPC** | ₹ / unit |
| Marginal Social Cost | **MSC** | ₹ / unit |
| Marginal External Cost | **MEC** | ₹ / unit |
| Pigouvian Tax | **t** | ₹ / unit |
| Marginal Damage | **MD** | ₹ / unit |

The fundamental divergence identity used throughout KTU problems:

$$\text{MSC} = \text{MPC} + \text{MEC}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Costs

A producer's decision-making cost stack can be visualized as a hierarchy:

**Step 1 — Identify Explicit Costs**
These are direct monetary outflows recorded in accounting books. Examples: salaries, rent, utility bills, raw material purchases, depreciation.

**Step 2 — Identify Implicit Costs**
The opportunity cost of using self-owned resources. Example: An entrepreneur starting a startup forgoes a ₹10 lakh job — that is an implicit cost.

**Step 3 — Sum to get Private Cost**
$$\text{PC} = \text{Explicit Cost} + \text{Implicit Cost}$$

**Step 4 — Identify External Costs**
Costs imposed on third parties not involved in the transaction. Examples: pollution, traffic congestion, noise, carbon emissions, water contamination.

**Step 5 — Combine to get Social Cost**
$$\text{SC} = \text{PC} + \text{EC}$$

### 2.2 Why the Divergence Matters — The Externality Link

When **MEC > 0** (negative externality exists), the producer's decision-making **systematically over-produces** the good because they only see MPC, while society bears MSC. This gap is the **economic loss to society** and is the textbook definition of **market failure**.

| Producer's View (Decision Basis) | Society's View (Welfare Basis) |
|---|---|
| Considers only MPC | Considers MSC = MPC + MEC |
| Sets output where MPC = Marginal Benefit (MB) | Optimal output is where MSC = MB |
| Produces **too much** | Desires lower production |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Interpretation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{PC} = \text{EC}_{\text{explicit}} + \text{EC}_{\text{implicit}}$ | Private cost decomposition | ₹ |
| 2 | $\text{SC} = \text{PC} + \text{EC}_{\text{external}}$ | Social cost = private + external | ₹ |
| 3 | $\text{MSC} = \text{MPC} + \text{MEC}$ | Marginal social cost divergence | ₹ / unit |
| 4 | $\text{MEC} = \text{MSC} - \text{MPC}$ | Marginal external cost extraction | ₹ / unit |
| 5 | $t^{*} = \text{MEC}$ at $Q^{*}$ | Pigouvian tax (corrective tax) | ₹ / unit |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{market}} > Q_{\text{social-optimal}}$ | Negative externality creates over-production | units |
| 7 | $\text{DWL} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (Q_m - Q_s) \cdot (MSC - MPC)$ | Deadweight welfare loss | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** When asked to *"find the external cost,"* always compute $\text{EC} = \text{SC} - \text{PC}$ at the relevant output level. Many students mistakenly use absolute values or sign errors.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Policy Utility

- **Environmental Engineering**: Calculating the true cost of a power plant requires adding pollution-control costs and health damages to private operating cost.
- **Public Policy**: Carbon taxes in India are designed as Pigouvian taxes to force industries to internalize $\text{CO}_2$ external costs.
- **Infrastructure Projects**: Highway tolls are often justified as user fees that approximate the marginal external congestion cost.
- **Project Appraisal**: KTU's engineering economics module uses social cost to evaluate whether a dam, factory, or mining project is **socially optimal**, not just privately profitable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Social Cost Identity

We start from first principles.

**Step 1** — Let the production of $Q$ units require inputs $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ with market prices $(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$.

The private cost function is the minimum expenditure on inputs to produce $Q$:

$$\text{PC}(Q) = \min \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad f(x_1, \dots, x_n) \geq Q$$

**Step 2** — In addition, the production process generates external damages $D(Q)$ to third parties (pollution, health costs, congestion).

These damages are real resource costs to society, but are **not paid** by the producer.

**Step 3** — The total cost to society for producing $Q$ units is the sum of what the producer pays and what third parties suffer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SC}(Q) &= \text{PC}(Q) + D(Q) \\
&= \text{PC}(Q) + \text{EC}(Q)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Differentiating both sides with respect to $Q$ to obtain marginal quantities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(\text{SC})}{dQ} &= \frac{d(\text{PC})}{dQ} + \frac{d(\text{EC})}{dQ} \\
\text{MSC}(Q) &= \text{MPC}(Q) + \text{MEC}(Q)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU identity** for any problem involving externalities.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Pigouvian (Corrective) Tax

**Step 1** — Market equilibrium (producer's view) occurs where:

$$\text{MPC}(Q_m) = \text{MB}(Q_m)$$

**Step 2** — Social optimum (society's view) occurs where:

$$\text{MSC}(Q_s) = \text{MB}(Q_s)$$

**Step 3** — Subtract Step 1 from Step 2 using the identity $\text{MSC} = \text{MPC} + \text{MEC}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSC}(Q_s) - \text{MPC}(Q_m) &= \text{MB}(Q_s) - \text{MB}(Q_m) \\
\text{MPC}(Q_s) + \text{MEC}(Q_s) - \text{MPC}(Q_m) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Assume $\text{MB}$ is approximately constant in the relevant range and $\text{MPC}$ is increasing. At $Q_s$, the gap between MSC and MPC is exactly the external cost:

$$\text{MEC}(Q_s) = \text{MSC}(Q_s) - \text{MPC}(Q_s)$$

**Step 5** — To make the producer internalize this, impose a per-unit tax:

$$t^{*} = \text{MEC}(Q_s) = \text{MSC}(Q_s) - \text{MPC}(Q_s)$$

This shifts the MPC curve upward by $t^{*}$, so that the new effective MPC becomes MPC + $t^{*}$ = MSC, and the market produces $Q_s$ instead of $Q_m$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A cement factory has the following cost structure:
- $\text{MPC}(Q) = 5 + 0.5Q$ (in ₹ lakhs per 1000 tonnes)
- Marginal external cost: $\text{MEC}(Q) = 0.3Q$ (in ₹ lakhs per 1000 tonnes)
- Marginal benefit: $\text{MB}(Q) = 20 - 0.4Q$

**Find:** Market output, social optimum, Pigouvian tax, deadweight loss.

---

**Step 1 — Market Equilibrium (where MPC = MB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
5 + 0.5Q_m &= 20 - 0.4Q_m \\
0.5Q_m + 0.4Q_m &= 20 - 5 \\
0.9Q_m &= 15 \\
Q_m &= 16.67 \text{ (thousand tonnes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MSC function:**

$$\text{MSC}(Q) = \text{MPC}(Q) + \text{MEC}(Q) = (5 + 0.5Q) + 0.3Q = 5 + 0.8Q$$

**Step 3 — Social Optimum (where MSC = MB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
5 + 0.8Q_s &= 20 - 0.4Q_s \\
0.8Q_s + 0.4Q_s &= 20 - 5 \\
1.2Q_s &= 15 \\
Q_s &= 12.5 \text{ (thousand tonnes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Pigouvian Tax at $Q_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t^{*} &= \text{MEC}(Q_s) = 0.3 \times 12.5 \\
t^{*} &= 3.75 \text{ ₹ lakhs per 1000 tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Deadweight Loss (DWL):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DWL} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (Q_m - Q_s) \cdot \text{MEC}(Q_s) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (16.67 - 12.5) \cdot 3.75 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4.17 \cdot 3.75 \\
&= 7.81 \text{ ₹ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Implications

| Scenario | MPC vs MSC | Externality | Tax Required | Output Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| No externality | MPC = MSC | None | Zero | $Q_m = Q_s$ |
| Negative externality (pollution) | MPC < MSC | Cost to third party | $t^{*} = \text{MEC}$ | $Q_m \downarrow$ to $Q_s$ |
| Positive externality (R&D, education) | MPC > MSC | Benefit to third party | Subsidy $= \text{MEB}$ | $Q_m \uparrow$ to $Q_s$ |
| Pigouvian tax applied | MPC + $t$ = MSC | Internalized | $t = \text{MEC}$ at $Q_s$ | Equilibrium restored |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cost Component Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Cost of Production] --> B[Private Cost PC]
    A --> C[External Cost EC]
    
    B --> B1[Explicit Costs]
    B --> B2[Implicit Costs]
    
    B1 --> B1a[Wages and Salaries]
    B1 --> B1b[Raw Materials]
    B1 --> B1c[Rent and Utilities]
    B1 --> B1d[Interest on Loans]
    
    B2 --> B2a[Owner Time Forgone]
    B2 --> B2b[Self Owned Capital]
    B2 --> B2c[Self Owned Land]
    
    C --> C1[Pollution Damage]
    C --> C2[Health Costs]
    C --> C3[Congestion]
    C --> C4[Resource Depletion]
    
    A --> D[Marginal Social Cost MSC]
    B --> E[Marginal Private Cost MPC]
    C --> F[Marginal External Cost MEC]
    
    D --> G["MSC = MPC plus MEC"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

### 4.2 Market vs Social Equilibrium — Pigouvian Tax Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1["Market Outcome Without Tax"]
        direction TB
        A1["MPC equals MB at Qm"] --> A2["Producer Output Qm High"]
        A2 --> A3["Society bears MEC"]
    end
    
    subgraph M2["Social Optimum With Pigouvian Tax t Star"]
        direction TB
        B1["MSC equals MB at Qs"] --> B2["Reduced Output Qs Lower"]
        B2 --> B3["External Cost Eliminated"]
    end
    
    M1 -->|Government Imposes Tax t Star equal to MEC| M2
    
    A2 -->|Over production| C1["Deadweight Loss DWL Triangle"]
    B2 -->|Efficient production| C2["No DWL Social Optimum Achieved"]
    
    style A3 fill:#ffe5e5
    style B3 fill:#e5ffe5
    style C1 fill:#ffcccc
    style C2 fill:#ccffcc
```

### 4.3 Divergence Schematic — MPC and MSC Curve Relation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Producer Decision Basis] --> P2["Marginal Private Cost MPC Curve"]
    S1[Society Welfare Basis] --> S2["Marginal Social Cost MSC Curve"]
    E1[External Cost MEC] --> E2["Vertical Gap between MSC and MPC"]
    
    P2 --> R1["Market Equilibrium Qm where MPC = MB"]
    S2 --> R2["Social Optimum Qs where MSC = MB"]
    E2 --> R3["Gap equals Pigouvian Tax"]
    
    R1 --> O1["Qm Greater Than Qs Over Production"]
    R2 --> O2["Qs Lower than Qm Efficient Output"]
    R3 --> O3["Government Intervention Needed"]
    
    O1 --> W1["Deadweight Loss to Society"]
    O2 --> W2["Maximum Social Welfare Achieved"]
    
    style P2 fill:#fff4e1
    style S2 fill:#e1f0ff
    style E2 fill:#ffe1e1
    style W1 fill:#ffcccc
    style W2 fill:#ccffcc
```

### 4.4 Pigouvian Tax Effect (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Producer with MPC Curve] --> Tax{Goverment Applies Pigouvian Tax t Star}
    Tax -->|No| M1[Market Qm where MPC = MB]
    Tax -->|Yes| M2[New Effective MC equals MPC + t Star equals MSC]
    
    M1 --> Out1[Qm Higher Over Production]
    M2 --> Out2[Qs Lower Optimal Output]
    
    Out1 --> Cost1[Society Pays MEC Externally]
    Out2 --> Cost2[Producer Internalizes Externality]
    
    Cost1 --> Loss[Deadweight Loss DWL]
    Cost2 --> Win[Social Welfare Maximized]
    
    style Start fill:#e1f5ff
    style M1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style M2 fill:#e1ffe1
    style Loss fill:#ffcccc
    style Win fill:#ccffcc
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1.** Distinguish between **private cost** and **social cost**. Why does this distinction matter in engineering project appraisal?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Private Cost (1 mark):** Private cost is the actual expenditure incurred by a producer (firm) in the production process. It includes explicit costs (wages, rent, materials) and implicit costs (owner's opportunity cost). It is reflected in the market price of the output.
- **Social Cost (1 mark):** Social cost is the total cost incurred by society as a whole, equal to private cost plus external cost imposed on third parties (pollution, health damage, congestion).
- **Why it matters in engineering projects (1 mark):** Engineering projects often generate externalities — dams displace people, factories pollute, highways cause noise. KTU project appraisal requires Social Cost-Benefit Analysis (SCBA) so that an apparently profitable project is not approved if its social cost exceeds social benefit. This prevents hidden welfare losses to the community.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2.** Define **negative externality**. Explain how a **Pigouvian tax** corrects the divergence between marginal private cost and marginal social cost.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Negative Externality (1 mark):** A negative externality is a cost imposed on a third party not involved in the production or consumption of a good — e.g., a factory's smoke causing respiratory illness in nearby residents. Mathematically, $\text{MEC} > 0$, hence $\text{MSC} > \text{MPC}$.
- **Pigouvian Tax Mechanism (2 marks):** A Pigouvian tax is a per-unit tax imposed on the producer equal to the marginal external cost at the socially optimal output: $t^{*} = \text{MEC}(Q_s)$. This tax shifts the MPC curve upward by exactly $t^{*}$, so that $\text{MPC} + t^{*} = \text{MSC}$. The producer now internalizes the externality, output falls from $Q_m$ to $Q_s$, deadweight loss is eliminated, and social welfare is maximized.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — University ESE Model Paper, Module 2]**

---

#### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Option 1

**(a)** *[7 marks]* A paper mill discharges effluents into a river. The marginal private cost of production is $\text{MPC}(Q) = 4 + 0.6Q$ and the marginal external cost is $\text{MEC}(Q) = 0.4Q$ (all in ₹ crore per 1000 tonnes). The marginal benefit to consumers is $\text{MB}(Q) = 22 - 0.5Q$.

1. Determine the market equilibrium output $Q_m$.
2. Determine the socially optimal output $Q_s$.
3. Calculate the Pigouvian tax required to restore social optimum.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* Explain with the help of a diagram the concept of **deadweight loss** arising from negative externalities. How does the Pigouvian tax eliminate this loss?

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Solution [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 — Market equilibrium where $\text{MPC} = \text{MB}$:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 + 0.6Q_m &= 22 - 0.5Q_m \\
0.6Q_m + 0.5Q_m &= 22 - 4 \\
1.1Q_m &= 18 \\
Q_m &= 16.36 \text{ thousand tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 marks]**

*Step 2 — Social optimum: First compute MSC:*

$$\text{MSC}(Q) = \text{MPC}(Q) + \text{MEC}(Q) = (4 + 0.6Q) + 0.4Q = 4 + Q$$

Set $\text{MSC} = \text{MB}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 + Q_s &= 22 - 0.5Q_s \\
Q_s + 0.5Q_s &= 22 - 4 \\
1.5Q_s &= 18 \\
Q_s &= 12 \text{ thousand tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**[3 marks]**

*Step 3 — Pigouvian tax at $Q_s$:*

$$t^{*} = \text{MEC}(Q_s) = 0.4 \times 12 = 4.8 \text{ ₹ crore per 1000 tonnes}$$

**[2 marks]**

*Verification:* New effective MC = MPC + $t^{*}$ = $4 + 0.6Q + 4.8$ = $8.8 + 0.6Q$.
Setting this equal to MB: $8.8 + 0.6Q = 22 - 0.5Q \Rightarrow 1.1Q = 13.2 \Rightarrow Q = 12$ ✓ (matches $Q_s$)

---

**(b) Solution [7 marks]:**

*Definition [2 marks]:* Deadweight Loss (DWL) is the loss of total social welfare that occurs when the market produces a quantity different from the socially optimal quantity. In the presence of a negative externality, the market produces $Q_m > Q_s$, and the DWL is the area of the triangle bounded by the MSC curve, the MB curve, and the vertical line at $Q_s$.

*Diagrammatic Explanation [3 marks]:*

```
Cost
 │
 │         MSC = MPC + MEC
 │        /
 │       /│\
 │      / │ \
 │     /  │  \   MB
 │    /   │   \ /
 │   /    │ DWL\
 │  /     │  /\ \
 │ /      │ /  \ \
 │/ MPC___│/____\_\___ 
 │       Qs       Qm   Output
```

- The triangle DWL = $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (Q_m - Q_s) \cdot \text{MEC}(Q_s)$
- Numerical value: $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (16.36 - 12) \cdot 4.8 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4.36 \cdot 4.8 = 10.46$ ₹ crore

*How Pigouvian Tax Eliminates DWL [2 marks]:* A Pigouvian tax of $t^{*} = 4.8$ ₹ crore per 1000 tonnes shifts MPC upward to coincide with MSC. Producers now face their true marginal social cost and reduce output from $Q_m = 16.36$ to $Q_s = 12$. The DWL triangle vanishes and social welfare is maximized at the point where MSC = MB.

---

#### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Option 2

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Explain the components of **private cost** and **social cost** with suitable examples. Why are they often unequal in real-world engineering projects?

**(b)** *[7 marks]* A thermal power plant has the following cost functions:
- $\text{MPC}(Q) = 10 + 0.4Q$
- $\text{MEC}(Q) = 0.2Q$
- $\text{MB}(Q) = 30 - 0.3Q$

Determine:
1. The market output and the socially optimal output.
2. The magnitude of deadweight loss if no corrective tax is imposed.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Solution [7 marks]:**

*Components of Private Cost [2 marks]:*
Private cost has two components:
- **Explicit costs:** Direct monetary payments — wages ₹X, raw materials ₹Y, rent ₹Z, fuel ₹W, interest on capital ₹V. Example: A construction firm paying ₹50 lakh for steel, ₹20 lakh for cement, ₹30 lakh for labour.
- **Implicit costs:** Opportunity cost of self-owned resources. Example: A promoter using her own land worth ₹1 crore for which she forgoes rent of ₹8 lakh/year.

*Components of Social Cost [2 marks]:*
Social cost = Private cost + External cost.
- **External costs:** Costs imposed on third parties not in the transaction. Example: Air pollution from the same construction site causing asthma treatment costs of ₹5 lakh to nearby residents.

*Why they are often unequal [3 marks]:*
Real-world engineering projects almost always generate externalities:
- **Environmental degradation** (mining, thermal plants, chemical factories).
- **Health costs** to neighbouring communities.
- **Resource depletion** affecting future generations.
- **Congestion and noise** from infrastructure projects.
- **Carbon emissions** contributing to climate change.
Hence $\text{EC} > 0$ in most industrial contexts, and $\text{SC} > \text{PC}$. KTU emphasizes this because a project that is privately profitable may impose net welfare loss on society if externalities are not internalized.

---

**(b) Solution [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 — Market equilibrium:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 + 0.4Q_m &= 30 - 0.3Q_m \\
0.7Q_m &= 20 \\
Q_m &= 28.57 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 marks]**

*Step 2 — Compute MSC:*

$$\text{MSC}(Q) = (10 + 0.4Q) + 0.2Q = 10 + 0.6Q$$

*Step 3 — Social optimum where MSC = MB:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 + 0.6Q_s &= 30 - 0.3Q_s \\
0.9Q_s &= 20 \\
Q_s &= 22.22 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**[3 marks]**

*Step 4 — Deadweight loss:*

Pigouvian tax at $Q_s$:
$$t^{*} = \text{MEC}(Q_s) = 0.2 \times 22.22 = 4.44$$

Deadweight loss:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DWL} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (Q_m - Q_s) \cdot t^{*} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (28.57 - 22.22) \cdot 4.44 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 6.35 \cdot 4.44 \\
&= 14.10 \text{ ₹ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> - **Sign Error on MEC (2-mark loss trap):** Students often forget that $\text{MEC} > 0$ for negative externalities and write $\text{MSC} = \text{MPC} - \text{MEC}$. Remember: Social cost is **higher** than private cost when externalities are harmful.
> - **Confusing $Q_m$ with $Q_s$ (1-mark loss):** Always clearly label market vs social optimum. Use $Q_m$ for the producer's choice and $Q_s$ for society's choice. KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous labelling.
> - **DWL Triangle Formula (2-mark trap):** The DWL triangle's height is $\text{MEC}(Q_s)$, **not** $\text{MEC}(Q_m)$. Many students incorrectly compute height at the wrong output.
> - **Pigouvian Tax Verification (1-mark bonus):** Always verify your $t^{*}$ by substituting it back: $\text{MPC}(Q_s) + t^{*} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{MB}(Q_s)$. This shows the examiner that the new equilibrium is exactly $Q_s$, earning full method marks.
> - **No Diagram = Mark Loss:** KTU's 14-mark question on externality almost always expects a labelled cost-output diagram showing MPC, MSC, MB, $Q_m$, $Q_s$, and the DWL triangle. Omitting the diagram typically costs 2–3 marks.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- **Private Cost (PC)** = Explicit costs + Implicit costs. Borne **only** by the producer. Captured in market price.
- **Social Cost (SC)** = Private Cost + External Cost. Borne by **entire society**, including uninvolved third parties.
- **External Cost (EC)** = Cost imposed on third parties not part of the transaction (pollution, noise, congestion, health damage).
- **Master Identity (must memorize):** $\text{MSC} = \text{MPC} + \text{MEC}$.
- **Negative Externality:** $\text{MEC} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{MSC} > \text{MPC} \Rightarrow$ market over-produces ($Q_m > Q_s$).
- **Positive Externality:** $\text{MSC} < \text{MPC} \Rightarrow$ market under-produces ($Q_m < Q_s$); corrective instrument is a **subsidy**, not a tax.
- **Pigouvian Tax:** $t^{*} = \text{MEC}(Q_s)$. This is the **per-unit** tax that exactly internalizes the externality, shifting MPC up to MSC.
- **Deadweight Loss (DWL) Triangle:** $\text{DWL} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (Q_m - Q_s) \cdot \text{MEC}(Q_s)$. Represents welfare loss to society due to over-production.
- **Market Failure:** Divergence between private and social cost/benefit is the textbook definition. KTU expects you to identify it, quantify it, and prescribe Pigouvian tax as remedy.
- **Always verify equilibrium:** After computing $t^{*}$, substitute back to confirm $\text{MPC}(Q_s) + t^{*} = \text{MB}(Q_s)$. This step is worth bonus marks in KTU valuation.
- **Engineering relevance:** KTU repeatedly tests application to power plants, factories, mining, transport, and infrastructure — always frame answers in such contexts.
- **Diagram is mandatory** for full marks in 14-mark questions involving externalities, divergence, or Pigouvian tax.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sunk Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sunk Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sunk Cost (Definition):**
> A **sunk cost** is an expenditure that has already been incurred in the past, is **irrecoverable** (cannot be recovered or salvaged), and **must not influence** any current or future business decision. From an *Economics for Engineers* standpoint, sunk costs are **historically irreversible** and therefore **irrelevant** to marginal decision-making, marginal costing, and incremental analysis.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, sunk cost is positioned under **Module 2: Cost Concepts and Cost Behaviour**, alongside concepts like opportunity cost, marginal cost, differential cost, and relevant cost. The central idea is that **a rational engineer or manager evaluates decisions based on *future* cash flows, not past commitments**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Movie Ticket You Cannot Refund"

Imagine you buy a movie ticket for ₹300. Thirty minutes into the film, you realize the movie is terrible. You have two choices:

- **Option A:** Leave the theatre and study for tomorrow's exam (high future value).
- **Option B:** Stay and finish the movie "because you already paid ₹300" (sunk cost trap).

The ₹300 is **gone forever** — whether you stay or leave, it cannot be recovered. A rational decision-maker *ignores* the ₹300 and chooses whichever option has the better **future payoff** (studying). The act of staying "to recover the cost" is the classic **Sunk Cost Fallacy** (also called *Concorde Fallacy*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:**
> Examiners love testing whether you can **distinguish a sunk cost from a relevant cost** in numerical problems. A common trap is treating a past expense as a "deductible loss" in a present decision — this is **wrong** in marginal economic reasoning.

## 1.3 Examples Common in KTU Question Papers

| S.No | Example | Why it is Sunk |
|------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Depreciation on a machine already purchased | The money was spent when bought; depreciation is just an accounting allocation, not a cash outflow today. |
| 2 | Past R&D expenditure on a failed prototype | Money is gone; cannot influence whether to continue the project. |
| 3 | Money spent on advertising a discontinued product | Irrecoverable, regardless of future strategy. |
| 4 | Salary paid to existing permanent staff (committed) | Legally and contractually committed; cannot be avoided. |
| 5 | Specialized mould cost for a now-obsolete design | Past expense with zero salvage or alternative use. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, students are expected to (a) **identify** sunk costs, (b) **separate** them from relevant (incremental) costs, and (c) **demonstrate** the role of sunk costs in *make-or-buy*, *replace-vs-retain*, and *continue-or-abandon* decisions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Defining Characteristics of a Sunk Cost

1. **Already Incurred:** The cash has physically left the organization in a prior period.
2. **Irrecoverable:** No possible action in the present can bring the money back.
3. **Irrelevant to Marginal Decision:** Should be excluded from incremental cash-flow analysis.
4. **Independent of Future Action:** Whether you accept or reject a project, the cost remains paid.
5. **Not a "Future Avoidable" Cost:** Unlike avoidable or discretionary costs, sunk costs cannot be escaped by saying "no."

## 2.2 Why Engineers Must Understand Sunk Cost

Engineering projects — bridges, machines, software, chemical plants — are **capital-intensive** with **long gestation periods**. Engineers often make the mistake of *defending a failing project* simply because:

> *"We have already invested ₹50 lakhs, we cannot stop now."*

This is the **Escalation of Commitment** bias. The KTU 2024 module stresses that **economic rationality** demands ignoring the ₹50 lakhs and evaluating only the **incremental future cash flows**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Project Management (PMP/PRINCE2):** Gate reviews explicitly call out sunk costs as non-decisional.
> - **Lean Manufacturing:** Justifies "stopping the bleeding" on a defective production line.
> - **Software Engineering:** Aborting a failed Agile sprint without guilt over prior sprint costs.
> - **Civil Engineering:** Demolishing a sub-standard building rather than pouring more concrete into it.

## 2.3 Classification of Costs — Where Does Sunk Cost Sit?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{Fixed Cost} + \text{Variable Cost} \\[4pt]
\text{Decision-Relevant Costs} &= \text{Avoidable Cost} + \text{Opportunity Cost} \\[4pt]
\text{Decision-Irrelevant Costs} &= \text{Sunk Cost} + \text{Committed Fixed Cost (Historical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental equation of decision-making in engineering economics is:
> $$\text{Relevant Cost} = \text{Future Avoidable Cost} + \text{Opportunity Cost}$$
> Anything outside this — i.e., past, irreversible, non-cash — is sunk and must be **excluded**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Key Condition | Unit / Type |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Total Relevant Cost (TRC) | $\text{TRC} = \text{Avoidable Cost} + \text{Opportunity Cost}$ | Future costs only | ₹ (Currency) |
| 2 | Incremental Cost (IC) | $\text{IC} = \text{TC}_2 - \text{TC}_1$ | Between two alternatives | ₹ (Currency) |
| 3 | Depreciation (Non-Cash) | $D = \frac{C - S}{n}$ | Straight-line method | ₹/year |
| 4 | Book Value (BV) | $\text{BV}_n = C - \sum_{t=1}^{n} D_t$ | Historical accounting value | ₹ |
| 5 | Marginal Revenue – Marginal Cost Rule | $\text{Profit} = \text{MR} - \text{MC}$ | Optimization condition | ₹/unit |
| 6 | Opportunity Cost | $\text{OC} = \text{Best Forgone Alternative Return}$ | Always relevant | ₹ |
| 7 | Sunk Cost Exclusion Principle | $\text{Decision Metric} = f(\text{Future Cash Flows only})$ | Past costs are excluded | Logical Rule |
| 8 | Total Cash Outflow (Decision-Relevant) | $\text{TCO} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{FC}_t + \text{VC}_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Discounted future outflows | ₹ (PV) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write the depreciation expense as a *cash outflow* in a *replace-vs-retain* problem. **Depreciation is NOT a cash outflow** — it is a sunk/non-cash allocation. Only the **actual future cash spending** matters.

## 2.5 Sunk Cost vs. Other Confusing Cost Concepts

| Feature | Sunk Cost | Opportunity Cost | Avoidable Cost | Committed Cost |
|---------|-----------|------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Time of occurrence | Past | Future (implicit) | Future (explicit) | Past/Present |
| Recoverable? | No | N/A (forgone benefit) | Yes (by rejecting) | No |
| Relevant to decision? | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes** | No (in short-run) |
| Cash or Non-cash? | Either | Non-cash | Cash | Usually non-cash |
| KTU Example | Past R&D | Rent of own building | New machine cost | Manager's salary |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section demonstrates **quantitative application of the sunk cost concept** as expected in KTU numerical problems. Every step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Replace vs. Retain (The "Depreciation Trap")

**Problem Statement:**
A company purchased a machine 3 years ago for ₹1,00,000. It has a useful life of 10 years and a scrap value of ₹10,000 (straight-line depreciation). The current market value of the machine is ₹40,000. A new, more efficient machine costs ₹1,50,000, has a life of 10 years, and a scrap value of ₹15,000. The new machine will reduce annual operating cost by ₹20,000. The required rate of return is 10%. Should the company replace the machine?

### Step 1 — Compute the Book Value of the Old Machine

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Depreciation (Old)} &= \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}000}{10} = ₹9{,}000/\text{year} \\[4pt]
\text{Accumulated Depreciation (3 years)} &= 9{,}000 \times 3 = ₹27{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Book Value today} &= 1{,}00{,}000 - 27{,}000 = ₹73{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify What is Sunk and What is Relevant

| Item | Amount (₹) | Status | Reasoning |
|------|------------|--------|-----------|
| Original purchase cost | 1,00,000 | **Sunk** | Past expenditure, irrecoverable |
| Accumulated depreciation | 27,000 | **Non-cash, Sunk** | Accounting allocation only |
| Book value | 73,000 | **Sunk (accounting)** | Historical figure, not actual outflow |
| Current market value (salvage) | 40,000 | **Relevant (Opportunity cost)** | Realizable if sold today |
| Cost of new machine | 1,50,000 | **Relevant** | Future avoidable if we retain |
| Annual saving | 20,000 | **Relevant** | Future incremental benefit |

### Step 3 — Compute the Incremental Initial Investment

The real cash outflow today if we **replace** = New machine cost − Sale proceeds of old machine.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Initial Outlay} &= \text{Cost of New} - \text{Sale of Old} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}50{,}000 - 40{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= ₹1{,}10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **book value of ₹73,000 is COMPLETELY IGNORED** in this calculation. Why? Because whether we keep the machine or sell it, the ₹1,00,000 original cost is already gone. The only *real* cash we can capture today is the **market value of ₹40,000**.

### Step 4 — Compute the Present Value of Savings (10 years, 10%)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(10\%, 10) &= \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10} = 6.1446 \\[4pt]
\text{PV of Annual Savings} &= 20{,}000 \times 6.1446 = ₹1{,}22{,}892
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Incremental Scrap Value at Year 10

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Incremental Scrap at Year 10} &= (\text{New Scrap}) - (\text{Old Scrap}) \\[4pt]
&= 15{,}000 - 10{,}000 = ₹5{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{PV of Incremental Scrap} &= \frac{5{,}000}{(1.10)^{10}} = \frac{5{,}000}{2.5937} = ₹1{,}928
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Net Present Value of the Replacement Decision

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{\text{replacement}} &= \text{PV of Savings} + \text{PV of Scrap} - \text{Net Initial Outlay} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}22{,}892 + 1{,}928 - 1{,}10{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= ₹14{,}820
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Decision

Since $\text{NPV} = +₹14{,}820 > 0$, **replace the machine**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often subtract the *book value* (₹73,000) instead of the *market value* (₹40,000), or add back depreciation as a "cash inflow." Both are wrong. Only **real, future, incremental cash flows** count.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Continue or Abandon a Failing Project

**Problem Statement:**
A firm invested ₹5,00,000 two years ago in a product that has not succeeded. To complete and launch the product, an **additional ₹3,00,000** must be spent. Expected revenue next year is ₹4,50,000 and operating cost is ₹1,00,000. The firm can abandon the project today and salvage ₹50,000 by selling the semi-finished inventory. What should the firm do? (Ignore discounting for simplicity.)

### Analytical Matrix

| Decision Option | Cash Inflow (₹) | Cash Outflow (₹) | Net Cash (₹) | Sunk Cost Included? |
|-----------------|------------------|--------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| Continue | 4,50,000 | 1,00,000 + 3,00,000 = 4,00,000 | **+50,000** | ❌ No (₹5,00,000 ignored) |
| Abandon | 50,000 (salvage) | 0 | **+50,000** | ❌ No (₹5,00,000 ignored) |

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Continue Net Benefit} &= \text{Revenue} - \text{Operating Cost} - \text{Additional Investment} \\[4pt]
&= 4{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= +₹50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Abandon Net Benefit} &= \text{Salvage Value of Inventory} = +₹50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Decision

Both options yield ₹50,000. **Indifferent.** The original ₹5,00,000 is **sunk** and irrelevant. If the firm expected revenue to fall below ₹4,00,000 next year, it would prefer to abandon. The KTU examiner expects you to **explicitly state** that the ₹5,00,000 is ignored.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Template for KTU Answers)

For any "should I do X or Y?" problem, follow this matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{List all future cash flows for Option A and Option B.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compute Incremental Cash Flow} = \text{CF}_A - \text{CF}_B. \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Identify and EXCLUDE sunk costs (past, irrecoverable).} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Include opportunity costs (best forgone alternative).} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Discount if multi-period: } \text{NPV} = \sum \frac{\text{NCF}_t}{(1+r)^t}. \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Choose option with } \max(\text{NPV}) \text{ or } \max(\text{Net Cash Benefit}).
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always write the line:
> *"The past expenditure of ₹X is a sunk cost and is therefore irrelevant to the present decision."*
> Examiners award marks for this **explicit statement**, not just the arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Cost Classification for Decision-Making

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Total Cost of the Firm]) --> Q1{Is the cost already incurred?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Can it be recovered or sold?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Future Cost}
    Q2 -- No --> SunkA[Sunk Cost IGNORE in decision]
    Q2 -- Yes --> SunkB[Recoverable Past Cost - Treat as Relevant Inflow]
    Q3 --> Q4{Avoidable by rejecting project?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Av[Avoidable / Discretionary Cost - RELEVANT]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Committed Contractually?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Com[Committed Fixed Cost - IGNORE in short run]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Implied forgone benefit?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Opp[Opportunity Cost - RELEVANT]
    Q6 -- No --> Oth[Other Future Cost - Evaluate individually]
    SunkA --> Final([Relevant Cost Set: Avoidable + Opportunity + Recoverable])
    SunkB --> Final
    Av --> Final
    Opp --> Final
    Com --> FinalNote[Committed cost ignored here]
    Oth --> Final
    Com --> FinalNote
    FinalNote --> Final
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Replace vs. Retain Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Old Machine Purchased in Past] --> B{Is Cost Recoverable?}
    B -- No, Fully Sunk --> X[Depreciation and Book Value IGNORED]
    B -- Yes, Market Value Exists --> Y[Use Market Value as Opportunity Cost]
    X --> Decision[Compute Incremental NPV of Replacement]
    Y --> Decision
    Decision --> P{NPV greater than 0?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[REPLACE the Machine]
    P -- No --> R[RETAIN the Old Machine]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Sunk Cost vs. Relevant Cost Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PastPeriod[PAST - Irrelevant Zone]
        P1[Original Purchase Price]
        P2[Past R and D Spend]
        P3[Past Advertising Cost]
        P4[Historical Depreciation]
    end
    subgraph FuturePeriod[FUTURE - Relevant Zone]
        F1[New Investment Required]
        F2[Future Operating Costs]
        F3[Future Salvage Value]
        F4[Opportunity Cost of Capital]
    end
    PastPeriod --> Boundary{Sunk Cost Boundary}
    FuturePeriod --> Boundary
    Boundary --> Out[Decision: NPV of Future Flows Only]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Students:** The diagram visually partitions all costs into "past" and "future" buckets. Only the future bucket feeds the decision — the past bucket is a **frozen historical fact** that no present action can alter.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*"What is a sunk cost? Explain with two examples why it should be ignored in decision-making."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A sunk cost is a cost that has already been incurred in the past and cannot be recovered by any future action; it is therefore irrelevant to current decision-making.
- **Example 1 (1 mark):** Depreciation on a previously purchased machine is a sunk, non-cash allocation and should be ignored in a replace-vs-retain decision.
- **Example 2 (1 mark):** Past R&D expenditure on a product prototype that did not work should be ignored when deciding whether to continue the project; only the additional future investment matters.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*"Distinguish between sunk cost and opportunity cost."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Sunk Cost | Opportunity Cost |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| Timing | Past | Future (implicit) |
| Recoverable? | No | N/A (it is forgone benefit) |
| Relevant? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Example | Past advertising expense | Rent foregone on own building |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Replace vs. Retain Decision

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

A company bought a machine 4 years ago for ₹2,00,000. Its estimated life is 10 years with a scrap value of ₹20,000 (straight-line depreciation). The present market value of the machine is ₹60,000. A new automatic machine is available for ₹3,00,000 with a 10-year life and a scrap value of ₹30,000. The new machine will save ₹40,000 per year in labour and material costs. The cost of capital is 12%. Should the company replace the old machine?

**Sub-parts:**
**(a)** Identify the sunk cost in the problem. Why is it irrelevant? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the NPV of replacement and advise. **(7 marks)**

#### (a) Model Solution — Identifying Sunk Cost (7 Marks)

**Step 1: List all costs and tag them.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original Cost} &= ₹2{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(Sunk — Past)} \\[4pt]
\text{Annual Depreciation} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000}{10} = ₹18{,}000/\text{year} \\[4pt]
\text{Accumulated Depreciation (4 years)} &= 18{,}000 \times 4 = ₹72{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Book Value today} &= 2{,}00{,}000 - 72{,}000 = ₹1{,}28{,}000 \quad \text{(Sunk Accounting Figure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Explain the irrelevance (3 marks of writing):**
- The original cost of ₹2,00,000 was paid 4 years ago; no future decision can recover it.
- Depreciation is a non-cash accounting charge, not an actual cash outflow.
- The book value of ₹1,28,000 is a historical figure; only the **market value of ₹60,000** represents realisable cash if the machine is sold.
- Therefore, the only relevant inflow from the old machine is the **₹60,000 salvage opportunity cost**, and the ₹1,28,000 book value must be **ignored**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Marks are lost when students:
> 1. Include the book value (₹1,28,000) in the initial investment calculation — **WRONG**.
> 2. Treat depreciation as a yearly cash saving — **WRONG** (it is non-cash).
> 3. Forget to subtract the current market value of the old machine — **WRONG**.

#### (b) Model Solution — NPV of Replacement (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Net Initial Investment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Initial Outlay} &= \text{Cost of New} - \text{Sale of Old} \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000 = ₹2{,}40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Present Value of Annual Savings (10 years, 12%)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(12\%, 10) &= \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-10}}{0.12} = 5.6502 \\[4pt]
\text{PV of Savings} &= 40{,}000 \times 5.6502 = ₹2{,}26{,}008
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Incremental Scrap Value at Year 10**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Incremental Scrap} &= 30{,}000 - 20{,}000 = ₹10{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{PV of Scrap} &= \frac{10{,}000}{(1.12)^{10}} = \frac{10{,}000}{3.1058} = ₹3{,}220
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: NPV Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{\text{replacement}} &= \text{PV of Savings} + \text{PV of Scrap} - \text{Net Initial Outlay} \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}26{,}008 + 3{,}220 - 2{,}40{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= -₹10{,}772
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $\text{NPV} = -₹10{,}772 < 0$, **the company should NOT replace the machine** and should retain the old one.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Continue vs. Abandon

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

A pharmaceutical firm has spent ₹8,00,000 over the last two years developing a new drug. Market response is poor. An additional ₹5,00,000 investment is needed for clinical trials. Expected revenue in year 1 after launch is ₹7,00,000 with variable cost of ₹3,00,000. If abandoned now, the firm can recover ₹2,00,000 by selling the patent application and inventory. The cost of capital is 10%. Advise the firm.

**Sub-parts:**
**(a)** Identify the sunk and relevant costs. Compute the NPV of continuing. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the NPV of abandoning. Compare and advise. **(7 marks)**

#### (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sunk Cost} &= ₹8{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(Past R&D, IGNORED)} \\[4pt]
\text{Relevant Initial Outlay} &= ₹5{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Net Cash Flow Year 1} &= \text{Revenue} - \text{Variable Cost} = 7{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{PV of Year 1 CF} &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.10} = ₹3{,}63{,}636 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV}_{\text{continue}} &= 3{,}63{,}636 - 5{,}00{,}000 = -₹1{,}36{,}364
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sunk Cost Identification (within the 7 marks):**
- ₹8,00,000 past R&D is sunk — it cannot be recovered whether the firm continues or abandons.
- It must therefore be **excluded** from the decision calculation.

#### (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{\text{abandon}} &= \text{Salvage Value Today} - \text{Future Investment} \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}00{,}000 - 0 = +₹2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison and Decision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{\text{continue}} &= -₹1{,}36{,}364 \\[4pt]
\text{NPV}_{\text{abandon}} &= +₹2{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Difference} &= 2{,}00{,}000 - (-1{,}36{,}364) = +₹3{,}36{,}364 \text{ in favour of abandoning}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** The firm should **abandon** the project. The ₹8,00,000 is a sunk cost and must not influence the decision; rational economic analysis shows continuing causes a real loss of ₹1,36,364, while abandoning yields a positive ₹2,00,000.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Continue-vs-Abandon Problems):**
> 1. Treating the past ₹8,00,000 as a "reason to continue" is the **Sunk Cost Fallacy** — full marks deducted.
> 2. Forgetting to discount the year-1 cash flow at the cost of capital.
> 3. Adding depreciation on a project that has not yet created a fixed asset — irrelevant.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Sunk cost = past, irrecoverable, irrelevant to current decision.
- ✅ **Golden Rule:** *Past expenditure, no matter how large, must NEVER influence future decisions.*
- ✅ **Depreciation is NOT a cash outflow** — exclude it from incremental cash-flow analysis.
- ✅ **Use Market Value, not Book Value**, when computing the opportunity cost of an old asset.
- ✅ **Sunk Cost Fallacy (Concorde Fallacy):** Continuing a failing project because of past investment = irrational.
- ✅ **Always state explicitly** in KTU answers: *"This is a sunk cost and therefore ignored in the decision."*
- ✅ **Three Decision-Relevant Costs:** Avoidable + Opportunity + Incremental Revenue.
- ✅ **Two Decision-Irrelevant Costs:** Sunk + Committed Fixed (in short run).
- ✅ **Replace-vs-Retain formula:** $\text{Net Outlay} = \text{New Cost} - \text{Market Value of Old}$.
- ✅ **Continue-vs-Abandon rule:** Compare $\max(\text{NPV}_{\text{continue}}, \text{NPV}_{\text{abandon}})$ based on *future* flows only.
- ✅ **Engineering Applications:** Lean manufacturing, project gate reviews, software sprint termination, demolition of sub-standard structures.
- ✅ **Behavioural Pitfall:** Escalation of commitment is a *psychological* trap, not an economic one — recognize and avoid it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Opportunity Cost, Explicit and Implicit Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Opportunity Cost, Explicit and Implicit Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Opportunity Cost — The Real Cost of Every Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Opportunity Cost** is the value of the **next best alternative foregone** (sacrificed) when a choice is made between two or more mutually exclusive alternatives. It represents the benefits that *could have been obtained* from the option that was *not* chosen.

Opportunity cost is the cornerstone concept of managerial and engineering economics. Unlike the cost shown in an invoice or paid in cash, opportunity cost is a **conceptual cost** — it measures what you *give up* in order to pursue a particular course of action.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are a final-year B.Tech student with two offers in hand:
- **Offer A:** Software Engineer role at ₹6,00,000 per annum.
- **Offer B:** Higher studies (M.Tech) at a premier institute with a stipend of ₹3,00,000 per annum.

If you *choose* Offer B (M.Tech), then your **Opportunity Cost of choosing M.Tech is ₹6,00,000 per year** — because that is the salary you have foregone by not accepting the software job. The cash you actually receive (₹3,00,000) is your accounting earning, but the *true economic cost* of your decision is ₹6,00,000.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Opportunity cost is always measured in terms of the **second-best (next best) alternative**, not just *any* alternative. Students often lose marks by mentioning "the cost of the best alternative" — always clarify it is the **next best** forgone opportunity.

---

## 1.2 Explicit Cost — The Out-of-Pocket Accounting Cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Explicit Cost** refers to the **actual monetary payments (out-of-pocket expenses)** made by a firm to other parties (factors of production owned by outsiders) in exchange for goods and services used in the production process.

These are the costs recorded in the firm's **books of accounts** and form the basis of **Accounting Profit**. They involve a clear, verifiable cash transaction.

**Examples of Explicit Costs in an Engineering Firm:**
- Wages and salaries paid to employees and engineers.
- Rent paid for office/factory premises.
- Interest paid on borrowed capital (loans, debentures).
- Cost of raw materials, components, and consumables.
- Utility bills (electricity, water, internet).
- Insurance premiums, taxes, and depreciation on assets.
- Advertising and marketing expenditure.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of explicit costs as everything you would enter into a **Tally/ERP software invoice** when running an engineering start-up. Every rupee that *leaves* your bank account to pay someone else is an explicit cost.

---

## 1.3 Implicit Cost — The Hidden Cost of Self-Owned Resources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Implicit Cost** refers to the **opportunity cost of using self-owned and self-employed resources** in the production process. These costs do **not** involve any direct monetary payment and are **not recorded in the books of accounts**.

Implicit costs represent the **notional earnings** the owner sacrifices by using their own resources for a particular venture instead of deploying them elsewhere.

**Examples of Implicit Costs:**
- The salary the proprietor *could have earned* by working elsewhere.
- The rent *not earned* on a building owned and used by the firm itself.
- The interest *foregone* on the owner's own capital invested in the firm.
- The depreciation on owner-utilised machinery (notional depreciation).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a B.Tech graduate starts a tech-venture using **their own savings (₹5,00,000)** as seed capital and works full-time in it. The ₹50,000 they *could have earned* as an employee elsewhere, and the ₹40,000 interest that money *would have earned* in a bank FD — these are implicit costs. No cash leaves their pocket, but an economist still counts them as a cost of doing business.

---

## 1.4 The Triad Visualized — Relationship Overview

| Cost Type | Involves Cash? | Recorded in Books? | Form of Payment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Explicit Cost** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Monetary |
| **Implicit Cost** | ❌ No | ❌ No | Notional / Foregone |
| **Opportunity Cost** | ❌ (Usually implicit) | ❌ | Value of next best alternative |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — Why These Costs Matter

### 2.1.1 The Logic of Opportunity Cost
1. **Scarcity of Resources:** Resources (time, capital, labour) are always limited.
2. **Multiple Alternative Uses:** Every resource has competing uses.
3. **Forced Choice:** Choosing one use automatically rejects others.
4. **Valuation of Sacrifice:** The benefit of the rejected alternative becomes the opportunity cost.
5. **Decision-Making Tool:** Rational decision-makers compare *expected returns* against opportunity costs.

### 2.1.2 Explicit vs. Implicit — The Two Worlds of Cost
- **Explicit Costs** belong to the **domain of accounting** — they are objective, verifiable, and auditable.
- **Implicit Costs** belong to the **domain of economics** — they are subjective, notional, and require estimation.
- Together they form **Economic Cost**, which is what truly matters for **long-run managerial decisions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Engineering students often confuse *economic cost* with *accounting cost*. Accounting cost = Explicit cost only. Economic cost = Explicit + Implicit costs. This distinction is the single most-tested concept in Module 2.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TC_{Econ} = TC_{Explicit} + TC_{Implicit}$ | Total Economic Cost | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | $Profit_{Acct} = TR - TC_{Explicit}$ | Accounting Profit | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 3 | $Profit_{Econ} = TR - TC_{Econ}$ | Economic Profit | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 4 | $Profit_{Econ} = Profit_{Acct} - TC_{Implicit}$ | Economic Profit from Accounting Profit | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 5 | $OC = \text{Return on Next Best Alternative}$ | Opportunity Cost Definition | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 6 | $OC = \text{Forgone Earnings}$ | Opportunity Cost as Foregone Benefit | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 7 | $Implicit\ Cost = \text{Normal\ Return\ on\ Self-Owned\ Resources}$ | Implicit Cost Notion | ₹ (Rupees) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** In **perfect competition long-run equilibrium**, Economic Profit = 0 (but Accounting Profit > 0). This is because firms only earn their implicit costs as part of accounting profit in the long run.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Capital Budgeting:** When an engineering firm invests in new machinery, the opportunity cost is the return it *could have earned* by investing in government bonds or a competitor's stock.
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** The implicit cost of using in-house machinery must be compared against the explicit cost of outsourcing.
- **R&D Project Selection:** Choosing Project A over Project B means the opportunity cost is the NPV of Project B.
- **Personal Finance:** An engineer working overtime gives up leisure time — the value of leisure is the opportunity cost.
- **Government Policy:** Opportunity cost of funds spent on a metro rail project is the schools/hospitals that could not be funded with the same budget.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Economic Cost from Explicit and Implicit Components

### Problem Context
Let us mathematically derive the relationship between economic cost, accounting cost (explicit cost), and implicit cost for a generic firm.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the **Total Cost (TC)** that a firm actually incurs. This consists of two mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive parts.

$$
TC_{Total} = \text{Payments to Outsiders} + \text{Notional Payments to Self}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the formal terms. Payments to outsiders are **Explicit Costs**. Notional payments to self are **Implicit Costs**.

$$
TC_{Total} = TC_{Explicit} + TC_{Implicit}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the **total cost from an economist's perspective** is the **Economic Cost**, while the total cost from an accountant's perspective is only the **Explicit Cost**.

$$
TC_{Econ} = TC_{Acct} + TC_{Implicit}
$$

**Step 4 (Conclusion):** Hence, Economic Cost always exceeds Accounting Cost by the magnitude of Implicit Cost.

$$
\boxed{TC_{Econ} = TC_{Acct} + TC_{Implicit}}
$$

This identity is the foundation of all managerial economics problems in KTU.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Accounting Profit and Economic Profit

### Problem Context
Derive the algebraic relation between accounting profit and economic profit given total revenue (TR).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with the **Accounting Profit** definition. Accountants deduct only explicit costs from total revenue.

$$
\Pi_{Acct} = TR - TC_{Explicit}
$$

**Step 2:** Start with the **Economic Profit** definition. Economists deduct the *full* economic cost (explicit + implicit) from total revenue.

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = TR - TC_{Econ}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $TC_{Econ} = TC_{Explicit} + TC_{Implicit}$ into the economic profit equation.

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = TR - (TC_{Explicit} + TC_{Implicit})
$$

**Step 4:** Distribute the negative sign.

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = TR - TC_{Explicit} - TC_{Implicit}
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $TR - TC_{Explicit}$ is exactly $\Pi_{Acct}$ from Step 1.

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = \Pi_{Acct} - TC_{Implicit}
$$

**Step 6 (Conclusion):** Therefore, the relationship is:

$$
\boxed{\Pi_{Econ} = \Pi_{Acct} - TC_{Implicit}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Economic Profit is always **less than or equal to** Accounting Profit. The gap between the two is exactly equal to the total Implicit Cost of self-owned resources.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

### Problem Statement
A B.Tech graduate quits a software job paying ₹8,00,000 per year to start a robotics firm. He invests ₹10,00,000 of his own savings (which would have earned 8% interest in a bank). In the first year:
- He pays ₹3,00,000 as salary to one hired engineer.
- He spends ₹1,50,000 on raw materials and rent.
- His total revenue from sales is ₹7,00,000.

**Calculate:** (i) Explicit Cost, (ii) Implicit Cost, (iii) Total Economic Cost, (iv) Accounting Profit, (v) Economic Profit.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Identify Explicit Costs**

Explicit costs are the actual out-of-pocket payments.
- Salary to engineer = ₹3,00,000
- Raw materials and rent = ₹1,50,000

$$
TC_{Explicit} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Identify Implicit Costs**

Implicit costs are the foregone earnings on self-owned resources.
- Foregone salary from software job = ₹8,00,000
- Foregone interest on own capital = 8% of ₹10,00,000 = ₹80,000

$$
TC_{Implicit} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 = \text{₹ } 8{,}80{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Economic Cost**

$$
TC_{Econ} = TC_{Explicit} + TC_{Implicit}
$$

$$
TC_{Econ} = 4{,}50{,}000 + 8{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹ } 13{,}30{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Accounting Profit**

$$
\Pi_{Acct} = TR - TC_{Explicit}
$$

$$
\Pi_{Acct} = 7{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Economic Profit**

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = TR - TC_{Econ}
$$

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = 7{,}00{,}000 - 13{,}30{,}000 = -\text{₹ } 6{,}30{,}000
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** Although the graduate has a positive **Accounting Profit** of ₹2,50,000, his **Economic Profit is negative ₹6,30,000**. From an economist's perspective, he is actually destroying value by running his own firm. He would be financially better off by returning to his software job and parking his savings in the bank.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Cost

| Decision Scenario | Cost to Use | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Filing income tax returns | Explicit Cost | Tax laws only recognise monetary transactions |
| Computing firm valuation for M&A | Economic Cost | Buyer cares about opportunity cost of capital |
| Bank loan application | Explicit Cost | Bank assesses verifiable cash flows |
| Long-run equilibrium of a firm | Economic Cost | Firms earn normal profit (zero economic profit) |
| Short-run shutdown decision | Explicit Cost | Only out-of-pocket costs matter |
| Choosing between R&D projects | Opportunity Cost | Value of the next best alternative project |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Total Cost Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TOTAL COST OF FIRM"] --> B["EXPLICIT COST"]
    A --> C["IMPLICIT COST"]
    B --> B1["Wages and Salaries"]
    B --> B2["Rent Paid"]
    B --> B3["Interest on Borrowed Capital"]
    B --> B4["Raw Materials and Utilities"]

    C --> C2["Foregone Interest on Own Capital"]
    C --> C3["Foregone Rent on Own Building"]
    C --> C4["Notional Depreciation on Own Assets"]
    A --> D["ECONOMIC COST = EXPLICIT + IMPLICIT"]
    D --> E["ACCOUNTING PROFIT = TR minus EXPLICIT"]
    D --> F["ECONOMIC PROFIT = TR minus ECONOMIC COST"]
```

## 4.2 Process Diagram — Opportunity Cost Decision Logic

```mermaid

    P1["Decision Maker Faces Multiple Alternatives"] --> P2["Identify All Available Options"]
    P2 --> P3["Determine the Best Option"]
    P2 --> P4["Determine the Next Best Option"]
    P3 --> P5["Choose the Best Option"]
    P4 --> P6["Opportunity Cost = Value of Next Best Option Forgone"]
    P5 --> P7["Compare Benefit of Chosen Option vs Opportunity Cost"]
    P7 --> P8["Proceed if Benefit Exceeds Opportunity Cost"]
```

```mermaid
    R --> EP["ECONOMIC PROFIT"]

    EC["ECONOMIC COST = EXPLICIT PLUS IMPLICIT"] --> EP
    AP -.->|"GAP = IMPLICIT COST"| EP
```
