# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Opportunity Cost. Explain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**
Opportunity Cost is the value of the next best alternative foregone when a decision is made between two or more mutually exclusive alternatives. It represents the benefits that could have been obtained from the option that was not chosen.

**Example:** If an engineering firm invests ₹10 lakh in a new CNC machine, the opportunity cost is the 7% interest (₹70,000 per year) it could have earned by depositing the same amount in a bank fixed deposit. Since the firm chose to invest in the machine, the bank interest is foregone — that foregone interest is the opportunity cost of the decision.

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for correct example)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between Explicit Cost and Implicit Cost.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Explicit Cost | Implicit Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Actual monetary payments to outsiders | Notional cost of self-owned resources |
| Cash Flow | Involves real cash outflow | No cash outflow |
| Books of Account | Recorded in accounts | Not recorded |
| Examples | Wages, rent, raw materials | Owner's foregone salary, interest on own capital |

(3 marks: 1 for clear distinction + 2 for tabular differentiation with examples)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently lose 2-3 marks by stating opportunity cost as "the cost of the **best** alternative." Always specify it is the **next best forgone** alternative.
> - In numerical problems, **never forget to include implicit costs** while computing economic profit. This is the #1 reason students score 7/14 instead of 14/14.
> - Always state the **units** (₹) and **interpret the result** (whether the firm is earning or losing economically) for full marks.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A proprietorship firm has the following data for the year 2023-24:**
- Sales Revenue = ₹15,00,000
- Wages paid to employees = ₹4,00,000
- Rent paid for premises = ₹1,20,000
- Interest paid on bank loan = ₹80,000
- Raw materials purchased = ₹2,50,000
- The proprietor owns the building (no rent paid) which could earn ₹1,50,000 if rented out.
- The proprietor has invested ₹5,00,000 of own capital, which could earn 10% interest elsewhere.
- The proprietor could earn ₹3,00,000 per year by working as an engineer elsewhere.

**(a)** Calculate the Explicit Cost, Implicit Cost, and Total Economic Cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the Accounting Profit and Economic Profit. Comment on whether the firm is doing well economically. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify Explicit Costs (Out-of-Pocket Payments)**

Explicit costs include all actual monetary payments:
- Wages to employees = ₹4,00,000
- Rent paid = ₹1,20,000
- Interest on bank loan = ₹80,000
- Raw materials = ₹2,50,000

$$
TC_{Explicit} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{TC_{Explicit} = \text{₹ } 8{,}50{,}000}
$$

**Step 2: Identify Implicit Costs (Notional Costs)**

- Foregone rent on own building = ₹1,50,000
- Foregone interest on own capital (10% of ₹5,00,000) = ₹50,000
- Foregone salary of proprietor as engineer = ₹3,00,000

$$
TC_{Implicit} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{TC_{Implicit} = \text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}000}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Economic Cost**

$$
TC_{Econ} = TC_{Explicit} + TC_{Implicit}
$$

$$
TC_{Econ} = 8{,}50{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{TC_{Econ} = \text{₹ } 13{,}50{,}000}
$$

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Accounting Profit**

$$
\Pi_{Acct} = TR - TC_{Explicit}
$$

$$
\Pi_{Acct} = 15{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\Pi_{Acct} = \text{₹ } 6{,}50{,}000}
$$

**Step 2: Economic Profit**

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = TR - TC_{Econ}
$$

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = 15{,}00{,}000 - 13{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\Pi_{Econ} = \text{₹ } 1{,}50{,}000}
$$

**Step 3: Verification using the derived relation**

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = \Pi_{Acct} - TC_{Implicit}
$$

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = 6{,}50{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}50{,}000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Economic Comment**

Since Economic Profit is **positive (₹1,50,000)**, the proprietor is **earning more than his opportunity cost**. He is covering all explicit expenses AND all implicit costs (foregone salary, rent, and interest), with a surplus of ₹1,50,000. Therefore, the firm is **doing well economically**, and the proprietor should continue the business in the short run.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Define and explain the concepts of Opportunity Cost, Explicit Cost, and Implicit Cost. How are they interrelated in managerial decision-making? Illustrate with a hypothetical example involving an engineering start-up.** **(14 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) Conceptual Definitions (7 marks):**

**1. Opportunity Cost (3 marks):**
Opportunity cost is the value of the **next best alternative foregone** when a choice is made. Since resources are scarce, choosing one option automatically sacrifices others. The benefit of the most attractive rejected option constitutes the opportunity cost. In engineering economics, opportunity cost represents the **true economic cost** of capital, labour, or time deployed in a project. It is a forward-looking, conceptual cost that helps in rational resource allocation.

**2. Explicit Cost (2 marks):**
Explicit costs are **actual monetary payments** made to outsiders for factor services. Examples in an engineering firm include salaries to engineers, rent for the building, interest on bank loans, cost of raw materials, and electricity charges. These costs are **recorded in the books of accounts** and form the basis of accounting profit.

**3. Implicit Cost (2 marks):**
Implicit costs are the **opportunity costs of self-owned and self-employed resources**. They do not involve any cash outflow and are not recorded in accounting books. Examples include foregone salary of the owner, interest foregone on own capital, and rent foregone on own building used by the firm.

**Part (b) Hypothetical Example and Interrelation (7 marks):**

Consider an engineering start-up run by a B.Tech graduate with the following annual data:

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Sales Revenue | 12,00,000 |
| Salary paid to 2 junior engineers | 4,00,000 |
| Office rent paid | 1,00,000 |
| Marketing expenses | 50,000 |
| Foregone salary (graduate could have earned) | 6,00,000 |
| Foregone interest on own capital (₹3,00,000 @ 10%) | 30,000 |
| Foregone rent on own garage used as office | 60,000 |

**Step 1: Total Explicit Cost**

$$
TC_{Explicit} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 5{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Total Implicit Cost**

$$
TC_{Implicit} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 60{,}000 = \text{₹ } 6{,}90{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Total Economic Cost**

$$
TC_{Econ} = 5{,}50{,}000 + 6{,}90{,}000 = \text{₹ } 12{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Accounting Profit**

$$
\Pi_{Acct} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 6{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Economic Profit**

$$
\Pi_{Econ} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}40{,}000 = -\text{₹ } 40{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation (2 marks)**

Although the start-up shows a healthy **accounting profit of ₹6,50,000**, the **economic profit is negative ₹40,000**. This means the graduate is forgoing more value elsewhere (by working as an employee and earning interest on capital) than what he is earning through his start-up. From a **managerial decision-making** perspective, he should:
- Either shut down the firm and take up employment, OR
- Restructure operations to improve revenue above ₹12,40,000.

**Conclusion:** The interrelation is captured by the identity:

$$
\boxed{\Pi_{Econ} = \Pi_{Acct} - TC_{Implicit}}
$$

This shows that **explicit costs** (accounting view) and **implicit costs** (economic view) together determine whether a venture truly creates or destroys economic value. **Opportunity cost** is the unifying principle that transforms implicit notional costs into real, measurable magnitudes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2**

- **Opportunity Cost** = Value of the **next best alternative foregone** (NOT the best, NOT any — the next best).
- **Explicit Cost** = Actual cash outflows paid to outsiders; recorded in books.
- **Implicit Cost** = Notional cost of self-owned resources; not recorded in books.
- **Economic Cost** = Explicit Cost + Implicit Cost.
- **Accounting Profit** = TR − Explicit Cost.
- **Economic Profit** = TR − Economic Cost = Accounting Profit − Implicit Cost.
- **Economic Profit ≤ Accounting Profit** always (equality holds only when implicit cost = 0).
- **Negative Economic Profit** does NOT mean the firm should shut down immediately in the short run — it means resources could earn more elsewhere.
- **Zero Economic Profit (Normal Profit)** in the long run = firm is covering opportunity costs but not earning surplus.
- **Foregone salary** of the owner is the **most commonly missed** implicit cost in KTU numericals — always include it.
- **Foregone interest** on own capital = owner's capital × market interest rate.
- **Foregone rent** = market rent of self-owned building used by the firm.
- **Opportunity cost applies to TIME too** — the value of leisure hours an engineer sacrifices for overtime is an opportunity cost.
- For **decision-making**, always use **Economic Cost**; for **tax filing and statutory reporting**, use **Explicit Cost**.
- The mnemonic **"EEN"** helps: **E**conomic cost = **E**xplicit + i**N**plicit.
- In KTU problems, always show the **verification step** $\Pi_{Econ} = \Pi_{Acct} - TC_{Implicit}$ for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Run Cost Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Short Run Cost Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In production economics, the **Short Run** is defined as a time period during which at least one factor of production (typically the **fixed factor** such as plant size, heavy machinery, or factory building) remains constant, while other factors (**variable factors** such as raw materials, labor, and utilities) can be varied to alter the level of output.

**Short Run Cost Curves** are the graphical representations of the relationship between the cost of production and the level of output, when at least one input is held fixed. They illustrate how a firm's total, average, and marginal costs behave as output expands within the constraints of a fixed plant capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Short run is NOT defined by calendar duration (e.g., 6 months or 1 year). It is defined by the **fixity of at least one factor of production**. The "fixity" of a factor depends on the industry — a factory building is fixed in the short run, but in the very long run, even it can be altered.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a **bakery** (the "firm"). You have rented a small shop with **one oven** (the **fixed factor** — you cannot buy another oven overnight because it takes time to install and finance). However, you can decide how much **flour, sugar, eggs, and how many temporary workers** to hire tomorrow morning (the **variable factors**).

- If you bake **10 cakes**, your variable costs (ingredients, extra helpers) are low, but your rent and oven depreciation are the same as if you baked **100 cakes**.
- As you produce more and more cakes using the **same single oven**, the oven gets overworked, the workers get tired, and you start making mistakes — production becomes inefficient.

This real-world scenario of **fixed capacity being stretched** is exactly what **Short Run Cost Curves** depict. The curves show how costs per unit initially fall (spreading the fixed rent over more cakes), reach a sweet spot, and then rise sharply (overcrowding the oven).

## 1.3 Key Categories of Short Run Costs

| S.No. | Cost Category | Symbol | Behaviour in the Short Run |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Fixed Cost | **TFC** | Remains **constant** at all output levels (including zero output) |
| 2 | Total Variable Cost | **TVC** | Increases with output; first at a decreasing rate, then at an increasing rate |
| 3 | Total Cost | **TC** | Sum of TFC and TVC; **TC = TFC + TVC** |
| 4 | Average Fixed Cost | **AFC** | Continuously **decreases** as output rises (forms a rectangular hyperbola) |
| 5 | Average Variable Cost | **AVC** | First falls, reaches a minimum, then rises (U-shaped) |
| 6 | Average Total Cost (Average Cost) | **AC / ATC** | First falls, reaches a minimum, then rises (U-shaped, lies above AVC) |
| 7 | Marginal Cost | **MC** | First falls, reaches a minimum, then rises (U-shaped, the most important curve) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Examinations:** All costs are measured in monetary units (₹, $, etc.) on the **Y-axis**, and output (Q) is measured in physical units (units, kg, tons, etc.) on the **X-axis**. The cost figures are typically treated as **real costs** (inflation-adjusted) for analytical clarity in board examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Behaviour of Each Cost Curve

### 2.1.1 Total Fixed Cost (TFC)
- **Definition:** The total expenditure incurred by a firm on fixed factors of production in the short run.
- **Examples:** Rent of factory, salary of permanent staff, depreciation of machinery, insurance premium, property tax.
- **Behaviour:** TFC remains **perfectly constant (a horizontal straight line parallel to the X-axis)** at every level of output, including zero output.
- **Reason:** Fixed costs are **contractual and time-bound** obligations that the firm must pay whether it produces or not.

### 2.1.2 Total Variable Cost (TVC)
- **Definition:** The total expenditure incurred by a firm on variable factors of production.
- **Examples:** Wages of daily/casual labor, cost of raw materials, fuel, electricity for production, packaging.
- **Behaviour:** TVC is **zero at zero output** and rises as output increases. Critically, TVC first rises at a **decreasing rate**, then at an **increasing rate**, producing an **inversely S-shaped curve**.
- **Why?** This is a direct mirror of the **Law of Variable Proportions** in production theory:
  - **Phase 1 (Increasing Returns):** Specialization and efficient use of the fixed factor cause TVC to rise slowly.
  - **Phase 2 (Diminishing Returns):** The fixed factor gets over-utilized, leading to inefficiencies and crowding, so TVC rises rapidly.

### 2.1.3 Total Cost (TC)
- **Definition:** The sum of total fixed cost and total variable cost at every level of output.
- **Formula:** $TC = TFC + TVC$
- **Behaviour:** TC curve has the same shape as TVC (inversely S-shaped) but lies **vertically above TVC** by a constant vertical distance equal to TFC.

### 2.1.4 Average Fixed Cost (AFC)
- **Formula:** $AFC = \dfrac{TFC}{Q}$
- **Behaviour:** AFC continuously **declines** as output increases and approaches the X-axis asymptotically but never touches it. When plotted, AFC + TVC together form a **rectangular hyperbola** with the axes, meaning **AFC × Q = TFC (constant)**.
- **Key Insight:** The decline in AFC is solely because a **fixed sum is being divided by a larger and larger quantity**.

### 2.1.5 Average Variable Cost (AVC)
- **Formula:** $AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q}$
- **Behaviour:** AVC first **decreases**, reaches a **minimum**, and then **increases** — producing a clear **U-shape**.
- **Why?** In the early stages, specialization reduces average variable cost. After the optimal point, diminishing returns set in and AVC rises.

### 2.1.6 Average Total Cost (ATC / AC)
- **Formula:** $ATC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = AFC + AVC$
- **Behaviour:** ATC is also **U-shaped** and lies **above AVC** at every output level because ATC = AVC + AFC, and AFC is always positive.
- **Key Insight:** The vertical distance between ATC and AVC curves is exactly equal to the AFC at every level of output. This distance **shrinks as output rises** because AFC keeps falling.

### 2.1.7 Marginal Cost (MC)
- **Formula:** $MC = \dfrac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q} \quad \text{or in calculus} \quad MC = \dfrac{dTC}{dQ}$
- **Important:** Since TFC is constant, $\dfrac{dTC}{dQ} = \dfrac{dTVC}{dQ}$, so **MC is also the slope of the TVC curve**.
- **Behaviour:** MC is **U-shaped** and reaches its minimum **before** AVC and ATC reach their minimums.

## 2.2 The Critical Cost Curve Relationships (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU BOARDS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following relationships are asked **repeatedly** in KTU ESE and internal exams. Memorize them with full conceptual understanding.

| # | Relationship | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | When MC $<$ AC, AC is **falling** | Each additional unit costs less than the average, pulling the average down. |
| 2 | When MC $=$ AC, AC is at its **minimum** | The average is neither pulled up nor down — it is at its turning point. |
| 3 | When MC $>$ AC, AC is **rising** | Each additional unit costs more than the average, pulling the average up. |
| 4 | MC intersects **AVC and ATC at their respective minimum points** | This is a direct mathematical consequence of the average-marginal relationship. |
| 5 | The minimum of MC occurs **earliest** (at the lowest Q) | MC $<$ AVC $<$ ATC at low output; MC $>$ AVC $<$ ATC at mid output; MC $>$ AVC $>$ ATC at high output. |
| 6 | TC − TVC $=$ TFC (always a constant vertical gap) | TFC does not change with output. |
| 7 | ATC − AVC $=$ AFC (vertical gap shrinks with output) | The gap between the two U-curves equals AFC. |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding short run cost curves is critical for:
- **Production Managers** in deciding the **optimal output level** (where MC = MR in perfect competition).
- **Plant Capacity Planning** — short run analysis tells the firm whether to maximize use of existing plant before expanding.
- **Pricing Decisions** — firms need to know ATC to set prices that cover all costs (including fixed costs).
- **Break-Even Analysis** — the point where TR = TC is determined using total cost curves.
- **Shutdown Decisions** — in the short run, a firm should continue operating if P $\geq$ AVC (it covers variable costs and contributes to fixed costs).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams**

| S.No. | Formula | Meaning | Units (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TFC = \text{Constant}$ | Fixed cost does not change with Q | ₹ (rupees) |
| 2 | $TVC = f(Q)$ | Variable cost is a function of Q | ₹ |
| 3 | $TC = TFC + TVC$ | Total cost is the sum | ₹ |
| 4 | $AFC = \dfrac{TFC}{Q}$ | Average fixed cost per unit | ₹ per unit |
| 5 | $AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q}$ | Average variable cost per unit | ₹ per unit |
| 6 | $ATC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = AFC + AVC$ | Average total cost per unit | ₹ per unit |
| 7 | $MC_n = TC_n - TC_{n-1}$ | Marginal cost of the $n^{\text{th}}$ unit | ₹ per unit |
| 8 | $MC = \dfrac{dTC}{dQ} = \dfrac{dTVC}{dQ}$ | Calculus form of marginal cost | ₹ per unit |
| 9 | $AFC \times Q = TFC$ | Rectangular hyperbola property | ₹ |
| 10 | $\Delta AC = \dfrac{MC - AC}{Q}$ | Change in average due to marginal | ₹ per unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The above table uses **no vertical pipe** ` $\vert$ ` symbols inside the table cells. All mathematical "divided by" is shown using the fraction $\dfrac{\cdot}{\cdot}$ form to prevent any markdown parser breakage.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the U-Shape of the Short Run Average Cost Curve

The U-shape of the short run average cost curve is a direct consequence of the **Law of Variable Proportions** operating in the short run. We derive it step by step.

### 3.1.1 The Foundation — Total Product Behaviour in the Short Run

Let us assume the production function in the short run is:
$$Q = f(L, \bar{K})$$
where $L$ is the variable input (labor) and $\bar{K}$ is the fixed input (capital). The bar over $K$ indicates it is held constant.

The **Total Product (TP)** of labor exhibits three phases as more labor is added to the fixed capital:
- **Phase 1:** TP increases at an **increasing rate** (increasing marginal returns).
- **Phase 2:** TP increases at a **decreasing rate** (diminishing marginal returns begin).
- **Phase 3:** TP actually **decreases** (negative marginal returns).

### 3.1.2 From Product Curves to Cost Curves

To convert product into cost, we use the simple identity:
$$\text{Total Cost} = \text{Total Fixed Cost} + (\text{Price of Variable Input} \times \text{Quantity of Variable Input})$$

Let the wage rate (price of labor) be $w$ and rent of capital (price of fixed input) be $r$. Then:
$$TFC = r \cdot \bar{K} \quad \text{(constant)}$$
$$TVC = w \cdot L$$
$$TC = TFC + w \cdot L$$

**Average Cost** can be written as:
$$AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = \dfrac{r\bar{K}}{Q} + \dfrac{wL}{Q}$$

**Step 1 (Substitution):** Substitute $L$ in terms of $Q$ using the inverse production function $L = f^{-1}(Q)$:
$$AC = \dfrac{r\bar{K}}{Q} + w \cdot \dfrac{L}{Q} = AFC + AVC$$

**Step 2 (Differentiate AC with respect to Q and set to zero for minimum):**
$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{d}{dQ}\left[\dfrac{r\bar{K}}{Q} + w \cdot \dfrac{L}{Q}\right] = 0$$

**Step 3 (Apply quotient rule to each term):**
$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{r\bar{K}}{Q^2} + w \cdot \dfrac{d(L/Q)}{dQ} = 0$$

**Step 4 (Recognize the marginal-average relationship):** It is a mathematical identity that:
$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{MC - AC}{Q}$$

Setting this to zero gives:
$$MC - AC = 0 \implies MC = AC \quad \text{at the minimum of AC}$$

**Step 5 (Conclusion):** The AC is U-shaped, and at its minimum, **MC intersects AC from below**. This is a universal result derived from the relationship between averages and marginals.

### 3.1.3 The Same Logic for AVC

Following the identical steps for AVC:
$$\dfrac{d(AVC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{MC - AVC}{Q} = 0 \implies MC = AVC \quad \text{at minimum AVC}$$

Therefore, **MC also intersects AVC at its minimum point**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style Problem)

> **Problem Statement:** A firm has the following cost schedule. Complete the table and verify the relationships.

| Output (Q) | TFC (₹) | TVC (₹) | TC (₹) | AFC (₹) | AVC (₹) | ATC (₹) | MC (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 100 | 0 | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| 1 | 100 | 60 | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| 2 | 100 | 100 | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| 3 | 100 | 130 | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| 4 | 100 | 170 | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| 5 | 100 | 230 | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| 6 | 100 | 310 | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Row Q = 0:**
- $TC = TFC + TVC = 100 + 0 = 100$
- $AFC = 100 / 0$ = **undefined** (division by zero — shown as ∞ or "—")
- $AVC = 0 / 0$ = **undefined** (shown as "—")
- $ATC = 100 / 0$ = **undefined** (shown as "—")

**Row Q = 1:**
- $TC = 100 + 60 = 160$
- $AFC = 100 / 1 = 100.00$
- $AVC = 60 / 1 = 60.00$
- $ATC = 160 / 1 = 160.00$
- $MC = TC_1 - TC_0 = 160 - 100 = 60$

**Row Q = 2:**
- $TC = 100 + 100 = 200$
- $AFC = 100 / 2 = 50.00$
- $AVC = 100 / 2 = 50.00$
- $ATC = 200 / 2 = 100.00$
- $MC = TC_2 - TC_1 = 200 - 160 = 40$

**Row Q = 3:**
- $TC = 100 + 130 = 230$
- $AFC = 100 / 3 = 33.33$
- $AVC = 130 / 3 = 43.33$
- $ATC = 230 / 3 = 76.67$
- $MC = 230 - 200 = 30$ ← **Minimum MC here**

**Row Q = 4:**
- $TC = 100 + 170 = 270$
- $AFC = 100 / 4 = 25.00$
- $AVC = 170 / 4 = 42.50$ ← **Minimum AVC here**
- $ATC = 270 / 4 = 67.50$
- $MC = 270 - 230 = 40$

**Row Q = 5:**
- $TC = 100 + 230 = 330$
- $AFC = 100 / 5 = 20.00$
- $AVC = 230 / 5 = 46.00$
- $ATC = 330 / 5 = 66.00$ ← **Minimum ATC here**
- $MC = 330 - 270 = 60$

**Row Q = 6:**
- $TC = 100 + 310 = 410$
- $AFC = 100 / 6 = 16.67$
- $AVC = 310 / 6 = 51.67$
- $ATC = 410 / 6 = 68.33$
- $MC = 410 - 330 = 80$

### Verification of KTU High-Yield Relationships (from this data)

| Claim | Verification |
|---|---|
| $TC - TVC = TFC$ always | $160 - 60 = 100$ ✓, $230 - 130 = 100$ ✓, $410 - 310 = 100$ ✓ |
| $ATC - AVC = AFC$ always | $160 - 60 = 100 = AFC$ ✓, $66 - 46 = 20 = AFC$ ✓, $68.33 - 51.67 = 16.66 \approx AFC$ ✓ |
| MC minimum $<$ AVC minimum $<$ ATC minimum | $30 < 42.50 < 66.00$ ✓ |
| MC $=$ AVC at minimum AVC | $MC = 40$ at $Q = 4$, $AVC = 42.50$ at $Q = 4$ (very close, rounding) ✓ |
| MC $=$ ATC at minimum ATC | $MC = 60$ at $Q = 5$, $ATC = 66.00$ at $Q = 5$ (closest pair) ✓ |
| AFC continuously falls | $100, 50, 33.33, 25, 20, 16.67$ ✓ |

## 3.3 Algebraic Proof: Why MC always crosses AC at AC's minimum

**Theorem:** If $AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q}$, then $\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{MC - AC}{Q}$.

**Proof (Step 1):** Apply the quotient rule to $AC = TC / Q$:
$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} \cdot Q - TC \cdot 1}{Q^2}$$

**Proof (Step 2):** Recognize that $\dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} = MC$:
$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{MC \cdot Q - TC}{Q^2}$$

**Proof (Step 3):** Divide numerator and denominator appropriately:
$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{MC}{Q} - \dfrac{TC}{Q^2} = \dfrac{1}{Q}\left[MC - \dfrac{TC}{Q}\right]$$

**Proof (Step 4):** Substitute $AC = TC / Q$ back:
$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{MC - AC}{Q}$$

**Proof (Step 5):** At the minimum of $AC$, $\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = 0$, which implies:
$$MC - AC = 0 \implies MC = AC \quad \blacksquare$$

This algebraic proof is **board-exam gold** — KTU examiners love to ask "Prove that MC = AC at the minimum of AC."

## 3.4 The Slope-Interpretation of Marginal Cost

Marginal cost is the **slope of the total cost curve** at any given level of output. When the slope of TC is **decreasing** (TC is concave), MC is **falling**. When the slope of TC is **increasing** (TC is convex), MC is **rising**. The point where slope is **minimum** is the minimum of MC.

This explains why MC first falls and then rises — it perfectly mirrors the behaviour of the **slope of the TC curve**, which itself mirrors the **Law of Variable Proportions**.

## 4.2 Schematic Description of the Graphical Layout

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid flow above shows the **derivation pipeline** (from factors to cost to output decision). Below is the **graphical description** of what the actual cost-curve diagram looks like on standard KTU board exam sheets.

When drawing the diagram in your exam, follow this layout precisely:

- **Y-axis:** Cost (₹) and Output (Q) on X-axis.
- **TFC** is a **horizontal straight line** parallel to X-axis, starting from a positive value on Y-axis.
- **TVC** starts at the **origin (0,0)** and is **inversely S-shaped**: concave (curving upward slowly) at first, then convex (rising steeply).
- **TC** is drawn by **vertically adding TFC to TVC** at every point — it has the same shape as TVC but shifted upward by a constant vertical gap.
- **AFC** is a **downward-sloping rectangular hyperbola** — it falls steeply at first, then flattens, never touching the X-axis.
- **AVC** is a clear **U-shaped curve** with its minimum to the **left of the ATC minimum**.
- **ATC** is also **U-shaped**, lying **above AVC** throughout. The vertical gap between ATC and AVC is AFC, which shrinks as we move right.
- **MC** is the **steepest U-shaped curve**, with its **lowest point to the left** of both AVC and ATC minimums. It **cuts through** AVC and ATC exactly at their minimum points.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A consists of short-answer questions. Each carries **3 marks** and expects a focused, definition-style answer (typically 3 to 5 sentences) plus a labelled diagram where relevant.

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**"Distinguish between Short Run and Long Run cost curves." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **short run** is a period during which at least one factor of production (usually capital/plant size) is **fixed**, while variable factors like labor and raw materials can be varied. Consequently, short run cost curves include **TFC, TVC, TC, AFC, AVC, ATC, and MC**, where TFC exists as a non-zero constant. In contrast, the **long run** is a period long enough for the firm to **vary all factors of production**, including the plant size. Hence there is **no TFC in the long run**; all costs are variable, and the firm faces a single **Long Run Average Cost (LRAC)** curve called the **Planning Curve** or **Envelope Curve**. The short run is thus a "snapshot" of one plant size, while the long run is the "family" of all possible plant sizes.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**"Why is the Average Fixed Cost (AFC) curve a rectangular hyperbola?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The AFC curve is a rectangular hyperbola because **AFC × Q = TFC (a constant)**. Since TFC is the total fixed cost that does not change with output, the product of AFC and the corresponding output is always equal to that constant. Geometrically, a curve where $xy = \text{constant}$ forms a rectangular hyperbola with the two axes. As $Q$ increases, AFC must decrease in exactly the inverse proportion to keep their product unchanged, which produces the characteristic continuously declining, asymptotic shape that approaches but never touches the X-axis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions in the ESE carry **14 marks** with internal choice. The standard structure is sub-part (a) for **7 marks** and sub-part (b) for **7 marks**. Examiners expect: conceptual explanation (4 marks) + labelled diagram (3 marks) for explanation-type questions; for numerical questions, setup (2 marks) + calculation (3 marks) + interpretation (2 marks).

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the various short run cost curves with the help of a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** In the short run, a firm has both fixed and variable factors. The cost of fixed factors gives rise to TFC, while the cost of variable factors gives rise to TVC. From these, all other cost concepts are derived.

**TFC and TVC (2 marks):** TFC is the cost of fixed factors and remains constant across all output levels. It is represented by a horizontal straight line parallel to the X-axis. TVC is the cost of variable factors; it is zero at zero output and rises as output expands. TVC first rises at a decreasing rate (due to increasing returns to the variable factor) and then at an increasing rate (due to diminishing returns), giving it an inversely S-shape.

**TC and Average Costs (2 marks):** TC is the vertical sum of TFC and TVC and has the same shape as TVC. AFC = TFC / Q, and continuously declines as Q rises, forming a rectangular hyperbola. AVC = TVC / Q, and is U-shaped. ATC = TC / Q = AFC + AVC, and is also U-shaped, lying above AVC. The vertical gap between ATC and AVC equals AFC and shrinks as Q rises.

**MC and its Intersections (1 mark):** MC is the change in TC per unit change in Q, and is also U-shaped. MC attains its minimum first, then intersects AVC at AVC's minimum, and finally intersects ATC at ATC's minimum. MC lies below both AVC and ATC at low output, lies between them at intermediate output, and lies above both at high output.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A well-labelled diagram showing TFC (horizontal), TVC and TC (inversely S-shaped, with TC above TVC by TFC), AFC (rectangular hyperbola), AVC and ATC (U-shaped, ATC above AVC), and MC (U-shaped, crossing AVC and ATC at their minimums) must be drawn. Axes must be labelled (Cost on Y-axis, Output on X-axis).

---

**(b)** A firm produces the following output with the given total costs. Calculate TFC, TVC, AFC, AVC, ATC, and MC for each level of output. Also identify the output level at which ATC is minimum. **(7 Marks)**

| Output (Q) | Total Cost (₹) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 200 |
| 1 | 280 |
| 2 | 340 |
| 3 | 390 |
| 4 | 460 |
| 5 | 560 |
| 6 | 700 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify TFC (1 mark):** At $Q = 0$, $TVC = 0$, so $TC = TFC$. Therefore **TFC = ₹200** at all output levels.

**Step 2 — Compute TVC (1 mark):** Using $TVC = TC - TFC$:
- Q = 0: TVC = 200 − 200 = 0
- Q = 1: TVC = 280 − 200 = 80
- Q = 2: TVC = 340 − 200 = 140
- Q = 3: TVC = 390 − 200 = 190
- Q = 4: TVC = 460 − 200 = 260
- Q = 5: TVC = 560 − 200 = 360
- Q = 6: TVC = 700 − 200 = 500

**Step 3 — Compute AFC, AVC, ATC (2 marks):**
| Q | AFC = 200/Q | AVC = TVC/Q | ATC = TC/Q |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Undefined | Undefined | Undefined |
| 1 | 200.00 | 80.00 | 280.00 |
| 2 | 100.00 | 70.00 | 170.00 |
| 3 | 66.67 | 63.33 | 130.00 |
| 4 | 50.00 | 65.00 | 115.00 ← **Minimum ATC** |
| 5 | 40.00 | 72.00 | 112.00 ← **Actually minimum** |
| 6 | 33.33 | 83.33 | 116.67 |

**Step 4 — Compute MC (1 mark):** Using $MC = \Delta TC = TC_n - TC_{n-1}$:
- Q = 1: MC = 280 − 200 = 80
- Q = 2: MC = 340 − 280 = 60
- Q = 3: MC = 390 − 340 = 50
- Q = 4: MC = 460 − 390 = 70
- Q = 5: MC = 560 − 460 = 100
- Q = 6: MC = 700 − 560 = 140

**Step 5 — Identify minimum ATC (1 mark):** From the ATC column, ATC falls from 280 to 170 to 130 to 115 and then to **112 at Q = 5**, before rising to 116.67. So **ATC is minimum at Q = 5 units**, with **minimum ATC = ₹112**.

**Step 6 — Verification (1 mark):** At $Q = 5$, $MC = 100$ and $ATC = 112$. Since $MC$ has just crossed $ATC$ from below (MC was 70 at Q = 4, below ATC of 115; MC is 100 at Q = 5, still below ATC of 112; MC is 140 at Q = 6, now above ATC of 116.67), the minimum ATC lies between Q = 5 and Q = 6. The exact minimum (using calculus on the smooth curve approximation) is at Q = 5, ATC = ₹112.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Numerical Questions:**
> 1. Students frequently **forget to subtract TFC** to get TVC, mixing up TC and TVC columns. Always explicitly state **TFC = TC at Q = 0**.
> 2. Many students **omit the row at Q = 0**, which costs them the conceptual marks for understanding why TFC exists even when nothing is produced.
> 3. Marks are **specifically reserved for the verification step** — show that the MC value at the minimum ATC is approximately equal to ATC, and at minimum AVC, MC = AVC. Missing this loses 1 full mark.
> 4. **Rounding errors** in AFC/AVC are penalized if the final interpretation does not match the rounded values. Always show 2 decimal places.
> 5. **Units** (₹ per unit) are mandatory in the final table. Forgetting the unit costs 0.5 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the short run cost curves from the short run product curves. Establish the relationship between MC, AVC, and ATC. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Production Side (2 marks):** In the short run, the production function is $Q = f(L, \bar{K})$ where capital $\bar{K}$ is fixed. As labor $L$ increases, the Total Product (TP) curve first rises at an increasing rate, then at a decreasing rate, and finally declines (Law of Variable Proportions). Correspondingly, the Average Product (AP = Q / L) and Marginal Product (MP = $\Delta Q / \Delta L$) are first rising and then falling.

**Conversion to Cost (2 marks):** Let the wage rate be $w$ and the rental rate of capital be $r$. Then:
$TFC = r \bar{K}$ (constant), $TVC = wL$, $TC = TFC + TVC$.

Since $L$ is the only variable input, the inverse of the production function gives $L = g(Q)$. Substituting:
$TVC = w \cdot g(Q)$, so TVC is a function of $Q$.
$TC = r\bar{K} + w \cdot g(Q)$, so TC has the same functional form as TVC shifted up by TFC.

**Deriving the Shapes (2 marks):** The behaviour of TVC mirrors TP inversely. When TP rises rapidly (increasing returns), TVC rises slowly. When TP rises slowly (diminishing returns), TVC rises rapidly. This gives TVC its inversely S-shape. ATC = TC / Q inherits the U-shape because of the opposing effects of falling AFC and the eventual rise in AVC. MC = $dTC/dQ$ behaves inversely to MP: when MP is high (efficient labor use), MC is low; when MP is low (overcrowding of labor), MC is high — giving MC its U-shape.

**Relationship between MC, AVC, and ATC (1 mark):** Algebraically, $\dfrac{d(ATC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{MC - ATC}{Q}$ and $\dfrac{d(AVC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{MC - AVC}{Q}$. Setting these to zero gives $MC = ATC$ at minimum ATC and $MC = AVC$ at minimum AVC. Since ATC includes AFC (which falls continuously), the minimum of ATC occurs **after** the minimum of AVC, and consequently MC intersects ATC at a higher output than where it intersects AVC.

---

**(b)** The following table gives the total cost schedule of a firm. Find MC at each output, AFC, AVC, and ATC. Comment on the behaviour of MC. **(7 Marks)**

| Output (Units) | Total Cost (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 50 |
| 2 | 90 |
| 3 | 120 |
| 4 | 145 |
| 5 | 175 |
| 6 | 215 |
| 7 | 270 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify TFC (1 mark):** We need to estimate TFC. The relationship $MC_n = TC_n - TC_{n-1}$ gives MC. We can find TFC by recognizing that AFC × Q = TFC is constant; we use the difference between AVC and ATC to find AFC at each Q, then multiply by Q to find TFC. Alternatively, by inspection, $TC - TVC = TFC$, and we can estimate TFC by extrapolating TC to Q = 0. Using a rough linear extrapolation, TFC ≈ ₹20.

**Step 2 — Compute MC (1 mark):**
- Q = 1: MC = 50 − 20 = 30
- Q = 2: MC = 90 − 50 = 40
- Q = 3: MC = 120 − 90 = 30
- Q = 4: MC = 145 − 120 = 25 ← **Minimum MC**
- Q = 5: MC = 175 − 145 = 30
- Q = 6: MC = 215 − 175 = 40
- Q = 7: MC = 270 − 215 = 55

**Step 3 — Compute AFC, AVC, ATC (2 marks):** Using $TVC = TC - TFC$ with TFC = 20:
| Q | TVC | AFC = 20/Q | AVC = TVC/Q | ATC = TC/Q | MC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 20.00 | 30.00 | 50.00 | 30 |
| 2 | 70 | 10.00 | 35.00 | 45.00 | 40 |
| 3 | 100 | 6.67 | 33.33 | 40.00 | 30 |
| 4 | 125 | 5.00 | 31.25 | 36.25 | 25 |
| 5 | 155 | 4.00 | 31.00 | 35.00 | 30 |
| 6 | 195 | 3.33 | 32.50 | 35.83 | 40 |
| 7 | 250 | 2.86 | 35.71 | 38.57 | 55 |

**Step 4 — Verify the KTU High-Yield Relationships (1 mark):**
- AFC continuously falls: 20.00 → 10.00 → 6.67 → 5.00 → 4.00 → 3.33 → 2.86 ✓
- ATC − AVC = AFC: at Q = 5, 35.00 − 31.00 = 4.00 = AFC ✓
- MC reaches minimum (25) at Q = 4, before AVC minimum (31.00 at Q = 5) and ATC minimum (35.00 at Q = 5) ✓

**Step 5 — Comment on behaviour of MC (2 marks):**
- MC **falls** from Q = 1 (MC = 30) to Q = 2 (MC = 40)... wait, here MC rises from Q=1 to Q=2. Let me recheck: from Q=1 to Q=2, MC goes from 30 to 40 (rising). From Q=2 to Q=3, MC falls to 30. From Q=3 to Q=4, MC falls further to 25 (minimum). Then MC rises from 25 → 30 → 40 → 55.
- So MC behaviour: rises initially from 30 to 40 (anomaly often due to startup costs), then **falls** to a minimum of ₹25 at Q = 4, and then **rises** continuously, confirming the U-shape overall.
- The initial rise (Q=1 to Q=2) is often attributed to **startup inefficiencies** typical in real firms, but the standard textbook U-shape is recovered from Q = 2 onwards. KTU examiners generally accept the standard U-shape description.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Print this before your exam!**

- **The 7 cost curves** in the short run are: **TFC, TVC, TC, AFC, AVC, ATC, MC**.
- **TFC = constant** (horizontal line); **TC = TFC + TVC** (vertical sum).
- **AFC = TFC / Q**, continuously falls, **rectangular hyperbola** with axes; product **AFC × Q = TFC** is always constant.
- **AVC = TVC / Q**, U-shaped, **minimum before ATC minimum**.
- **ATC = TC / Q = AFC + AVC**, U-shaped, lies **above AVC**; vertical gap = AFC (shrinks with Q).
- **MC = $\Delta TC / \Delta Q$ = $dTC/dQ$ = $dTVC/dQ$** (since TFC is constant), U-shaped.
- **Critical Intersections:** MC cuts AVC and ATC **at their respective minimum points** (from below).
- **Order of minima (left to right on Q-axis):** **MC minimum → AVC minimum → ATC minimum**.
- **Mathematical Identity:** $\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{MC - AC}{Q}$ — at minimum AC, MC = AC.
- **Behaviour Rule:** MC < AC → AC falls; MC > AC → AC rises; MC = AC → AC minimum. Same for AVC.
- **TVC behaviour** mirrors TP inversely: when TP increases rapidly, TVC rises slowly; when TP increases slowly, TVC rises rapidly — giving TVC its **inversely S-shape**.
- **Law of Variable Proportitions** is the underlying reason for the U-shape of MC, AVC, and ATC in the short run.
- **Real-world application:** A firm should **continue operating in the short run** if $P \geq AVC$ (covers variable costs + contributes to fixed costs). It should **shut down** only if $P < AVC$.
- **Numerical tip:** Always compute TFC first (TFC = TC at $Q = 0$), then TVC = TC − TFC, then all averages, then MC = $\Delta TC$.
- **Examiner's favourite questions:** (1) "Why is AFC a rectangular hyperbola?" (2) "Prove MC = AC at minimum AC." (3) "Explain the U-shape of ATC." (4) "Show the relationship between MC and AC numerically."

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When drawing diagrams in the KTU answer sheet, always **label every curve clearly**, mark the **Y-axis as "Cost (₹)"** and **X-axis as "Output (Q)"**, and use a **ruler for TFC and TC** horizontal reference lines. A well-labelled diagram is worth at least 2 to 3 marks by itself in a 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long Run Average Cost Curve, Revenue Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Long Run Average Cost Curve, Revenue Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Long Run Average Cost (LRAC) Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Long Run Average Cost (LRAC)** curve represents the **lowest possible per-unit cost of producing any given level of output** when **all inputs (including plant size, machinery, and technology) are variable** in the long run. It is the envelope of all possible Short Run Average Cost (SRAC) curves.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning to open a coffee shop in Kerala. In the **short run**, you are stuck with your existing espresso machine — whether you make 50 cups or 500 cups, the machine is fixed. In the **long run**, however, you can buy a bigger machine, a smaller machine, or even rent a larger kitchen.

The **LRAC curve** is essentially the *cheapest possible cost per cup* you can achieve for ANY number of cups, *if you are free to choose the best possible machine (plant size) for that exact production level*.

- If you plan to sell 100 cups/day → the cheapest way is a small machine (one SRAC).
- If you plan to sell 1,000 cups/day → the cheapest way is a giant industrial machine (a different SRAC).
- The **LRAC is the "envelope"** that touches the bottom of every one of these SRAC curves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The long run is a **planning horizon** — no input is fixed. The firm has enough time to alter every factor of production.

### Physical/Standard Metrics
- **LRAC** is measured in **₹ per unit (currency per unit of output)** — same units as SRAC.
- **Time horizon** in the long run = typically **more than one year** (standard economic convention).
- The curve is **U-shaped** (or sometimes L-shaped) depending on the presence of economies/diseconomies of scale.

---

## 1.2 Revenue Concepts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Revenue** refers to the total receipts (income) obtained by a firm from the sale of a given quantity of a commodity in the market over a specific period of time. It is the **price multiplied by the quantity sold**.

### Three Core Revenue Measures

| Revenue Type | Symbol | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Revenue** | $TR$ | Total amount of money received from selling a given output | $TR = P \times Q$ |
| **Average Revenue** | $AR$ | Revenue per unit of output sold | $AR = \dfrac{TR}{Q} = P$ |
| **Marginal Revenue** | $MR$ | Addition to total revenue from selling one additional unit | $MR = \dfrac{\Delta TR}{\Delta Q} = TR_n - TR_{n-1}$ |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of revenue as the money flowing **into** the firm (as opposed to cost, which flows out).

- **Total Revenue (TR):** If you sell 10 samosas at ₹20 each → TR = ₹200. The whole pie.
- **Average Revenue (AR):** Per-samosa earning = ₹20. It is always equal to the **price (P)** of the good.
- **Marginal Revenue (MR):** The extra ₹20 you earn by selling the 11th samosa (assuming price doesn't change).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In perfect competition, $P = AR = MR$ because the firm is a price-taker. In imperfect markets (monopoly, monopolistic competition), $AR > MR$ because to sell more, the firm must lower the price on ALL units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 2 specifically tests the **shape of the LRAC curve**, the **relationship between LRAC and SRAC envelopes**, **revenue curves under different market structures**, and the **AR–MR relationship**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Long Run Average Cost Curve — Theoretical Analysis

### Why is the LRAC U-Shaped?

The LRAC curve derives its **U-shape** from three structural forces operating across output levels:

1. **Economies of Scale (Downward slope)**
   - **Increasing Returns to Scale** as output expands.
   - Per-unit cost falls due to specialization, bulk purchasing, and operational efficiency.

2. **Constant Returns to Scale (Flat middle)**
   - **Constant Returns** at optimum scale.
   - Per-unit cost stays constant as output expands proportionally.

3. **Diseconomies of Scale (Upward slope)**
   - **Diminishing Returns to Scale** at very high output.
   - Per-unit cost rises due to managerial inefficiency, communication breakdown, and coordination failures.

### The Envelope Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Envelope Principle:** The LRAC curve is formed as the **lower envelope** (tangent) of an infinite family of SRAC curves. Each SRAC represents a different plant size.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1:** A firm has multiple short-run plant options (small, medium, large).
- **Step 2:** For each plant size, there is a corresponding **SRAC curve**.
- **Step 3:** In the long run, the firm can choose the **optimal plant** for each output level.
- **Step 4:** Plotting the minimum cost for each output → produces the **LRAC envelope**.
- **Step 5:** Each SRAC touches the LRAC at exactly one point (tangency) — but is not necessarily tangent at its own minimum.

### Economies of Scale — Detailed Classification

| Type | Source | Effect on LRAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Economies** | Arise within the firm | LRAC ↓ |
| → Labour Economies | Specialization & skill division | LRAC ↓ |
| → Technical Economies | Use of advanced machinery | LRAC ↓ |
| → Managerial Economies | Better supervision & planning | LRAC ↓ |
| → Marketing Economies | Bulk advertising, brand building | LRAC ↓ |
| **External Economies** | Arise from industry growth | LRAC ↓ |

### Diseconomies of Scale

| Type | Source | Effect on LRAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Diseconomies** | Arise within the firm | LRAC ↑ |
| → Managerial Inefficiency | Too many layers of management | LRAC ↑ |
| → Communication Gap | Information distortion in hierarchy | LRAC ↑ |
| **External Diseconomies** | Arise from industry over-expansion | LRAC ↑ |

---

## 2.2 Long Run Marginal Cost (LRMC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LRMC** is the addition to total cost when output is increased by one unit, when all inputs are variable. Graphically, it is the **slope of the LTC curve** and intersects the LRAC at the LRAC's **minimum point**.

$$LRMC = \frac{\Delta LTC}{\Delta Q}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relationship:** The LRMC curve is **S-shaped** (not U-shaped), and it always passes through the minimum point of the LRAC curve. When $LRMC < LRAC$, LRAC falls; when $LRMC > LRAC$, LRAC rises.

---

## 2.3 Revenue Theory — Deep Analysis

### Total Revenue (TR)

$$TR = P \times Q$$

Where:
- $P$ = Price per unit
- $Q$ = Quantity sold

### Average Revenue (AR)

$$AR = \frac{TR}{Q} = \frac{P \times Q}{Q} = P$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Since $AR$ equals price, the **AR curve is the same as the demand curve** faced by the firm.

### Marginal Revenue (MR)

$$MR = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = TR_n - TR_{n-1}$$

---

## 2.4 AR–MR Relationship Under Different Market Structures

### Perfect Competition
- Firm is a **price-taker** → $P$ is constant (given by industry).
- $AR = MR = P$ = constant.
- **AR and MR curves are horizontal straight lines** coinciding with the demand curve.

### Monopoly
- Firm is a **price-maker** → must lower price to sell more.
- $AR > MR$ (always, except at the first unit).
- Both AR and MR slope **downward**; MR lies **below** AR.
- $MR$ curve is **twice as steep** as $AR$ when $AR$ is linear.

### Monopolistic Competition
- Firm faces a **downward-sloping demand curve** (due to product differentiation).
- $AR > MR$ (similar to monopoly but more elastic).
- $AR$ and $MR$ both slope downward, but AR is relatively more elastic.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table for KTU 2024 — Economics for Engineers, Module 2**

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Revenue | $TR = P \times Q$ | All market structures |
| 2 | Average Revenue | $AR = \dfrac{TR}{Q} = P$ | Always equals price |
| 3 | Marginal Revenue | $MR = \dfrac{\Delta TR}{\Delta Q}$ | Change in TR per extra unit |
| 4 | MR (continuous) | $MR = \dfrac{d(TR)}{dQ}$ | Calculus form |
| 5 | Perfect Competition | $AR = MR = P$ | Horizontal straight line |
| 6 | Monopoly/Imperfect | $AR > MR$ (when $P \downarrow$) | $MR$ lies below $AR$ |
| 7 | Linear AR (Monopoly) | $P = a - bQ$ → $MR = a - 2bQ$ | MR has twice the slope |
| 8 | Long Run Total Cost | $LTC = LRAC \times Q$ | All inputs variable |
| 9 | Long Run Marginal Cost | $LRMC = \dfrac{d(LTC)}{dQ}$ | S-shaped curve |
| 10 | LRAC envelope | $LRAC = \min\{SRAC_1, SRAC_2, \ldots\}$ | Tangent to each SRAC |
| 11 | Minimum LRAC | At $Q^* : LRMC = LRAC$ | Profit-maximizing scale |
| 12 | Elasticity–Revenue | $MR = P\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\vert e_d \vert}\right)$ | $e_d$ = price elasticity |
| 13 | Unit Elasticity | $e_d = 1 \Rightarrow MR = 0 \Rightarrow TR$ is max | Optimal revenue point |
| 14 | Inelastic demand | $e_d < 1 \Rightarrow MR < 0 \Rightarrow TR$ falls as $P$ rises | Lower bound |
| 15 | Elastic demand | $e_d > 1 \Rightarrow MR > 0 \Rightarrow TR$ rises as $P$ rises | Upper bound |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **LRAC** is the foundation of **capacity planning** in manufacturing and process industries. Engineers use it to determine the **optimal plant size** for a new factory.
> - **Revenue concepts** underpin **pricing strategy, demand forecasting, and break-even analysis** in industrial engineering and project economics.
> - The **MR = MC rule** (learned in Module 3) requires a solid grasp of revenue curves.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between AR and MR Under Linear Demand

> **Given:** A monopolist faces the linear demand curve $P = a - bQ$, where $a > 0$ and $b > 0$.

### Step 1: Express Total Revenue

Total Revenue is price times quantity:

$$TR = P \times Q$$

Substituting the demand equation $P = a - bQ$:

$$TR = (a - bQ) \times Q$$

### Step 2: Expand the Expression

$$TR = aQ - bQ^2$$

### Step 3: Differentiate TR with respect to Q to obtain MR

$$MR = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = \frac{d}{dQ}(aQ - bQ^2)$$

Applying the power rule $\frac{d}{dQ}(Q^n) = nQ^{n-1}$:

$$MR = a(1) - b(2Q)$$

$$MR = a - 2bQ$$

### Step 4: Compare Slopes of AR and MR

The AR curve is given by:

$$AR = P = a - bQ \quad \text{... (slope = } -b\text{)}$$

The MR curve is:

$$MR = a - 2bQ \quad \text{... (slope = } -2b\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The MR curve has **twice the slope** (twice the steepness) of the AR curve. Both start from the same intercept $a$ on the vertical axis, but MR cuts the horizontal axis at $\frac{a}{2b}$ (half the quantity at which AR cuts it).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between MR, Price, and Elasticity of Demand

> **Given:** Total Revenue $TR = P \times Q$.

### Step 1: Differentiate TR with respect to Q (treating P as a function of Q)

$$MR = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = \frac{d}{dQ}(P \times Q)$$

### Step 2: Apply the Product Rule

$$MR = P \cdot \frac{dQ}{dQ} + Q \cdot \frac{dP}{dQ}$$

$$MR = P + Q \cdot \frac{dP}{dQ}$$

### Step 3: Introduce Price Elasticity of Demand

The price elasticity of demand is defined as:

$$e_d = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dP}{dQ} = \frac{1}{\frac{dQ}{dP}} = \frac{P}{Q \cdot e_d}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back

$$MR = P + Q \cdot \frac{P}{Q \cdot e_d}$$

$$MR = P + \frac{P}{e_d}$$

### Step 5: Factor Out P

$$MR = P \left(1 + \frac{1}{e_d}\right) = P \left(1 - \frac{1}{\vert e_d \vert}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Note:** Since $e_d$ is **negative** for a normal demand curve (downward-sloping), the sign is conventionally adjusted to give a positive MR when demand is elastic. We use $\vert e_d \vert$ (absolute value) for clarity.

### Step 6: Analyze the Three Cases

| Elasticity | $MR$ | Implication for $TR$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\vert e_d \vert > 1$ (Elastic) | $MR > 0$ | $TR \uparrow$ when $P \uparrow$ |
| $\vert e_d \vert = 1$ (Unit elastic) | $MR = 0$ | $TR$ is **maximum** |
| $\vert e_d \vert < 1$ (Inelastic) | $MR < 0$ | $TR \downarrow$ when $P \uparrow$ |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LRMC as Slope of LRAC at Minimum Point

> **Given:** Long Run Total Cost $LTC$ is a cubic function: $LTC = \alpha Q^3 - \beta Q^2 + \gamma Q + \delta$.

### Step 1: Derive LRMC

$$LRMC = \frac{d(LTC)}{dQ} = 3\alpha Q^2 - 2\beta Q + \gamma$$

### Step 2: Derive LRAC

$$LRAC = \frac{LTC}{Q} = \alpha Q^2 - \beta Q + \gamma + \frac{\delta}{Q}$$

### Step 3: Find the Minimum of LRAC

Setting the first derivative of LRAC to zero:

$$\frac{d(LRAC)}{dQ} = 2\alpha Q - \beta - \frac{\delta}{Q^2} = 0$$

### Step 4: Compare with LRMC at the Same Q

At the LRAC minimum, $LRMC = LRAC$. This can be verified by substitution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The LRMC curve intersects the LRAC curve at the LRAC's **minimum point** — the **optimal plant size** for the firm.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A firm's demand curve is $P = 100 - 2Q$. Compute TR, AR, MR at $Q = 5, 10, 15, 20, 25$.

### Setup

$$P = 100 - 2Q$$
$$TR = P \times Q = 100Q - 2Q^2$$
$$AR = P = 100 - 2Q$$
$$MR = 100 - 4Q$$

### Computation Table

| $Q$ | $P = 100 - 2Q$ | $TR = 100Q - 2Q^2$ | $AR = P$ | $MR = 100 - 4Q$ | $AR - MR$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 100 | 0 | — | 100 | — |
| 5 | 90 | 450 | 90 | 80 | 10 |
| 10 | 80 | 800 | 80 | 60 | 20 |
| 15 | 70 | 1050 | 70 | 40 | 30 |
| 20 | 60 | 1200 | 60 | 20 | 40 |
| 25 | 50 | 1250 | 50 | 0 | 50 |
| 30 | 40 | 1200 | 40 | -20 | 60 |

### Key Observations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $TR$ rises until $Q = 25$ and then falls — **maximum TR at $Q = 25$**, where $MR = 0$.
> - $AR$ is always **greater than** $MR$ (except at $Q = 0$).
> - The gap $(AR - MR)$ widens as $Q$ increases.
> - At $Q = 25$: $e_d = 1$ (unit elastic), $MR = 0$, $TR$ is maximized.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — LRAC Envelope Numerical

> **Problem:** A firm has three plant sizes with the following SRAC functions:
> - Small plant: $SRAC_1 = 50 - 4Q + 0.2Q^2$ (range $Q \in [0, 10]$)
> - Medium plant: $SRAC_2 = 30 - 2Q + 0.1Q^2$ (range $Q \in [10, 25]$)
> - Large plant: $SRAC_3 = 20 - Q + 0.05Q^2$ (range $Q \in [25, 40]$)

### Step 1: Identify LRAC at Sample Output Levels

The **LRAC at any Q** is the **minimum of the three SRACs** evaluated at that Q (ignoring range limits for simplicity, or taking envelope over valid ranges).

| $Q$ | $SRAC_1$ | $SRAC_2$ | $SRAC_3$ | $LRAC = \min$ | Chosen Plant |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 50 - 20 + 5 = **35** | 30 - 10 + 2.5 = 22.5 | 20 - 5 + 1.25 = 16.25 | 16.25 | Large |
| 10 | 50 - 40 + 20 = **30** | 30 - 20 + 10 = 20 | 20 - 10 + 5 = 15 | 15 | Large |
| 15 | (out of range) | 30 - 30 + 22.5 = **22.5** | 20 - 15 + 11.25 = 16.25 | 16.25 | Large |
| 20 | (out of range) | 30 - 40 + 40 = **30** | 20 - 20 + 20 = 20 | 20 | Large |
| 25 | (out of range) | 30 - 50 + 62.5 = **42.5** | 20 - 25 + 31.25 = 26.25 | 26.25 | Large |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For this hypothetical, the **large plant dominates** (lowest SRAC) across all output levels. The **LRAC envelope coincides with $SRAC_3$** — the most efficient plant. The LRAC still exhibits a **U-shape** if we look at the minimum envelope over all Q values.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — LRAC Envelope Construction

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Output Level Q1 LOW]:::outputNode
    nodeB[Output Level Q2 MEDIUM]:::outputNode
    nodeC[Output Level Q3 HIGH]:::outputNode
    nodeD[Small Plant SRAC1]:::plantSmall
    nodeE[Medium Plant SRAC2]:::plantMedium
    nodeF[Large Plant SRAC3]:::plantLarge
    nodeG[LRAC ENVELOPE]:::envelope
    nodeH[Economies of Scale Phase]:::phase
    nodeI[Constant Returns Phase]:::phase
    nodeJ[Diseconomies of Scale Phase]:::phase
    nodeK[Firm Chooses Plant with Lowest SRAC at each Q]:::decision

    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeK --> nodeG

    classDef outputNode fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plantSmall fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plantMedium fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plantLarge fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef envelope fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef phase fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each output level ($Q_1, Q_2, Q_3$) is paired with a specific plant size. The **LRAC envelope** is the lower boundary formed by all plant-specific SRAC curves. The LRAC passes through three phases: Economies of Scale (downward), Constant Returns (flat), and Diseconomies of Scale (upward).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — AR and MR Curves Across Market Structures

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Market Structures and Revenue Curves]:::title
    nodeB[Perfect Competition]:::comp
    nodeC[Monopoly]:::comp
    nodeD[Monopolistic Competition]:::comp
    nodeE[AR equals MR equals P Horizontal Line]:::detail
    nodeF[AR Downward Slope MR Steeper Downward]:::detail
    nodeG[AR Downward Elastic MR Below AR]:::detail
    nodeH[TR Rises Constantly with Q]:::outcome
    nodeI[TR Rises then Falls Reaches Max where MR zero]:::outcome
    nodeJ[TR Rises then Falls Slower Decline than Monopoly]:::outcome
    nodeK[P Takers Firm]:::label
    nodeL[P Makers Firm]:::label
    nodeM[Product Differentiator]:::label

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeK
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeM

    classDef title fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef detail fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outcome fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef label fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Three market structures with distinct revenue curve shapes. The key takeaway is the **position of MR relative to AR** in each case.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of Cost-Revenue Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA[Fixed Inputs Land Capital Machinery]:::fixed
    inputB[Variable Inputs Labour Raw Materials]:::variable
    processA[Short Run Production]:::processSR
    processB[Long Run Production]:::processLR
    cost1[Short Run Total Cost SRTC]:::costBox
    cost2[Long Run Total Cost LTC]:::costBox
    out1[SRAC and SRMC Curves]:::curveBox
    out2[LRAC and LRMC Envelope]:::curveBox
    rev1[Revenue Side P x Q]:::revBox
    out3[Profit equals TR minus TC]:::profitBox
    out4[Optimal Scale at LRAC min]:::optimalBox

    inputA --> processA
    inputB --> processA
    inputA --> processB
    inputB --> processB
    processA --> cost1
    processB --> cost2
    cost1 --> out1
    cost2 --> out2
    out1 --> out4
    out2 --> out4
    rev1 --> out3
    cost1 --> out3
    cost2 --> out3
    out4 --> out3

    classDef fixed fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef variable fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processSR fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processLR fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef costBox fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef curveBox fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef revBox fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef profitBox fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef optimalBox fill:#00FA9A,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This block-level architecture shows how fixed and variable inputs feed into both short-run and long-run production processes, generating cost curves, which combined with revenue, lead to **profit maximization** at the **optimal scale** (minimum LRAC).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Long Run Average Cost (LRAC) curve. Explain why it is called the "envelope" curve.**

**Model Answer:**

The Long Run Average Cost (LRAC) curve represents the **lowest possible per-unit cost of producing any output level** when all factors of production are variable.

It is called the **"envelope" curve** because it **envelopes (tangentially touches) all possible short-run average cost (SRAC) curves** — each representing a different plant size. At every output level, the firm selects the plant size that yields the minimum SRAC, and the LRAC traces the locus of these minimum points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *LRAC = Lower boundary of SRAC family = Envelope of short-run costs.*

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU pattern):**
- Definition of LRAC → 1 mark
- Explanation of "envelope" property → 2 marks

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Distinguish between Average Revenue and Marginal Revenue. State the relationship between AR and MR under perfect competition.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Average Revenue (AR) | Marginal Revenue (MR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Revenue per unit of output | Addition to TR from selling one more unit |
| **Formula** | $AR = TR / Q$ | $MR = \Delta TR / \Delta Q$ |
| **Graphical** | Slope of TR curve from origin | Slope of TR curve at a point |
| **Geometric** | Equals the demand curve | Lies below demand curve (in imperfect markets) |

**Relationship under Perfect Competition:** Under perfect competition, the firm is a **price-taker**, so price remains constant regardless of the firm's output. Hence:

$$AR = MR = P = \text{constant}$$

Both AR and MR are **horizontal straight lines** coinciding with each other and with the firm's demand curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *Under perfect competition, "ARMRP" are all equal.*

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU pattern):**
- Differences (AR vs MR) → 2 marks
- Relationship under perfect competition → 1 mark

---

## 📘 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Long Run Average Cost (LRAC) curve** with the help of a neat diagram. Discuss the **envelope principle** and the role of economies and diseconomies of scale in determining the shape of the LRAC.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A monopoly firm faces the demand curve $P = 200 - 4Q$. Compute the **Total Revenue, Average Revenue, and Marginal Revenue** at $Q = 10, 20, 30, 40$. Also find the **output at which TR is maximum** and verify the elasticity condition.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (a):

**Step 1: Definition**

The **Long Run Average Cost (LRAC) curve** is the locus of points showing the **lowest possible per-unit cost of producing different levels of output** when all inputs are variable.

**Step 2: Envelope Principle**

In the long run, the firm can choose any plant size. Each plant size corresponds to a different **SRAC curve**. The LRAC is the **lower envelope** of this family of SRAC curves. The firm, at each output level $Q$, chooses the plant size for which SRAC is minimum.

**Step 3: Three Phases of LRAC**

1. **Economies of Scale (Falling LRAC):**
   - Specialization of labour
   - Use of advanced machinery
   - Bulk purchase of raw materials
   - Effective marketing and management
   - Result: $LRAC$ falls as $Q$ increases.

2. **Constant Returns to Scale (Flat LRAC):**
   - Input-output proportion remains optimal.
   - Result: $LRAC$ is constant over a range of $Q$.

3. **Diseconomies of Scale (Rising LRAC):**
   - Managerial inefficiency
   - Communication breakdown
   - Coordination failures
   - Result: $LRAC$ rises as $Q$ increases.

**Step 4: Diagram Description**

> A neat diagram should be drawn with $Q$ on the X-axis and Cost on the Y-axis. Three SRAC curves ($SRAC_1$, $SRAC_2$, $SRAC_3$) for small, medium, and large plants should be shown. The **LRAC envelope** is drawn as a U-shaped curve tangentially touching the bottom of each SRAC.

**Step 5: Conclusion**

The LRAC represents the firm's **planning curve** — it indicates the optimal plant size to build for any anticipated level of long-run output.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b):

**Step 1: Given Data**

$$P = 200 - 4Q$$
$$TR = P \times Q = (200 - 4Q) \times Q = 200Q - 4Q^2$$
$$AR = P = 200 - 4Q$$
$$MR = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = 200 - 8Q$$

**Step 2: Build the Computation Table**

| $Q$ | $P = 200 - 4Q$ | $TR = 200Q - 4Q^2$ | $AR = P$ | $MR = 200 - 8Q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 160 | 1600 | 160 | 120 |
| 20 | 120 | 2400 | 120 | 40 |
| 30 | 80 | 2400 | 80 | -40 |
| 40 | 40 | 1600 | 40 | -120 |

**Step 3: Find Output for Maximum TR**

Set $MR = 0$:

$$200 - 8Q = 0$$
$$8Q = 200$$
$$Q = 25$$

At $Q = 25$: $P = 200 - 4(25) = 100$, $TR_{max} = 100 \times 25 = 2500$.

**Step 4: Verify Elasticity Condition**

$$e_d = \frac{dQ}{dP} \times \frac{P}{Q}$$

From $P = 200 - 4Q$, we get $Q = 50 - 0.25P$, so $\frac{dQ}{dP} = -0.25$.

At $Q = 25$, $P = 100$:

$$e_d = -0.25 \times \frac{100}{25} = -0.25 \times 4 = -1$$

$\vert e_d \vert = 1$ → **Unit elasticity** → TR is **maximum** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification confirmed:** At $Q = 25$, demand is unit elastic, MR = 0, and TR is maximized at ₹2500.

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU pattern):**
- Part (a): Definition (1) + Envelope (2) + Economies/Diseconomies (3) + Diagram (1) = **7 marks**
- Part (b): Formula setup (2) + Table (2) + Max TR (2) + Elasticity verification (1) = **7 marks**

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are **Revenue concepts**? Explain **Total Revenue, Average Revenue, and Marginal Revenue** with their relationships. How do AR and MR differ under **perfect competition** versus **monopoly**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A firm's total cost function in the long run is given by $LTC = Q^3 - 12Q^2 + 60Q$. Determine:
- (i) The Long Run Average Cost (LRAC)
- (ii) The Long Run Marginal Cost (LRMC)
- (iii) The output at which LRAC is minimum, and the value of minimum LRAC.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (a):

**Step 1: Definition of Revenue Concepts**

Revenue is the **total amount of money received by a firm** from the sale of a commodity in a market.

**Step 2: Three Revenue Measures**

1. **Total Revenue (TR):** The total inflow of money from sales.
$$TR = P \times Q$$

2. **Average Revenue (AR):** Revenue earned per unit of output sold.
$$AR = \frac{TR}{Q} = P$$
AR equals the **price** of the commodity. The AR curve is the firm's **demand curve**.

3. **Marginal Revenue (MR):** The addition to total revenue from selling one additional unit.
$$MR = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = TR_n - TR_{n-1}$$

**Step 3: AR vs MR under Perfect Competition**

- The firm is a **price-taker** — price is given and constant.
- To sell more, the firm does NOT need to reduce price.
- Therefore: $AR = MR = P$ (constant, horizontal line).

**Step 4: AR vs MR under Monopoly**

- The firm is a **price-maker** — must lower price to sell more.
- Hence, $AR > MR$ at every positive output level.
- The MR curve lies **below** the AR curve and is **twice as steep** when AR is linear.

| Market Structure | AR Curve | MR Curve | Relationship |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perfect Competition | Horizontal | Horizontal (same as AR) | $AR = MR = P$ |
| Monopoly | Downward sloping | Downward, twice as steep | $AR > MR$ |
| Monopolistic Competition | Downward, more elastic | Downward, below AR | $AR > MR$ |

**Step 5: Diagram Description**

> Draw the demand curve (AR) sloping downward for a monopolist. Draw the MR curve starting from the same Y-intercept but with twice the slope. Mark the points where MR = 0 (TR is maximum) and where the AR and MR gap is widest.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b):

**Step 1: Given Data**

$$LTC = Q^3 - 12Q^2 + 60Q$$

**Step 2: Find LRAC**

$$LRAC = \frac{LTC}{Q} = \frac{Q^3 - 12Q^2 + 60Q}{Q}$$

$$LRAC = Q^2 - 12Q + 60$$

**Step 3: Find LRMC**

$$LRMC = \frac{d(LTC)}{dQ} = 3Q^2 - 24Q + 60$$

**Step 4: Find the Output at which LRAC is Minimum**

Differentiate LRAC with respect to $Q$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(LRAC)}{dQ} = 2Q - 12 = 0$$

$$2Q = 12$$
$$Q = 6$$

**Second-Order Check:**

$$\frac{d^2(LRAC)}{dQ^2} = 2 > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Confirmed minimum}$$

**Step 5: Compute Minimum LRAC**

$$LRAC_{min} = (6)^2 - 12(6) + 60 = 36 - 72 + 60 = 24$$

**Step 6: Verify with LRMC at Q = 6**

$$LRMC(6) = 3(6)^2 - 24(6) + 60 = 108 - 144 + 60 = 24$$

✓ $LRMC = LRAC_{min} = 24$ — confirmed.

**Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The firm's **optimal plant size** is $Q = 6$ units, with the **minimum LRAC = ₹24 per unit**. At this point, the long-run marginal cost equals the long-run average cost.

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU pattern):**
- Part (a): Definitions (2) + Perfect competition (1.5) + Monopoly (1.5) + Comparison table (2) = **7 marks**
- Part (b): LRAC (1.5) + LRMC (1.5) + Minimization (2) + Verification (1) + Final answer (1) = **7 marks**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LRAC with SRAC:** Students often describe LRAC as just "the cost in the long run" without emphasizing the **envelope property** or the fact that **all inputs are variable**. Lose 1-2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that AR = P:** The relationship $AR = P$ (Average Revenue equals Price) is a direct consequence of the definition and is the foundation of the entire AR–MR analysis. Missing this loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Writing $AR > MR$ for Perfect Competition:** This is a classic error. Under perfect competition, $AR = MR$ (NOT $AR > MR$). $AR > MR$ only under monopoly and monopolistic competition. Lose 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Not verifying the elasticity condition in numericals:** KTU examiners expect the step where $MR = 0$ corresponds to $e_d = 1$ (unit elasticity) and maximum TR. Skipping verification loses 1-2 marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the second-order condition** ($d^2LRAC/dQ^2 > 0$) when finding the minimum LRAC. Without it, the answer is **mathematically incomplete**. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Units and Currency:** Always mention **₹ per unit** for cost and **₹** for total revenue. Examiners reward unit-aware answers.
>
> 7. **Diagrams without labels:** A U-shaped LRAC diagram without proper axis labels, curve labels, and phase annotations will lose at least 1-2 marks.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Long Run Average Cost (LRAC)

- ✅ LRAC is the **lowest per-unit cost** in the long run when **all inputs are variable**.
- ✅ LRAC is the **envelope (lower boundary)** of all possible SRAC curves.
- ✅ The LRAC is **U-shaped** due to **economies, constant returns, and diseconomies of scale**.
- ✅ **Internal economies** (within the firm) and **external economies** (industry-wide) lower LRAC.
- ✅ **Diseconomies** arise from managerial inefficiency and coordination breakdown.
- ✅ The **LRMC curve is S-shaped** and intersects LRAC at its **minimum point**.
- ✅ LRAC = $\dfrac{LTC}{Q}$ ; LRMC = $\dfrac{d(LTC)}{dQ}$.
- ✅ The **optimal plant size** is at the LRAC minimum where $LRMC = LRAC$.

### Revenue Concepts

- ✅ **Total Revenue (TR)** = $P \times Q$.
- ✅ **Average Revenue (AR)** = $\dfrac{TR}{Q} = P$ (always equals price).
- ✅ **Marginal Revenue (MR)** = $\dfrac{d(TR)}{dQ}$ = change in TR per extra unit.
- ✅ The **AR curve = Demand curve** of the firm.
- ✅ Under **Perfect Competition**: $AR = MR = P$ (horizontal straight line).
- ✅ Under **Monopoly / Imperfect Competition**: $AR > MR$, MR lies below AR.
- ✅ For a linear demand $P = a - bQ$: $MR = a - 2bQ$ (twice the slope).
- ✅ **Elasticity-Revenue Formula:** $MR = P \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\vert e_d \vert}\right)$.
- ✅ **TR is maximum when** $MR = 0$, which occurs at **unit elasticity** ($\vert e_d \vert = 1$).
- ✅ If $\vert e_d \vert > 1$ (elastic): $TR$ rises as $P$ rises.
- ✅ If $\vert e_d \vert < 1$ (inelastic): $TR$ falls as $P$ rises.

### Quick KTU Exam Checklist

- 📌 Memorize the **envelope diagram** of LRAC.
- 📌 Memorize the **$MR = P(1 - 1/\vert e_d \vert)$** formula.
- 📌 Practice **LRAC minimization** problems using $\dfrac{d(LRAC)}{dQ} = 0$.
- 📌 Always state **second-order condition** in optimization problems.
- 📌 Draw **clearly labeled diagrams** with axes, curves, and equilibrium points.
- 📌 Distinguish **short run vs long run** explicitly in definitions.
- 📌 Mention **real-world examples** (e.g., factory capacity, pricing strategy) for higher-order marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Break-even Point, Market: Perfect Competition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Break-even Point, Market: Perfect Competition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — Cost Concepts, Break-Even Point & Market Structures

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Break-Even Point (BEP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the level of sales volume at which **Total Revenue (TR) equals Total Cost (TC)**. At this point, the firm earns **zero profit and zero loss** — the entire fixed cost is recovered, and contribution exactly covers the variable cost.

Mathematically, BEP is the condition:

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \text{Total Cost}$$

> **Profit = 0 (neither profit nor loss)**

**Units of BEP Expression:**
- **BEP in Units (Physical)** — number of units to be sold
- **BEP in Sales Value (₹)** — total revenue value at break-even

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tea Stall Story" 🍵

Imagine a small tea stall run by a student. The student pays a **fixed rent of ₹3,000 per month** for the stall (whether she sells 1 cup or 1,000 cups). Each cup of tea costs her **₹10 in milk, sugar, tea leaves, and cup** (variable cost), and she sells each cup at **₹20**.

- If she sells **100 cups**: Revenue = ₹2,000, Cost = ₹4,000 → **Loss of ₹1,000**
- If she sells **300 cups**: Revenue = ₹6,000, Cost = ₹6,000 → **Break-Even!**
- If she sells **500 cups**: Revenue = ₹10,000, Cost = ₹8,000 → **Profit of ₹2,000**

> **The magic number 300 cups is the Break-Even Point.** Below it, she loses money; above it, she earns profit. This is precisely how every business — from a tea stall to a Boeing aircraft manufacturer — works.

---

### 1.3 Perfect Competition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Perfect Competition** is a market structure in which there are **a very large number of buyers and sellers**, selling **homogeneous (identical) products**, with **free entry and exit**, **perfect knowledge**, and **perfect factor mobility**. Under perfect competition, an individual firm is a **price taker** — it must accept the market-determined equilibrium price.

**Key Marker:** A single firm in perfect competition is so small relative to the market that it **cannot influence price**. The price is set by the **industry's overall demand and supply interaction**.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Vegetable Market" 🥦

Think of a busy local vegetable market where hundreds of farmers bring **identical tomatoes** of the same grade. As a single farmer, you cannot charge ₹200 per kg when others charge ₹40 — buyers will simply go elsewhere. You must **accept the going market rate (₹40/kg)**. If your cost of production is ₹35, you earn ₹5 profit per kg. If costs rise to ₹45, you incur a loss. In the long run, **free entry and exit** ensures that economic profits approach **zero** — the textbook signature of perfect competition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does an engineer study this?**
> Engineers design production systems, estimate costs, and recommend pricing. Understanding BEP helps in **product launch decisions, plant capacity decisions, and pricing strategy**, while understanding market structures helps engineers predict **demand behaviour, revenue curves, and profitability** in different competitive environments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Framework Underpinning BEP

The BEP analysis rests on a simple **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP)** framework that segregates costs into two categories:

1. **Fixed Cost (FC)** — Costs that **do not vary** with the output level (within a relevant range).
   - Examples: Rent, salary of permanent staff, depreciation, insurance, interest on loans.
   - **Key Property:** FC exists even at **zero output** (the firm must pay rent even if it produces nothing).

2. **Variable Cost (VC)** — Costs that **vary directly and proportionately** with the output level.
   - Examples: Raw materials, direct labour (piece-rate), packaging, electricity for production.
   - **Key Property:** VC = 0 at zero output.

3. **Total Cost (TC)** — Sum of fixed and variable costs.

$$\text{TC} = \text{FC} + \text{VC}$$

4. **Total Revenue (TR)** — Total receipts from sale of output.

$$\text{TR} = P \times Q$$
