where **P** = selling price per unit, **Q** = quantity sold.

5. **Contribution (C)** — The amount each unit contributes toward covering fixed costs and generating profit.

$$\text{Contribution per unit} = P - \text{AVC (Average Variable Cost)}$$

$$\text{Total Contribution} = (P - \text{AVC}) \times Q = \text{TR} - \text{VC}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Contribution"?**
> Each unit sold first "contributes" to recovering the fixed cost. Once the cumulative contribution equals the fixed cost, the firm reaches **break-even**. After that, every additional unit's contribution becomes pure profit.

---

## 2.2 The BEP Condition — Logical Flow

**Step 1:** Define profit: $\pi = \text{TR} - \text{TC}$

**Step 2:** Substitute the cost identity: $\pi = (P \times Q) - (\text{FC} + \text{VC})$

**Step 3:** At break-even: $\pi = 0 \implies P \times Q = \text{FC} + \text{VC}$

**Step 4:** Since VC = AVC × Q, the break-even condition becomes:

$$P \times Q = \text{FC} + (\text{AVC} \times Q)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for Q — this yields the **BEP in units**.

---

## 2.3 Assumptions of Break-Even Analysis (KTU Board Favourite)

1. All costs can be classified neatly into fixed and variable categories.
2. Variable cost per unit is **constant** (linear VC behaviour).
3. Selling price per unit is **constant** (no discount, no quantity rebates).
4. The firm produces and sells a **single product** (or constant sales mix).
5. The analysis is valid only within the **relevant range** of production.
6. Inventory levels are **constant** (production = sales).
7. Efficiency and productivity remain **unchanged**.

---

## 2.4 Perfect Competition — Characteristic Checklist (High-Yield)

| # | Characteristic | Implication for the Firm |
|---|----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Very large number of buyers and sellers** | No single participant can influence price |
| 2 | **Homogeneous (identical) products** | Buyers are indifferent between sellers |
| 3 | **Free entry and exit** | Long-run economic profit = 0 |
| 4 | **Perfect knowledge** | All participants know prices, quality, techniques |
| 5 | **Perfect factor mobility** | Resources move freely across firms |
| 6 | **No government intervention** | No taxes, subsidies, or price controls |
| 7 | **No transport costs** | Location is irrelevant to buyers |

---

## 2.5 Price Determination in Perfect Competition

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry vs. Firm — the critical distinction in KTU 2024.**
> - The **Industry** determines the **market price** through aggregate Demand and Supply.
> - The **Individual Firm** is a price taker — it faces a **perfectly elastic (horizontal) demand curve** at the equilibrium price.

**The Industry's Equilibrium Price:**

$$\text{Industry Demand} = \text{Industry Supply}$$

This yields the **equilibrium market price $P^*$**. Every firm must sell at $P^*$ — selling above $P^*$ loses all customers; selling below $P^*$ is unnecessary (since it can sell unlimited quantity at $P^*$).

**The Firm's Demand Curve:**

$$P = P^* = \text{constant}$$

The firm maximises profit where **Marginal Cost (MC) = Marginal Revenue (MR) = Price (P)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Short-Run Equilibrium of a Firm in Perfect Competition:**
> - **Profit** when $P > \text{AC}_{\text{min}}$ (Price > Average Cost)
> - **Loss (but continues operating)** when $\text{AVC}_{\text{min}} < P < \text{AC}_{\text{min}}$
> - **Shut-down** when $P < \text{AVC}_{\text{min}}$
> - **Long-Run Equilibrium:** $P = \text{AC}_{\text{min}} = \text{MC}$ and economic profit = 0.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-tested** for KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorise the variable definitions: $Q$ = quantity, $P$ = price per unit, FC = fixed cost, VC = variable cost, TC = total cost, AVC = average variable cost, AC = average cost, MC = marginal cost, MR = marginal revenue, $S$ = total sales value, $V$ = variable cost, $F$ = fixed cost.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Description |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Total Cost | $\text{TC} = \text{FC} + \text{VC}$ | Sum of fixed + variable costs |
| 2 | Total Revenue | $\text{TR} = P \times Q$ | Price times quantity |
| 3 | Profit | $\pi = \text{TR} - \text{TC}$ | Revenue minus total cost |
| 4 | Contribution per unit | $c = P - \text{AVC}$ | Selling price minus variable cost/unit |
| 5 | **BEP in Units** | $Q_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{P - \text{AVC}}$ | Fixed cost divided by contribution/unit |
| 6 | **BEP in Sales Value (₹)** | $S_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{1 - \dfrac{\text{VC}}{S}}$ | Using P/V ratio (generalised form) |
| 7 | **P/V Ratio (Profit Volume Ratio)** | $\text{P/V} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$ | Percentage of sales that is contribution |
| 8 | BEP in Sales Value (using P/V) | $S_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{\text{P/V ratio}}$ | Direct formula using P/V ratio |
| 9 | Margin of Safety (Units) | $\text{MOS} = Q_{\text{actual}} - Q_{\text{BEP}}$ | Buffer volume above BEP |
| 10 | Margin of Safety (₹) | $\text{MOS}_S = S_{\text{actual}} - S_{\text{BEP}}$ | Buffer sales value above BEP |
| 11 | MOS as % of Sales | $\text{MOS \%} = \dfrac{\text{MOS}_S}{S_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | Relative buffer measure |
| 12 | Profit at given output | $\pi = (P - \text{AVC}) \times Q - \text{FC}$ | Contribution minus fixed cost |
| 13 | Profit using P/V ratio | $\pi = \text{P/V ratio} \times S_{\text{actual}} - \text{FC}$ | Direct profit calculation |
| 14 | **Firm's profit in PC (short run)** | $\pi = (P - \text{AC}) \times Q$ | Per-unit margin times quantity |
| 15 | **Long-run PC equilibrium condition** | $P = \text{AC}_{\text{min}} = \text{MC}$ | Price touches minimum of AC curve |
| 16 | Shutdown Point | $P < \text{AVC}_{\text{min}}$ | Firm ceases production in SR |
| 17 | Shut-down BEP in sales | $S_{\text{SD}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{1 - \dfrac{\text{AVC}}{P}}$ | Sales level at AVC minimum |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Application | Use of BEP / Perfect Competition Concept |
|------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Product launch decision** | Estimate minimum sales to recover R&D and tooling costs |
| **Plant capacity planning** | Compare BEP at different capacity levels |
| **Make-or-buy decision** | Compute BEP for in-house vs. outsourced production |
| **Pricing strategy** | Use P/V ratio to set price for target profit |
| **Cost control** | Identify contribution margin sensitivity |
| **Market analysis for new product** | Determine if product enters a perfectly competitive or monopolistic market |
| **Government policy (KTU economics)** | Justify why subsidies exist in PC markets (long-run zero profit) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — BEP in Units

**Starting condition** (profit = 0 at BEP):

$$\pi = 0$$

**Step 1 — Profit identity:**

$$\pi = \text{TR} - \text{TC}$$

**Step 2 — Expand TR and TC using definitions:**

$$0 = (P \times Q) - (\text{FC} + \text{VC})$$

**Step 3 — Move FC to the left side:**

$$P \times Q = \text{FC} + \text{VC}$$

**Step 4 — Express VC as AVC × Q (variable cost per unit times units):**

$$P \times Q = \text{FC} + (\text{AVC} \times Q)$$

**Step 5 — Factor out Q on the right-hand side:**

$$P \times Q = \text{FC} + \text{AVC} \times Q$$

**Step 6 — Group Q terms on one side:**

$$P \times Q - \text{AVC} \times Q = \text{FC}$$

**Step 7 — Factor out Q:**

$$Q \times (P - \text{AVC}) = \text{FC}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for Q (the BEP in units):**

$$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{P - \text{AVC}}\,}$$

The denominator $(P - \text{AVC})$ is the **contribution per unit (c)**. Hence an alternative form is:

$$Q_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{c}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — BEP in Sales Value (₹)

**Step 1 — Start from the break-even condition in revenue terms:**

$$\text{TR} = \text{TC}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute TR = $S$ and TC = FC + VC:**

$$S = \text{FC} + \text{VC}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$S - \text{VC} = \text{FC}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that $S - \text{VC}$ = Total Contribution. So:**

$$\text{Total Contribution} = \text{FC at BEP}$$

**Step 5 — Define the P/V ratio:**

$$\text{P/V ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} = \dfrac{S - \text{VC}}{S}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to express sales in terms of contribution:**

$$\text{Contribution} = S \times \text{P/V ratio}$$

**Step 7 — Apply at BEP (Contribution = FC):**

$$\text{FC} = S_{\text{BEP}} \times \text{P/V ratio}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $S_{\text{BEP}}$:**

$$\boxed{\,S_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{\text{P/V ratio}}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Profit at a Given Sales Level (using P/V ratio)

**Step 1 — Start with the profit identity:**

$$\pi = \text{TR} - \text{TC}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\pi = S - (\text{FC} + \text{VC})$$

**Step 3 — Group the contribution term:**

$$\pi = (S - \text{VC}) - \text{FC}$$

**Step 4 — Replace $(S - \text{VC})$ with $S \times \text{P/V ratio}$:**

$$\pi = (S \times \text{P/V ratio}) - \text{FC}$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed form (highly useful in KTU problems):**

$$\boxed{\,\pi = S \times \text{P/V ratio} - \text{FC}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Short-Run Profit in Perfect Competition

**Step 1 — The firm is a price taker, so $P$ is constant.**

**Step 2 — Total revenue of the firm:**

$$\text{TR} = P \times Q$$

**Step 3 — Total cost of the firm:**

$$\text{TC} = \text{FC} + (\text{AC} \times Q)$$

where AC is average cost. In perfect competition, $\text{AC} = \text{AVC} + \text{AFC}$ (average fixed cost per unit).

**Step 4 — Profit:**

$$\pi = \text{TR} - \text{TC} = (P \times Q) - (\text{FC} + \text{AC} \times Q)$$

**Step 5 — Factor Q:**

$$\pi = (P - \text{AC}) \times Q - \text{FC}$$

> **For a long-run equilibrium in perfect competition, $P = \text{AC}_{\text{min}}$, so $\pi = 0$.**

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A firm sells a product at ₹50 per unit. The variable cost per unit is ₹30, and the total fixed cost is ₹4,000. Calculate:
(a) BEP in units
(b) BEP in sales value
(c) Profit at a sales level of 200 units
(d) Margin of safety in units and as a percentage, if the actual sales are 250 units.

**Given Data:**

- Selling price $P$ = ₹50/unit
- Variable cost per unit AVC = ₹30/unit
- Fixed cost FC = ₹4,000
- Actual sales $Q_{\text{actual}}$ = 250 units

**Solution:**

**(a) BEP in Units**

$$Q_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{P - \text{AVC}} = \dfrac{4000}{50 - 30} = \dfrac{4000}{20} = 200 \text{ units}$$

**(b) BEP in Sales Value**

$$\text{P/V ratio} = \dfrac{P - \text{AVC}}{P} = \dfrac{20}{50} = 0.40 \;\text{or}\; 40\%$$

$$S_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{\text{P/V ratio}} = \dfrac{4000}{0.40} = 10{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Cross-check:** $S_{\text{BEP}} = 200 \text{ units} \times ₹50 = ₹10{,}000$ ✓

**(c) Profit at $Q = 200$ units**

$$\pi = (P - \text{AVC}) \times Q - \text{FC} = (20)(200) - 4000 = 4000 - 4000 = 0$$

(The break-even point by definition yields zero profit ✓)

**(d) Margin of Safety (MOS)**

$$\text{MOS (units)} = Q_{\text{actual}} - Q_{\text{BEP}} = 250 - 200 = 50 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{MOS (\₹)} = 50 \times 50 = ₹2{,}500$$

$$\text{MOS \%} = \dfrac{2500}{250 \times 50} \times 100 = \dfrac{2500}{12500} \times 100 = 20\%$$

> **Interpretation:** Actual sales can fall by up to 50 units (or 20%) before the firm slips into a loss.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula (Examiner's Heuristic)

| Problem Clue in the Question | Formula to Use Directly | Why |
|------------------------------|-------------------------|-----|
| "Find the break-even **in units**" | $Q_{\text{BEP}} = \text{FC} / (P - \text{AVC})$ | Direct units formula |
| "Find the break-even **in sales (₹)**" | $S_{\text{BEP}} = \text{FC} / \text{P/V ratio}$ | Avoids computing units first |
| "Find profit when sales are ₹X" | $\pi = S \times \text{P/V} - \text{FC}$ | Direct profit formula |
| "Margin of safety at actual sales of Y" | $\text{MOS} = \text{Actual sales} - S_{\text{BEP}}$ | Buffer sales above BEP |
| "What is the **shut-down** point?" | $P = \text{AVC}_{\text{min}}$ | Operating below AVC = shut down |
| "Long-run PC equilibrium?" | $P = \text{AC}_{\text{min}} = \text{MC}$ and $\pi = 0$ | Tangency condition |
| "How many units to sell for a target profit?" | $Q = (\text{FC} + \text{Target Profit}) / (P - \text{AVC})$ | Add target profit to numerator |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Break-Even Chart — Block Architecture of Cost & Revenue Flows

The BEP chart is a graph with **Sales/Output (₹ or units) on the X-axis** and **Cost/Revenue (₹) on the Y-axis**. The BEP is the intersection of the **Total Revenue (TR) line** and the **Total Cost (TC) line**. Below it lies the **Loss region**; above it lies the **Profit region**.

The schematic below maps the **functional architecture** of how cost lines, revenue lines, fixed cost, and the BEP interact — a Board-friendly visual.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Fixed Cost FC horizontal line starting at FC on Y axis"]
    nodeB["Variable Cost VC = AVC x Q line starting at origin with slope AVC"]
    nodeC["Total Cost TC = FC + VC line starts at FC intercept parallel to VC"]
    nodeD["Total Revenue TR = P x Q line starts at origin with slope P"]
    nodeE["INTERSECTION POINT BEP = TC = TR"]
    nodeF["LOSS REGION left of BEP where TR is less than TC"]
    nodeG["PROFIT REGION right of BEP where TR is greater than TC"]
    nodeH["Contribution Margin = TR minus VC at any Q"]
    nodeI["At BEP the cumulative Contribution equals FC"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeA --> nodeI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the chart:** The vertical distance between the TR line and the TC line is the **profit (or loss)**. To the **left** of the BEP, TC > TR → **LOSS**. To the **right** of the BEP, TR > TC → **PROFIT**. The fixed cost line is horizontal, and the gap between the TR line and the VC line is the **contribution margin**.

---

## 4.2 Perfect Competition — Industry and Firm Equilibrium

In perfect competition, the **industry** sets the market price, and the **firm** is a price taker. The block diagram below illustrates this two-tier architecture — exactly how KTU 2024 examiners want students to depict it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    indA["INDUSTRY LEVEL"]
    indB["Aggregate Demand Curve DD downward sloping"]
    indC["Aggregate Supply Curve SS upward sloping"]
    indD["Equilibrium Price P star set by DD equals SS"]
    indE["Total Market Quantity Q star"]
    indF["FIRM LEVEL Price Taker"]
    indG["Firm Demand Curve horizontal line at P star Perfectly Elastic"]
    indH["Firm Cost Curves MC upward then AC U shaped"]
    indI["Profit Maximisation MR equals MC where P star equals MC"]
    indJ["Long Run Equilibrium P equals AC min equals MC Profit is zero"]

    indA --> indB
    indA --> indC
    indB --> indD
    indC --> indD
    indD --> indE
    indD --> indF
    indF --> indG
    indF --> indH
    indG --> indI
    indH --> indI
    indI --> indJ
```

---

## 4.3 Short-Run vs. Long-Run Equilibrium — Decision Logic

This flowchart summarises how a perfectly competitive firm decides whether to **produce, shut down, or stay in the market**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["Firm in Perfect Competition given Market Price P"]
    q2{"Is P greater than AVC minimum"}
    q3["SHUT DOWN No production Incur only FC as loss"]
    q4{"Is P greater than AC minimum"}
    q5["SHORT RUN SUPER NORMAL PROFIT P greater than AC"]
    q6["NORMAL PROFIT ONLY Break even with economic profit zero"]
    q7["LONG RUN EQUILIBRIUM P equals AC min equals MC Firms earn normal profit only"]
    q8["SHORT RUN LOSS but continue operating since P greater than AVC"]
    q9["LONG RUN EXIT Firms leave the market Supply falls Price rises"]

    q1 --> q2
    q2 -- No --> q3
    q2 -- Yes --> q4
    q4 -- Yes --> q5
    q4 -- No --> q6
    q5 --> q7
    q6 --> q7
    q6 -- If Loss continues --> q8
    q8 --> q9
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory anchor for the Board exam:** In the **long run**, perfect competition yields **only normal profit** (zero economic profit). Any short-run super-normal profit attracts new entrants, increasing supply, lowering price, and eroding profit back to zero. This is the famous **"invisible hand"** of Adam Smith.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define Break-Even Point (BEP). Explain the concept of P/V ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the level of sales at which **Total Revenue equals Total Cost**, resulting in **zero profit and zero loss**. At this point, the entire fixed cost is recovered, and contribution exactly equals fixed cost.

The **P/V ratio (Profit/Volume ratio)** is the ratio of **contribution to sales**, expressed as a percentage:

$$\text{P/V ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 = \dfrac{P - \text{AVC}}{P} \times 100$$

A higher P/V ratio indicates a **higher margin of safety** and faster break-even recovery. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`
**List any four salient features of a perfectly competitive market.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Very large number of buyers and sellers** — no participant can influence price.
2. **Homogeneous products** — buyers are indifferent between sellers.
3. **Free entry and exit of firms** — ensures long-run normal profit.
4. **Perfect knowledge** — all buyers and sellers know prices, quality, and techniques.

(Any four features, 0.75 marks each = 3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A company manufactures a single product sold at ₹100 per unit. The variable cost per unit is ₹60, and the total fixed cost is ₹80,000 per month.
- (i) Calculate the **Break-Even Point in units** and **in sales value (₹)**.
- (ii) If the company desires a **target profit of ₹40,000 per month**, how many units must it sell?
- (iii) Calculate the **Margin of Safety** as a percentage if the actual sales are 3,000 units per month.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Perfect Competition**? Explain the **short-run and long-run equilibrium of a firm** under perfect competition with the help of a cost-revenue diagram.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

##### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- Selling price $P$ = ₹100/unit
- Variable cost per unit AVC = ₹60/unit
- Fixed cost FC = ₹80,000/month
- Target profit = ₹40,000
- Actual sales = 3,000 units

**Step 1 — Contribution per unit:**

$$c = P - \text{AVC} = 100 - 60 = ₹40 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 2 — P/V ratio:**

$$\text{P/V} = \dfrac{c}{P} = \dfrac{40}{100} = 0.40 \;\text{or}\; 40\%$$

**Step 3 — (i) BEP in Units:**

$$Q_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{c} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{40} = 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — BEP in Sales Value:**

$$S_{\text{BEP}} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{\text{P/V ratio}} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{0.40} = ₹2{,}00{,}000$$

**Cross-check:** $2{,}000 \text{ units} \times ₹100 = ₹2{,}00{,}000$ ✓

**Step 5 — (ii) Units for Target Profit of ₹40,000:**

$$Q = \dfrac{\text{FC} + \text{Target Profit}}{c} = \dfrac{80{,}000 + 40{,}000}{40} = \dfrac{1{,}20{,}000}{40} = 3{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Margin of Safety (units):**

$$\text{MOS} = Q_{\text{actual}} - Q_{\text{BEP}} = 3{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 1{,}000 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{MOS \%} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{3{,}000} \times 100 = 33.33\%$$

**Interpretation:** Sales can drop by up to 33.33% before the firm incurs a loss.

**(Part (a) full marks distributed: 2 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 7 marks)**

---

##### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Definition of Perfect Competition (2 marks):**

Perfect competition is a market structure characterised by a **very large number of buyers and sellers** dealing in **homogeneous products**, with **free entry and exit**, **perfect knowledge**, and **no government intervention**. Each firm is a **price taker** and faces a **perfectly elastic demand curve** at the equilibrium market price.

**Short-Run Equilibrium (2.5 marks):**

In the short run, the firm maximises profit where **MR = MC**. Since the firm is a price taker, $\text{MR} = P$.

- If $P > \text{AC}_{\text{min}}$ → **Supernormal profit** (per-unit profit $\times Q$).
- If $\text{AVC}_{\text{min}} < P < \text{AC}_{\text{min}}$ → **Loss**, but firm continues operating (covers variable cost + part of fixed cost).
- If $P < \text{AVC}_{\text{min}}$ → **Shut down** (loss = FC, which is the minimum possible loss).

**Long-Run Equilibrium (2.5 marks):**

Due to **free entry and exit**, supernormal profits attract new firms (increasing supply, lowering price) and losses drive firms out (decreasing supply, raising price). In the long run:

$$P = \text{AC}_{\text{min}} = \text{MC}$$

The firm's demand curve is **tangent to the minimum point of the AC curve**, yielding **zero economic profit** (only normal profit). The firm produces at the **most efficient scale** — a hallmark of long-run perfect competition.

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with one numerical example each:
- (i) Contribution margin
- (ii) Margin of Safety
- (iii) P/V ratio
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish between **Perfect Competition and Monopoly** in a tabular form. Why is a firm under perfect competition called a **'price taker'** and under monopoly a **'price maker'**?
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

##### Part (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Contribution Margin (2.5 marks):**

Contribution margin is the **excess of selling price over variable cost per unit**. It is the amount each unit contributes toward covering fixed costs and generating profit.

$$\text{Contribution per unit} = P - \text{AVC}$$

**Example:** If $P = ₹100$ and AVC = ₹60, contribution = ₹40 per unit.

**(ii) Margin of Safety (2.5 marks):**

The Margin of Safety (MOS) is the difference between **actual sales and break-even sales**. It measures the **buffer** by which sales can fall before the firm slips into a loss.

$$\text{MOS} = \text{Actual Sales} - S_{\text{BEP}}$$

**Example:** If actual sales = ₹5,00,000 and BEP sales = ₹3,00,000, MOS = ₹2,00,000.

**(iii) P/V Ratio (2 marks):**

The P/V (Profit/Volume) ratio is the **ratio of contribution to sales**, expressed as a percentage. It indicates the rate at which contribution is generated per rupee of sales.

$$\text{P/V ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$$

**Example:** Contribution = ₹40, Sales = ₹100 → P/V ratio = 40%.

---

##### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Distinction Table (5 marks):**

| Basis | Perfect Competition | Monopoly |
|-------|--------------------|----------|
| Number of sellers | Very large | Single seller |
| Nature of product | Homogeneous | Unique, no close substitutes |
| Price control | None — firm is a price taker | Full — firm is a price maker |
| Entry and exit | Free | Blocked (barriers to entry) |
| Demand curve facing the firm | Perfectly elastic (horizontal) | Downward sloping (market demand itself) |
| Profit in long run | Only normal profit | Can earn supernormal profit (patent, etc.) |
| Selling costs | Nil | Significant (advertising, branding) |
| Example | Agricultural produce (wheat, rice) | Indian Railways (single provider) |

**Why price taker vs. price maker — Explanation (2 marks):**

A perfectly competitive firm is a **price taker** because its output is so small relative to the total market that any attempt to charge a price above the market equilibrium would result in **zero sales** (buyers will switch to identical products sold cheaper by other firms). The firm thus **accepts the market-determined price** as given.

A monopolist is a **price maker** because it is the **sole producer** of a product with no close substitutes. By varying its output, the monopolist can **influence the market price**. It faces the **entire market demand curve**, which is downward sloping, and chooses the price-quantity combination that maximises profit (where $\text{MR} = \text{MC}$).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing AVC with VC:** Variable cost per unit is AVC. Total VC = AVC × Q. Board examiners deduct 1 mark if you write "VC" instead of "AVC" in the BEP formula.
> 2. **Forgetting to add target profit:** When the question asks "how many units for a target profit of ₹X", many students compute $Q = \text{FC} / c$ and stop. Always add the target profit to the numerator.
> 3. **Mixing up P/V ratio form:** The P/V ratio is **Contribution / Sales**, not **Profit / Sales**. Profit = Contribution − FC. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 4. **In perfect competition, never draw a downward-sloping demand curve for the firm.** The firm's demand curve is a **horizontal straight line** at the market price. This is the most repeated mistake.
> 5. **Long-run PC equilibrium requires $P = \text{AC}_{\text{min}} = \text{MC}$.** Writing only $P = \text{MC}$ is incomplete and will cost at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Units must accompany numerical answers** (e.g., "2,000 units" not just "2,000").
> 7. **In MCQ/short questions on BEP, do not write "BEP is where profit is zero" alone.** Examiners expect: "BEP is the sales level at which TR = TC, and the contribution exactly equals fixed cost."

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: BEP & Perfect Competition**

- **BEP Definition:** Sales volume where **TR = TC**, profit = 0.
- **BEP in units:** $\text{FC} / (P - \text{AVC})$.
- **BEP in sales ₹:** $\text{FC} / \text{P/V ratio}$.
- **P/V ratio:** $\text{Contribution} / \text{Sales}$ (in %).
- **Margin of Safety:** $\text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$ (in units or ₹ or %).
- **Profit formula:** $\pi = (\text{P/V ratio}) \times \text{Actual Sales} - \text{FC}$.
- **Target units:** $(\text{FC} + \text{Target Profit}) / (P - \text{AVC})$.
- **Shut-down condition:** $P < \text{AVC}_{\text{min}}$.
- **Perfect competition = price taker**; **Monopoly = price maker**.
- **Firm's demand curve in PC:** horizontal line at market price (perfectly elastic).
- **Firm's profit maximisation in PC:** $\text{MR} = \text{MC}$ (with $\text{MR} = P$).
- **Short-run PC outcomes:** profit, loss, or shut-down based on $P$ vs. $\text{AC}$ and $\text{AVC}$.
- **Long-run PC equilibrium:** $P = \text{AC}_{\text{min}} = \text{MC}$ and economic profit = 0.
- **Free entry and exit** in PC drives long-run profit to normal level.
- **BEP assumptions:** linear VC, constant P, single product, constant sales mix.
- **Engineering applications:** product launch, plant capacity, make-or-buy, pricing, cost control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monopoly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT346 - ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monopoly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Monopoly** is a market structure characterized by a **single seller** (called the monopolist) who trades a **commodity that has no close substitutes**. The monopolist is a **price maker** (not a price taker), and faces a **downward-sloping demand curve** because the firm *is* the industry itself.

Formally, in the words of Prof. Joan Robinson:
> *"Monopoly is a market situation in which there is only one seller of a particular commodity, and entry into the field is closed."*

A more rigorous KTU-aligned definition is:

> A **monopolist** is the sole producer of a good with **no close substitutes**, where the entry of new firms is blocked by strong **barriers to entry**, giving the firm significant control over the price and quantity supplied in the market.

**Mathematical Representation (Inverse Demand):**

$$P = a - bQ \quad \text{where} \quad a > 0,\ b > 0$$

**Total Revenue (TR):**

$$TR = P \times Q = aQ - bQ^2$$

**Average Revenue (AR):**

$$AR = \frac{TR}{Q} = P = a - bQ$$

**Marginal Revenue (MR):**

$$MR = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = a - 2bQ$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A monopolist always faces a downward-sloping demand curve (AR curve). Since the price the firm charges is also the industry's price, **AR = P = Demand**. This is the single most important distinction from perfect competition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine a small, isolated hill-station town connected to the rest of the world by only one winding mountain road. There is exactly **one grocery store** in the town that sells rice, sugar, and oil. The owner of that store knows:

1. **No other shop can open easily** — the road is too narrow, and the government has given him a license.
2. **He sets the price** — if he raises the price of rice from ₹40 to ₹55, customers will grumble but still buy, because there is nowhere else to go.
3. **He knows each customer's need** — he may even charge different prices to a wealthy tourist versus a poor local worker.

This store is a **monopolist**.

| Real-World Analogy Element | Monopoly Concept |
| :--- | :--- |
| Single grocery store in the hill-station | **Single Seller** (one firm in the market) |
| Unique goods (specialty rice, local honey) | **No Close Substitutes** |
| Difficult mountain road + license | **Barriers to Entry** |
| He can charge ₹55 if he wants | **Price Maker** (sets the price) |
| Tourist vs. local charged differently | **Price Discrimination** |

## 1.3 Key Features of Monopoly (KTU Frequently Tested List)

> [!NOTE]
> These **Seven Features** are direct KTU Board Exam short-answer and 3-mark favorites. Memorize them verbatim.

1. **Single Seller** — The monopolist constitutes the entire industry.
2. **No Close Substitutes** — The product is unique in nature, quality, or branding.
3. **Price Maker** — The firm has the discretion to set the price of its product.
4. **Barriers to Entry** — Strong legal, technological, or natural barriers prevent new firms from entering.
5. **Downward-Sloping Demand Curve** — The firm faces the market demand curve itself.
6. **Profit Maximization Point** — Equilibrium occurs where **MR = MC**, and at this point, **P > MC** (a key welfare loss indicator).
7. **Possibility of Price Discrimination** — Since the monopolist controls supply, he can charge different prices to different buyers for the same good.

## 1.4 Types of Monopoly

| Type | Description | KTU Keyword |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pure / Simple Monopoly** | Single seller, single product, no price discrimination | Most basic model |
| **Discriminating Monopoly** | Charges different prices for the same product from different buyers | Price discrimination |
| **Natural Monopoly** | Arises due to economies of scale; one firm can serve the entire market at a lower cost (e.g., railways, water supply) | Economies of scale |
| **Legal Monopoly** | Granted by law/government (e.g., patents, copyrights) | Patent rights |
| **Technological Monopoly** | Based on superior technology or trade secrets (e.g., Google's search algorithm) | Innovation-based |
| **Government Monopoly** | State-owned monopoly (e.g., Indian Railways historically) | Public sector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deadweight Loss:** A monopoly causes a **deadweight loss (DWL)** to society because the monopolist restricts output to keep prices high. The **Allocative Efficiency Condition (P = MC)** is violated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Revenue Curves of a Monopolist — The Core Theory

For a monopolist, the relationship between **AR, MR, and Price Elasticity of Demand (Eₐ)** is foundational. Let us break this down step-by-step.

### 2.1.1 The AR-MR Relationship (Linear Demand Case)

For a linear inverse demand function:

$$P = a - bQ$$

**Step 1 — Total Revenue:**

$$TR = P \times Q = (a - bQ)Q = aQ - bQ^2$$

**Step 2 — Average Revenue:**

$$AR = \frac{TR}{Q} = \frac{aQ - bQ^2}{Q} = a - bQ$$

$$\boxed{AR = P = a - bQ}$$

> This confirms that the AR curve *is* the demand curve itself.

**Step 3 — Marginal Revenue:**

$$MR = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = \frac{d}{dQ}(aQ - bQ^2) = a - 2bQ$$

$$\boxed{MR = a - 2bQ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The MR curve has the **same intercept ($a$)** as the AR (demand) curve, but **twice the slope** ($-2b$ instead of $-b$). Geometrically, the MR curve lies *below* the AR curve and is **steeper**.

### 2.1.2 Elasticity and the MR–AR Relationship

There is a fundamental KTU-graded relationship between **AR, MR, and Price Elasticity of Demand (Eₐ)**:

$$\boxed{MR = AR \left(1 - \frac{1}{\vert E_d \vert}\right)}$$

**Derivation Hint (for KTU derivations):**

$$MR = P\left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad e = \vert E_d \vert$$

**Key Interpretations:**

| Value of $\vert E_d \vert$ | MR Value | Region of Operation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ (Elastic) | $MR > 0$ | Upper portion of demand curve |
| $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ (Unit Elastic) | $MR = 0$ | Midpoint of demand curve — **TR is maximum** |
| $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ (Inelastic) | $MR < 0$ | Lower portion of demand curve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A profit-maximizing monopolist will **always** operate in the **elastic region** of the demand curve. In the inelastic region, lowering the price *increases* total revenue and *decreases* total cost (since Q is being reduced relative to the inelastic drop), so losses mount.

### 2.1.3 Profit Maximization Equilibrium

A monopolist maximizes profit where:

$$\boxed{MR = MC}$$

**Subject to the second-order condition:**

$$\frac{d(MR)}{dQ} < \frac{d(MC)}{dQ} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{d^2(\pi)}{dQ^2} < 0$$

At equilibrium:

- **Profit-maximizing Output** is determined by **MR = MC** → find $Q^*$.
- **Profit-maximizing Price** is read from the demand curve at $Q^*$ → $P^* = a - bQ^*$.
- **Maximum Profit** is given by: $\pi^* = (P^* - ATC) \times Q^*$.

### 2.1.4 Supply Curve of a Monopolist — A Critical KTU Concept

> [!WARNING]
> **The monopolist does NOT have a well-defined supply curve!**
>
> Unlike a perfectly competitive firm, a monopolist can charge different prices for the same quantity (due to price discrimination) or supply the same quantity at different prices. Therefore, there is **no unique one-to-one mapping** between price and quantity. This is a classic KTU short-answer question.

## 2.2 Price Discrimination under Monopoly

**Price Discrimination** is the practice of charging **different prices from different buyers** for the **same good**, where the price differences are **not due to cost differences**.

### 2.2.1 Conditions (Prof. A.C. Pigou's Three Conditions)

1. **Market Power** — The seller must be a price maker (i.e., a monopolist).
2. **Market Segmentation** — The market must be dividable into sub-markets (e.g., domestic vs. industrial, peak vs. off-peak).
3. **No Resale** — Buyers in the cheap segment must not be able to resell to the expensive segment (prevention of arbitrage).

### 2.2.2 Degrees of Price Discrimination

| Degree | Name | Description | Real Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **First Degree** | **Perfect / Pure Discrimination** | The monopolist charges each consumer their **maximum willingness to pay** (the full consumer surplus is captured by the firm) | Personalized medical treatment, custom auctions |
| **Second Degree** | **Quantity Discrimination** | Price varies with the **quantity purchased** (bulk discounts, version pricing) | Electricity slabs, mobile data plans |
| **Third Degree** | **Market Segmentation** | Different prices for **different consumer groups** (students, senior citizens, peak hours) | Student discount, senior citizen fare, peak-hour tolls |

### 2.2.3 Profit-Maximizing Condition under Third-Degree Price Discrimination

The monopolist should allocate output such that the **marginal revenue in every sub-market equals the common marginal cost**:

$$\boxed{MR_1 = MR_2 = MR_3 = \dots = MC}$$

## 2.3 Regulation of Monopoly

To protect consumer welfare, governments often regulate monopolies. The two most common KTU-tested methods are:

### 2.3.1 Marginal Cost Pricing (Idealistic)

$$P = MC$$

- **Advantage:** Achieves **allocative efficiency** (no DWL).
- **Disadvantage:** If AC is declining (natural monopoly), $MC < AC$, leading to **losses** for the firm. The government must subsidize.

### 2.3.2 Average Cost Pricing (Fair-Return / Break-Even)

$$P = AC$$

- **Advantage:** The firm earns only a **normal profit** (zero economic profit). The firm does not lose money, and consumers are charged a "fair" price.
- **Disadvantage:** There is still a deadweight loss (since $P = AC > MC$), but smaller than an unregulated monopoly.

## 2.4 The Real-World Utility in Engineering

Engineers in product development, technology firms, and infrastructure design frequently encounter monopoly concepts:

1. **Patents and Intellectual Property** — A patented technology gives the firm temporary monopoly power. The price-setting rule $MR = MC$ informs R&D pricing decisions.
2. **Utility Services (Electricity, Water)** — These are often natural monopolies, regulated using the $P = AC$ fair-return rule.
3. **Platform Economics (Network Effects)** — Tech platforms like operating systems or social networks display monopolistic tendencies due to network externalities.
4. **Capacity and Output Decisions** — Engineering managers use the MR = MC rule to decide optimal production capacity.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary Condition / Note |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Inverse Demand | $P = a - bQ$ | $a, b > 0$ |
| 2 | Total Revenue | $TR = aQ - bQ^2$ | Quadratic, concave |
| 3 | Average Revenue | $AR = a - bQ$ | $AR = P$ (Demand curve) |
| 4 | Marginal Revenue | $MR = a - 2bQ$ | Same intercept, twice the slope |
| 5 | MR-AR-Elasticity Relation | $MR = AR\left(1 - \frac{1}{\vert E_d \vert}\right)$ | Always works |
| 6 | Profit Maximization | $MR = MC$ | Second-order: slope of MC > slope of MR |
| 7 | Equilibrium Price | $P^* = a - bQ^*$ | Read from demand curve at $Q^*$ |
| 8 | Maximum Profit | $\pi^* = (P^* - ATC) \times Q^*$ | If $P^* > ATC$, supernormal profit |
| 9 | MC Pricing Regulation | $P = MC$ | Ideal but causes losses for natural monopoly |
| 10 | AC Pricing Regulation | $P = AC$ | Fair-return pricing, normal profit |
| 11 | Discrimination Equilibrium | $MR_1 = MR_2 = \dots = MC$ | Third-degree discrimination |
| 12 | TR Max Condition | $MR = 0$ | Implies $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Profit-Maximizing Output and Price

**Given:**
- Inverse Demand: $P = 50 - 2Q$
- Total Cost: $TC = 100 + 10Q + Q^2$

**Step 1 — Compute Total Revenue (TR):**

$$TR = P \times Q = (50 - 2Q)Q = 50Q - 2Q^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Marginal Revenue (MR):**

$$MR = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = \frac{d}{dQ}(50Q - 2Q^2) = 50 - 4Q$$

**Step 3 — Compute Marginal Cost (MC):**

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = \frac{d}{dQ}(100 + 10Q + Q^2) = 10 + 2Q$$

**Step 4 — Apply Profit Maximization Condition MR = MC:**

$$50 - 4Q = 10 + 2Q$$

$$50 - 10 = 2Q + 4Q$$

$$40 = 6Q$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = \frac{40}{6} = 6.67 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Profit-Maximizing Price from the Demand Curve:**

$$P^* = 50 - 2(6.67)$$

$$P^* = 50 - 13.33$$

$$\boxed{P^* = 36.67}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Total Revenue and Total Cost at Equilibrium:**

$$TR^* = P^* \times Q^* = 36.67 \times 6.67 = 244.59$$

$$TC^* = 100 + 10(6.67) + (6.67)^2 = 100 + 66.7 + 44.49 = 211.19$$

**Step 7 — Compute Maximum Profit:**

$$\pi^* = TR^* - TC^* = 244.59 - 211.19$$

$$\boxed{\pi^* = 33.40 \text{ (supernormal profit)}}$$

**Step 8 — Second-Order Verification:**

$$\frac{d(MR)}{dQ} = -4 \quad ; \quad \frac{d(MC)}{dQ} = 2$$

Since $\frac{d(MR)}{dQ} = -4 < \frac{d(MC)}{dQ} = 2$, the second-order condition is satisfied — this is a **maximum**, not a minimum.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: MR-AR-Elasticity Relationship

**Starting Point:**

$$TR = P \times Q$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate TR with respect to Q (using the product rule):**

$$\frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = P \cdot \frac{dQ}{dQ} + Q \cdot \frac{dP}{dQ} = P + Q \cdot \frac{dP}{dQ}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the definition of Price Elasticity of Demand:**

$$E_d = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{dQ} = \frac{1}{\frac{dQ}{dP}} = \frac{P}{E_d \cdot Q}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $\frac{dP}{dQ}$ into Step 1:**

$$MR = P + Q \cdot \left(\frac{P}{E_d \cdot Q}\right) = P + \frac{P}{E_d} = P\left(1 + \frac{1}{E_d}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Final Result (using $\vert E_d \vert$ for elasticity magnitude):**

$$\boxed{MR = P \left(1 - \frac{1}{\vert E_d \vert}\right)}$$

> Note: We use $\vert E_d \vert$ because by convention, price elasticity of demand is reported as a positive number (even though mathematically negative).

## 3.3 Numerical Example: Comparing Monopoly vs. Perfect Competition

**Same Demand:** $P = 100 - Q$ (for both markets)
**Same Cost Function:** $TC = 10Q$

### 3.3.1 Perfect Competition Outcome

In perfect competition, firms produce where $P = MC$.

**Step 1 — Compute MC:**

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 10$$

**Step 2 — Apply $P = MC$:**

$$100 - Q = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{PC} = 90, \quad P_{PC} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Total Revenue and Profit:**

$$TR_{PC} = 10 \times 90 = 900, \quad TC_{PC} = 10 \times 90 = 900, \quad \pi_{PC} = 0$$

### 3.3.2 Monopoly Outcome

**Step 1 — Compute TR and MR:**

$$TR = (100 - Q)Q = 100Q - Q^2, \quad MR = 100 - 2Q$$

**Step 2 — Apply $MR = MC$:**

$$100 - 2Q = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_M = 45$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Monopoly Price:**

$$P_M = 100 - 45 = 55$$

**Step 4 — Compute Monopoly Profit:**

$$TR_M = 55 \times 45 = 2475, \quad TC_M = 10 \times 45 = 450, \quad \pi_M = 2025$$

### 3.3.3 Comparison Table

| Metric | Perfect Competition | Monopoly | Interpretation |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Output ($Q$) | 90 | 45 | Monopolist restricts output by **50%** |
| Price ($P$) | 10 | 55 | Monopolist charges **5.5× higher price** |
| Total Profit ($\pi$) | 0 | 2025 | Monopolist earns **supernormal profit** |
| Consumer Surplus | $\frac{1}{2}(100-10)(90) = 4050$ | $\frac{1}{2}(100-55)(45) = 1012.5$ | CS drops by **~75%** |
| Deadweight Loss | 0 | $\frac{1}{2}(55-10)(90-45) = 1012.5$ | Significant welfare loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The DWL triangle represents the lost economic surplus due to the monopolist's output restriction. This is the social cost of monopoly power and is a frequent 14-mark derivation question.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: Price Discrimination Across Two Markets

**Given:**
- Demand in Market 1: $P_1 = 80 - 4Q_1$
- Demand in Market 2: $P_2 = 100 - 5Q_2$
- Common Cost Function: $TC = 20 + 10Q_{\text{total}}$, where $Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2$

**Step 1 — Compute MR in Each Market:**

$$MR_1 = 80 - 8Q_1, \quad MR_2 = 100 - 10Q_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Common MC:**

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ_{\text{total}}} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Apply Equilibrium Condition $MR_1 = MR_2 = MC$:**

**From $MR_1 = MC$:**

$$80 - 8Q_1 = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_1 = 8.75$$

**From $MR_2 = MC$:**

$$100 - 10Q_2 = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_2 = 9$$

**Step 4 — Compute Equilibrium Prices:**

$$P_1 = 80 - 4(8.75) = 80 - 35 = 45$$

$$P_2 = 100 - 5(9) = 100 - 45 = 55$$

**Step 5 — Verify and Compute Profit:**

$$TR = 45(8.75) + 55(9) = 393.75 + 495 = 888.75$$

$$TC = 20 + 10(8.75 + 9) = 20 + 177.5 = 197.5$$

$$\pi = 888.75 - 197.5 = 691.25$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the price differential:** The monopolist charges ₹55 in Market 2 versus ₹45 in Market 1, even though the marginal cost is the same (₹10) in both markets. This is the essence of **third-degree price discrimination**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Regulation Outcomes

| Pricing Rule | Condition | Allocative Efficiency | Firm's Profit | Real Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unregulated Monopoly** | $MR = MC$ | ❌ Violated | Supernormal | Most private monopolies |
| **Marginal Cost Pricing** | $P = MC$ | ✅ Achieved | Loss (if AC > MC) | Theoretical ideal |
| **Average Cost Pricing** | $P = AC$ | ❌ Partially violated | Normal profit | Public utilities (e.g., electricity boards) |
| **Two-Part Tariff** | $T = MC \cdot Q + F$ | ✅ Achieved (efficient Q) | Captures consumer surplus | Mobile phone plans, gym memberships |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Monopolist's Decision-Making Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Market Demand Curve: P = a - bQ] --> B[Total Revenue: TR = P × Q]
    B --> C[Average Revenue: AR = P = a - bQ]
    B --> D[MR = dTR / dQ = a - 2bQ]
    E[Cost Function: TC = FC + VC] --> F[MC = dTC / dQ]
    D --> G{Profit Maximization}
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply: MR = MC]
    H --> I[Solve for Q*]
    I --> J[Read Price P* from Demand Curve]
    J --> K[Compute Profit: π = P* - ATC × Q*]
    K --> L{Decision Point}
    L --> M[If π > 0: Continue Production]
    L --> N[If π < 0: Consider Shutdown]
    L --> O[If π = 0: Normal Profit]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Types of Monopoly Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Monopoly] --> A[Pure Monopoly]
    M --> B[Discriminating Monopoly]
    M --> C[Natural Monopoly]
    M --> D[Legal Monopoly]
    M --> E[Technological Monopoly]
    M --> F[Government Monopoly]
    B --> B1[1st Degree: Perfect Discrimination]
    B --> B2[2nd Degree: Quantity Slabs]
    B --> B3[3rd Degree: Market Segmentation]
    C --> C1[Railways]
    C --> C2[Water Supply]
    C --> C3[Electricity Transmission]
    D --> D1[Patents]
    D --> D2[Copyrights]
    D --> D3[Trademarks]
    E --> E1[Microsoft Windows]
    E --> E2[Google Search Algorithm]
    F --> F1[Defence Production]
    F --> F2[Atomic Energy]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Equilibrium Comparison (Monopoly vs. Perfect Competition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[Perfect Competition: P = MC] --> QPC[High Output Qpc]
    PC --> PPC[Low Price Ppc]
    PC --> ProfPC[Zero Economic Profit]
    PC --> CSPC[Maximum Consumer Surplus]
    PC --> DWLP[Zero Deadweight Loss]
    MON[Monopoly: MR = MC] --> QMON[Low Output Qm]
    MON --> PMON[High Price Pm]
    MON --> ProfMON[Supernormal Profit]
    MON --> CSMON[Lower Consumer Surplus]
    MON --> DWL[Positive Deadweight Loss]
    QPC -.- QMON
    PPC -.- PMON
    CSPC -.- CSMON
    DWLP -.- DWL
    ProfPC -.- ProfMON
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Price Discrimination Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MK1 as Market 1
    participant MK2 as Market 2
    participant M as Monopolist
    participant C as Common Cost
    M ->> MK1: Estimate Demand P1
    M ->> MK2: Estimate Demand P2
    M ->> M: Compute MR1 and MR2
    M ->> C: Compute Common MC
    M ->> M: Apply MR1 = MR2 = MC
    M ->> MK1: Set P1, supply Q1
    M ->> MK2: Set P2, supply Q2
    Note over M: Higher price charged in more inelastic market
    M ->> M: Total Profit = TR1 + TR2 - TC
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Graph: Revenue Curves and Equilibrium

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Revenue_Curves [Revenue Curves of a Monopolist]
        A1[AR = a - bQ] --> A2[MR = a - 2bQ]
        A2 --> A3[MR has same intercept as AR, twice the slope]
        A2 --> A4[MR lies below AR for all Q > 0]
        A2 --> A5[MR = 0 when Q = a / 2b]
        A2 --> A6[TR is maximum at MR = 0]
    end
    subgraph Equilibrium [Profit Maximization Equilibrium]
        B1[Set MR = MC] --> B2[Find Q from intersection]
        B2 --> B3[Read P from AR curve at Q]
        B3 --> B4[Compute profit using ATC at Q]
    end
    subgraph Regulation [Government Regulation]
        C1[P = MC] --> C2[Allocative efficiency, possible loss]
        C1 --> C3[Use if AC is constant]
        C4[P = AC] --> C5[Normal profit, slight inefficiency]
        C4 --> C6[Use for natural monopoly]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions require **precise, bulleted, 3-4 line answers**. Avoid long paragraphs. Each answer should be 50–80 words.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define Monopoly. List any four features of a monopoly market.**

**Model Answer:**

**Monopoly** is a market structure in which there is a **single seller** of a product that has **no close substitutes**, and strong **barriers to entry** prevent new firms from entering.

**Four Features:**

1. **Single Seller** — The monopolist *is* the industry.
2. **Price Maker** — The firm has the power to set the price of its product.
3. **Barriers to Entry** — Legal, technological, or natural barriers block new entrants.
4. **Price Discrimination** — The monopolist can charge different prices to different buyers for the same good.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Explain the relationship between AR, MR, and elasticity of demand.**

**Model Answer:**

The relationship is given by the formula:

$$MR = AR \left(1 - \frac{1}{\vert E_d \vert}\right)$$

where $E_d$ is the price elasticity of demand.

- When $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ (elastic demand), **MR > 0** → TR increases as Q increases.
- When $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ (unit elastic), **MR = 0** → TR is at its maximum.
- When $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ (inelastic demand), **MR < 0** → TR decreases as Q increases.

A profit-maximizing monopolist always operates in the **elastic region** of the demand curve.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Long Answer with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. The full question has an **internal choice** — you must answer **either Option A or Option B**.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the equilibrium output and price for a monopolist from first principles. Show that at equilibrium, **P > MC**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The demand for a monopolist's product is $P = 200 - 4Q$, and the total cost function is $TC = 500 + 20Q + 2Q^2$. Find:
- (i) The profit-maximizing output and price
- (ii) The maximum profit earned
- (iii) The elasticity of demand at the equilibrium point **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the inverse demand function:**

$$P = a - bQ$$

**Step 2 — Compute TR:**

$$TR = P \times Q = aQ - bQ^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute MR:**

$$MR = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} = a - 2bQ$$

**Step 4 — Compute MC:**

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Profit Maximization Condition $MR = MC$:**

$$a - 2bQ = MC(Q) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Solve for } Q^*$$

**Step 6 — Read Price from Demand Curve:**

$$P^* = a - bQ^*$$

**Step 7 — Show P > MC:**

For a linear demand function, $MR = a - 2bQ < a - bQ = AR = P$ for any $Q > 0$.

At equilibrium $MR = MC$, so:

$$MC = a - 2bQ^* < a - bQ^* = P^*$$

$$\therefore P^* > MC \quad \blacksquare$$

This proves the **fundamental monopoly result**: price exceeds marginal cost, leading to **allocative inefficiency**.

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b):**

**Given:** $P = 200 - 4Q$ and $TC = 500 + 20Q + 2Q^2$

**Step 1 — Compute TR and MR:**

$$TR = (200 - 4Q)Q = 200Q - 4Q^2$$

$$MR = 200 - 8Q$$

**Step 2 — Compute MC:**

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 20 + 4Q$$

**Step 3 — Apply $MR = MC$:**

$$200 - 8Q = 20 + 4Q$$

$$180 = 12Q$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = 15 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Equilibrium Price:**

$$P^* = 200 - 4(15) = 200 - 60$$

$$\boxed{P^* = 140}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Maximum Profit:**

$$TR^* = 140 \times 15 = 2100$$

$$TC^* = 500 + 20(15) + 2(15)^2 = 500 + 300 + 450 = 1250$$

$$\boxed{\pi^* = TR^* - TC^* = 2100 - 1250 = 850}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Elasticity at Equilibrium:**

Using point elasticity formula $E_d = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}$:

From $P = 200 - 4Q$, we have $Q = 50 - \frac{P}{4}$, so:

$$\frac{dQ}{dP} = -\frac{1}{4}$$

At $P^* = 140$ and $Q^* = 15$:

$$E_d = -\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{140}{15} = -\frac{35}{15} = -2.33$$

$$\boxed{\vert E_d \vert = 2.33 \text{ (elastic demand at equilibrium)}}$$

This confirms the monopolist is operating in the **elastic region** of the demand curve.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** What is price discrimination? Explain the three degrees of price discrimination with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monopolist sells in two markets with the following demand functions:
- Market 1: $P_1 = 60 - 2Q_1$
- Market 2: $P_2 = 80 - 3Q_2$

The total cost function is $TC = 100 + 10Q$, where $Q = Q_1 + Q_2$.

Determine:
- (i) The profit-maximizing output in each market
- (ii) The price charged in each market
- (iii) The total profit earned **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a):**

**Definition:**

> **Price Discrimination** is the practice of charging **different prices from different buyers** for **essentially the same good**, where the price differences are **not justified by cost differences**.

**Conditions (Pigou's Three Conditions):**

1. **Market Power** — The seller must be a price maker.
2. **Market Segmentation** — The market must be divisible into identifiable sub-markets.
3. **No Resale (No Arbitrage)** — Buyers in the cheap market must not be able to resell in the expensive market.

**Three Degrees:**

| Degree | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **First Degree (Perfect)** | Each consumer is charged their **maximum willingness to pay**. The entire consumer surplus is captured by the seller. | Personalized medical consultation, art auctions |
| **Second Degree (Quantity)** | Price varies with the **quantity purchased**. Larger quantities may be cheaper per unit. | Bulk discounts, electricity slab rates |
| **Third Degree (Market)** | Different prices are charged to **different consumer groups** based on identifiable characteristics. | Student discounts, senior citizen fares, peak vs. off-peak pricing |

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute MR in Each Market:**

$$MR_1 = 60 - 4Q_1$$

$$MR_2 = 80 - 6Q_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Common MC:**

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Apply Equilibrium Condition $MR_1 = MR_2 = MC$:**

**From $MR_1 = MC$:**

$$60 - 4Q_1 = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4Q_1 = 50$$

$$\boxed{Q_1 = 12.5 \text{ units}}$$

**From $MR_2 = MC$:**

$$80 - 6Q_2 = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 6Q_2 = 70$$

$$\boxed{Q_2 = 11.67 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Equilibrium Prices:**

$$P_1 = 60 - 2(12.5) = 60 - 25 = 35$$

$$\boxed{P_1 = 35}$$

$$P_2 = 80 - 3(11.67) = 80 - 35 = 45$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 45}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Total Revenue and Total Cost:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 12.5 + 11.67 = 24.17$$

$$TR = (35)(12.5) + (45)(11.67) = 437.5 + 525.15 = 962.65$$

$$TC = 100 + 10(24.17) = 100 + 241.7 = 341.7$$

**Step 6 — Compute Total Profit:**

$$\boxed{\pi = 962.65 - 341.7 = 620.95}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the price differential:** The monopolist charges ₹45 in Market 2 versus ₹35 in Market 1, despite the marginal cost being ₹10 in both markets. This is because **Market 2 has a more inelastic demand** (lower price sensitivity), allowing the monopolist to charge a higher price.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions (Examiner-Verified):**
>
> 1. **Confusing AR with Demand:** Students often write $AR = \text{Demand}$, but the correct statement is **$AR = P = \text{Inverse Demand}$**. The AR curve *is* the demand curve plotted in the $(Q, P)$ plane. (Lose 0.5–1 mark)
>
> 2. **Writing "MR = MC and then P = MC":** This is the **single most common error**. In monopoly, $MR = MC$ for output, but the **price is read from the AR (demand) curve**, NOT from the MR curve. The price will be **higher than MC**. (Lose 1–2 marks)
>
> 3. **Forgetting Second-Order Conditions:** When asked for profit maximization, KTU examiners expect verification that $\frac{d^2(\pi)}{dQ^2} < 0$ (or equivalently, that the slope of MC exceeds the slope of MR at equilibrium). (Lose 0.5 mark)
>
> 4. **Saying "Monopolist has a supply curve":** This is **incorrect**. A monopolist does *not* have a unique supply curve. (Lose 0.5 mark)
>
> 5. **Mixing up the MR formula:** Students sometimes write $MR = a - bQ$ instead of $MR = a - 2bQ$. The slope of MR is **always twice the slope of AR**. (Lose 0.5 mark)
>
> 6. **Forgetting to verify the second-order condition in price discrimination problems:** You must show $MR_1 = MR_2 = MC$ AND verify that the MR curves are downward-sloping (so it's a true maximum). (Lose 0.5 mark)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam!)**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Monopoly** = Single seller + unique product + barriers to entry.
- ☐ **Monopolist is a price maker**, not a price taker.
- ☐ A monopolist **does not have a well-defined supply curve**.

### The 7 Features
- ☐ Single Seller
- ☐ No Close Substitutes
- ☐ Price Maker
- ☐ Strong Barriers to Entry
- ☐ Downward-Sloping Demand Curve (AR = P)
- ☐ Equilibrium at **MR = MC** (and **P > MC**)
- ☐ Possibility of **Price Discrimination**

### Critical Formulas
- ☐ $TR = aQ - bQ^2$
- ☐ $AR = a - bQ$
- ☐ $MR = a - 2bQ$ (twice the slope of AR)
- ☐ $MR = AR\left(1 - \frac{1}{\vert E_d \vert}\right)$
- ☐ Profit Max: **$MR = MC$** (NOT $P = MC$)
- ☐ Price Discrimination: $MR_1 = MR_2 = \dots = MC$

### Equilibrium Workflow
- ☐ **Step 1:** Compute TR → MR.
- ☐ **Step 2:** Compute MC.
- ☐ **Step 3:** Set MR = MC → solve for $Q^*$.
- ☐ **Step 4:** Read $P^*$ from the AR (demand) curve.
- ☐ **Step 5:** Compute profit: $\pi = (P^* - ATC) \times Q^*$.
- ☐ **Step 6:** Verify $P^* > MC$ (allocative inefficiency).

### Regulation Rules
- ☐ $P = MC$ → Allocatively efficient but may cause losses.
- ☐ $P = AC$ → Normal profit (fair-return pricing).

### Price Discrimination
- ☐ Requires: Market Power + Market Segmentation + No Resale.
- ☐ Three degrees: 1st (perfect), 2nd (quantity), 3rd (market segmentation).
- ☐ Higher price is charged in the more **inelastic** market.

### Comparison Points (Frequently Asked)
- ☐ Monopoly restricts output and raises price compared to perfect competition.
- ☐ Monopoly causes **deadweight loss** to society.
- ☐ Consumer surplus is **lower** under monopoly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
