### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**"A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects, Project A and Project B. The relevant data is given below. Use NPV and BCR methods to determine which project should be selected. Cost of capital = 10%."**

| Year | Project A (₹) | Project B (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (Initial Investment) | 1,00,000 | 1,20,000 |
| 1 | 30,000 | 40,000 |
| 2 | 35,000 | 45,000 |
| 3 | 40,000 | 50,000 |
| 4 | 45,000 | 40,000 |
| 5 | 50,000 | 30,000 |

**[14 Marks]**

#### Sub-Part (a) — NPV Computation [7 Marks]

**Discount factors at 10%:**

| Year | DF = 1/(1.10)^t |
|---|---|
| 1 | 0.9091 |
| 2 | 0.8264 |
| 3 | 0.7513 |
| 4 | 0.6830 |
| 5 | 0.6209 |

**Project A — Discounted Cash Flows:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 30{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 27{,}273 \\
PV_2 &= 35{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 28{,}924 \\
PV_3 &= 40{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 30{,}052 \\
PV_4 &= 45{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 30{,}735 \\
PV_5 &= 50{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 31{,}045 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Total PV of inflows (A) = ₹1,48,029

$$
NPV_A = 1{,}48{,}029 - 1{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{+₹48{,}029}
$$

**Project B — Discounted Cash Flows:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 40{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 36{,}364 \\
PV_2 &= 45{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 37{,}188 \\
PV_3 &= 50{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 37{,}565 \\
PV_4 &= 40{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 27{,}320 \\
PV_5 &= 30{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 18{,}627 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Total PV of inflows (B) = ₹1,57,064

$$
NPV_B = 1{,}57{,}064 - 1{,}20{,}000 = \boxed{+₹37{,}064}
$$

#### Sub-Part (b) — BCR Computation and Decision [7 Marks]

$$
BCR_A = \frac{PV \text{ of Inflows}}{PV \text{ of Outflows}} = \frac{1{,}48{,}029}{1{,}00{,}000} = \boxed{1.48}
$$

$$
BCR_B = \frac{1{,}57{,}064}{1{,}20{,}000} = \boxed{1.31}
$$

**Decision Summary:**

| Method | Project A | Project B | Best Project |
|---|---|---|---|
| NPV | ₹48,029 | ₹37,064 | **Project A** |
| BCR | 1.48 | 1.31 | **Project A** |

> **Final Recommendation:** Both projects are **acceptable** (NPV > 0, BCR > 1), but **Project A should be selected** because it has a higher NPV and higher BCR, indicating greater wealth creation per rupee invested.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Mixing sign conventions:** Cash outflows (initial investment) must be entered as NEGATIVE in NPV.
> 2. **Discount factor errors:** Always use $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^t}$, not $\dfrac{1}{(1+it)}$ (linear vs. compound).
> 3. **Decision rule misapplication:** For NPV, accept if NPV > 0; for BCR, accept if BCR > 1. For *mutually exclusive* projects, choose the one with **highest NPV** (not highest IRR).
> 4. **Ignoring NPV vs. BCR conflict:** When they conflict (rare for independent projects), NPV takes precedence.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply BCR to mutually exclusive projects carefully** — when projects have *different scales*, BCR can mislead; always cross-check with NPV.

## ADDITIONAL 14-MARK QUESTION — Capital Budgeting Mix (For Practice)

### Question: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`
**"A project requires an initial investment of ₹5,00,000 with the following expected cash inflows: Year 1: ₹1,50,000; Year 2: ₹2,00,000; Year 3: ₹2,50,000; Year 4: ₹1,00,000. The cost of capital is 12%. Compute NPV, IRR (using interpolation), and Payback Period. State whether the project is acceptable."** **[14 Marks]**

### Model Solution:

#### (a) NPV at 12% [5 Marks]

**Discount Factors at 12%:**
- Year 1: $\dfrac{1}{1.12} = 0.8929$
- Year 2: $\dfrac{1}{1.2544} = 0.7972$
- Year 3: $\dfrac{1}{1.4049} = 0.7118$
- Year 4: $\dfrac{1}{1.5735} = 0.6355$

**Discounted Cash Flows:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8929 = 1{,}33{,}935 \\
PV_2 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972 = 1{,}59{,}440 \\
PV_3 &= 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7118 = 1{,}77{,}950 \\
PV_4 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6355 = 63{,}550 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Total PV of inflows = ₹5,34,875

$$
NPV = 5{,}34{,}875 - 5{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{+₹34{,}875}
$$

#### (b) IRR using Interpolation [5 Marks]

**Try r = 15%:**
- PVIFA(15%,4) = 2.8550
- PV of inflows = 7,00,000 × 0.30 + 2,50,000 × 0.6575 ... let us compute directly:

Actually, using cumulative cash flows:
- $NPV_{15\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000(0.8696) + 2{,}00{,}000(0.7561) + 2{,}50{,}000(0.6575) + 1{,}00{,}000(0.5718)$
- $NPV_{15\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}30{,}440 + 1{,}51{,}220 + 1{,}64{,}375 + 57{,}180 = +3{,}215$

**Try r = 16%:**
- $NPV_{16\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000(0.8621) + 2{,}00{,}000(0.7432) + 2{,}50{,}000(0.6407) + 1{,}00{,}000(0.5523)$
- $NPV_{16\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}29{,}315 + 1{,}48{,}640 + 1{,}60{,}175 + 55{,}230 = -6{,}640$

**Linear Interpolation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR &= 15\% + \frac{3{,}215}{3{,}215 + 6{,}640} \times 1\% \\
&= 15\% + 0.326 \times 1\% \\
&\approx 15.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (c) Payback Period [4 Marks]

**Cumulative Cash Flows:**
- End of Year 1: ₹1,50,000
- End of Year 2: ₹3,50,000
- End of Year 3: ₹6,00,000 ✓ (Investment recovered)

$$
PBP = 2 + \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}50{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} = 2 + 0.6 = \boxed{2.6 \text{ years}}
$$

**Decision:**
- **NPV = +₹34,875 > 0** → Accept
- **IRR = 15.33% > 12% (cost of capital)** → Accept
- **PBP = 2.6 years** (acceptable, well within project life)

**The project is FINANCIALLY VIABLE and should be ACCEPTED.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse the Payback calculation by forgetting the cumulative sum. Always draw a **year-wise cumulative cash flow table** before computing PBP.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4**

### A. Value Engineering (VE)
- ✅ VE is **function-focused, not cost-focused** — optimize $V = F/C$.
- ✅ The **6-phase Job Plan** is the standard: Information → Function Analysis → Creative → Evaluation → Development → Implementation.
- ✅ **Two-word function analysis** (Verb + Noun) is mandatory in KTU answers.
- ✅ VE achieves **10–30% cost reduction** without quality compromise.
- ✅ Application areas: Construction, Manufacturing, Healthcare, IT, Energy, Government projects.

### B. Time Value of Money (TVM)
- ✅ **A rupee today > a rupee tomorrow** — this is the foundation of all discounted methods.
- ✅ $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+i)^n}$ and $FV = PV \times (1+i)^n$ — memorize and apply correctly.
- ✅ PV of Annuity formula: $A \times \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ — used in NPV for equal cash flows.

### C. Net Present Value (NPV)
- ✅ $NPV > 0$ → Accept; $NPV < 0$ → Reject.
- ✅ NPV is the **most reliable** method for mutually exclusive projects.
- ✅ Must use the **correct discount rate (cost of capital)**.

### D. Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)
- ✅ $BCR > 1$ → Accept; $BCR < 1$ → Reject.
- ✅ BCR is **commonly used in public/government projects** (highway, water supply).
- ✅ For mutually exclusive projects with different scales, **use NPV for final decision**.

### E. Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
- ✅ IRR is the discount rate that makes NPV = 0.
- ✅ Accept if **IRR > Cost of Capital**.
- ✅ Solved via **trial-and-error / linear interpolation** (KTU numerical questions).
- ✅ Drawback: **Multiple IRR problem** for non-conventional cash flows.

### F. Payback Period (PBP)
- ✅ $PBP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$
- ✅ Simple Payback **ignores TVM** — major drawback.
- ✅ **Discounted Payback** addresses this — use discounted cash flows.
- ✅ Decision rule: Accept if PBP < **target payback period**.

### G. Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)
- ✅ $ARR = \dfrac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100$
- ✅ Uses **accounting profit (not cash flow)** — major drawback.
- ✅ Decision rule: Accept if ARR > **required rate of return**.

### H. Method Selection — Quick Reference
| Situation | Best Method |
|---|---|
| Wealth maximization goal | **NPV** |
| Comparing return % across projects | **IRR** |
| Public infrastructure | **BCR** |
| Liquidity/risk-focused | **Payback** |
| Quick screening using accounting data | **ARR** |
| Mutually exclusive projects | **NPV (always)** |

### I. Common Numerical Mistakes to Avoid
- ⚠️ Using **simple interest** $(1 + in)$ instead of **compound interest** $(1+i)^n$.
- ⚠️ Forgetting to use **Year 0** for initial investment in NPV.
- ⚠️ Mixing up **PV of annuity** vs. **FV of annuity** formulas.
- ⚠️ IRR interpolation requires **two discount rates on opposite sides of zero NPV** — not both on the same side.
- ⚠️ In BCR, benefits and costs must be discounted at the **same rate**.

### J. KTU 2024 Exam Strategy
- 📌 For 3-mark questions: **Definition + 4 bullet points** = full marks.
- 📌 For 14-mark questions: Divide answer into **sub-parts (a) and (b)** as per question structure.
- 📌 Always show **formula → substitution → answer** in numericals — partial marking is possible.
- 📌 Draw **diagrams and tables** wherever possible to earn presentation marks.
- 📌 For VE procedure questions, **list all 6 phases with at least 2 lines each** to score full marks.
